explanation: The greedy choice property means that a locally optimal choice at each step will lead to a globally optimal solution.

text: What is the coin change problem (minimum coins)?
options: Finding minimum coins to make a target amount
Sorting coins by their face value order
Counting total coins in a collection
Finding the maximum number of coins used
explanation: The coin change problem asks for the minimum number of coins from given denominations to make a target amount. It is solved using DP.

text: What is the time complexity of the recursive Fibonacci without memoization?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: Without memoization, the recursive Fibonacci has O(2^n) time complexity due to redundant computation of the same subproblems.

text: How does memoization improve the Fibonacci computation?
options: It reduces time from O(2^n) to O(n) by caching
It makes the computation run in O(log n) time
It does not improve it at all
It makes the computation run in O(1) time
explanation: Memoization caches the result of each Fibonacci number, ensuring each is computed only once, reducing time from O(2^n) to O(n).

text: What is the N-Queens problem?
options: A graph traversal problem with N vertices
Sorting N queens by their rank and position
Finding N queens in a given dataset collection
Placing N queens so no two attack each other
explanation: The N-Queens problem asks to place N queens on an N×N chessboard so that no two queens share the same row, column, or diagonal. It is typically solved using backtracking.

text: What is the difference between top-down and bottom-up dynamic programming?
options: Top-down is always faster in practice than the bottom-up approach overall
Top-down uses recursion with memoization; bottom-up uses tabulation
Bottom-up always uses significantly more memory than the top-down approach
They are exactly the same fundamental approach overall
explanation: Top-down (memoization) uses recursion and caches results. Bottom-up (tabulation) iteratively fills a table from smallest subproblems upward.

text: What is Huffman coding an example of?
options: Greedy algorithm
Backtracking
Dynamic programming
Divide and conquer
explanation: Huffman coding is a greedy algorithm for constructing optimal prefix-free codes, always merging the two least frequent nodes first.

text: What is the Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS) problem's time complexity with DP?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
explanation: The standard DP solution for LIS runs in O(n^2) time. An optimized approach using binary search achieves O(n log n).

text: What is the matrix chain multiplication problem's time complexity using DP?
options: O(n!)
explanation: The matrix chain multiplication DP solution has O(n^3) time complexity and O(n^2) space, where n is the number of matrices.

text: What is the Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP)?
options: Finding the shortest path between two specific cities
Finding the fastest route avoiding all toll roads
Finding minimum cost Hamiltonian cycle visiting all cities
A minimum spanning tree problem for city networks
explanation: TSP asks for the shortest possible route that visits each city exactly once and returns to the starting city. It is NP-hard.

text: What is the time complexity of solving TSP using dynamic programming (Held-Karp)?
options: O(n!)
explanation: The Held-Karp DP algorithm solves TSP in O(n^2 × 2^n) time, much better than the brute-force O(n!) approach.

text: What is branch and bound?
options: A hashing technique for collision resolution
A type of comparison-based sorting method
An optimization technique pruning suboptimal branches
A graph traversal method for shortest paths
explanation: Branch and bound explores a tree of solutions, using bounds to prune branches that cannot lead to a better solution than the best found so far.

text: What is the edit distance (Levenshtein distance) problem?
options: The minimum single-character edits between two strings
The difference in the lengths of two strings
The number of common characters in two strings
The distance between two arrays in memory
explanation: Edit distance measures the minimum number of insertions, deletions, and substitutions needed to transform one string into another. Solved using DP in O(m × n).

text: What is the difference between the 0/1 knapsack and fractional knapsack?
options: 0/1 uses greedy approach; fractional uses DP approach
Fractional knapsack is computationally harder overall
They are exactly the same problem formulation
0/1 requires whole items (DP); fractional allows fractions (greedy)
explanation: In 0/1 knapsack, items must be taken whole (solved by DP). In fractional knapsack, items can be divided (solved greedily by value-to-weight ratio).

text: What is a randomized algorithm?
options: An algorithm with random bugs in code
An algorithm using random numbers in its decisions
An algorithm that shuffles input before sorting
An algorithm that produces random wrong output
explanation: A randomized algorithm uses random numbers in its logic, often achieving good expected performance (e.g., randomized quicksort, randomized primality testing).

text: What is an approximation algorithm?
options: An algorithm that gives approximate time complexity
An algorithm finding near-optimal solutions in polynomial time
An imprecise algorithm with unreliable output
An algorithm that estimates the input data size
explanation: An approximation algorithm finds solutions within a provable factor of the optimal for NP-hard problems, running in polynomial time.

text: What is the subset sum problem?
options: Finding the sum of all array elements
Summing consecutive elements in an array
Finding the maximum valued subset overall
Determining if a subset sums to a target value
explanation: The subset sum problem asks whether any subset of a given set of integers sums to a target value. It is NP-complete but solvable in pseudo-polynomial time using DP.

text: What is the meet-in-the-middle technique?
options: A two-pointer approach for sorted arrays
A median-finding technique using partitioning
Dividing the array in half for sorting
Splitting input in half and combining to reduce complexity
explanation: Meet-in-the-middle splits the input in half, independently computes results for each half, and combines them. It reduces O(2^n) to O(2^(n/2)) for problems like subset sum.

text: What is a priority queue?
options: A type of stack that also supports priority ordering
A queue that processes all elements in strict FIFO order
A sorted array of elements ordered by their value
A structure where higher priority elements are served first
explanation: A priority queue is an abstract data type where elements have priorities, and elements with higher priority are dequeued before lower priority ones.

text: What data structure typically implements a priority queue?
options: Stack structure
Heap structure
Array structure
Linked list structure
explanation: A binary heap is the most common implementation of a priority queue, providing O(log n) insertion and extraction.

text: What is a trie (prefix tree)?
options: A tree for storing and retrieving strings by prefix
A graph data structure for string matching
A binary search tree for numeric keys
A type of hash table for string storage
explanation: A trie is a tree where each node represents a character, and paths from root to nodes represent prefixes of stored strings. It enables fast prefix-based operations.

text: What is the time complexity of searching for a word of length L in a trie?
options: O(log n)
O(L)
O(n)
O(n × L)
explanation: Searching in a trie takes O(L) time where L is the length of the word, independent of the number of stored words.

text: What is a disjoint set (Union-Find)?
options: A hash set with unique elements
A structure tracking non-overlapping subsets
A set with no elements at all
A sorted set with ordered elements
explanation: A disjoint set (Union-Find) data structure tracks a collection of elements partitioned into non-overlapping subsets, supporting union and find operations efficiently.

text: What operation does 'find' perform in Union-Find?
options: Determines which set an element belongs to
Searches for an element in the set
Finds the minimum element in all sets
Finds all elements in a particular set
explanation: The find operation determines which set an element belongs to by returning the root representative of that set.

text: What is a min-heap?
options: A heap where minimum element is at root
A heap where maximum element is at root
A sorted array of elements by value
A balanced BST with min at leftmost
explanation: In a min-heap, the minimum element is always at the root, and every parent node has a value less than or equal to its children.

text: What is the main advantage of a trie over a hash table for string operations?
options: Has a simpler implementation overall
Uses less memory usage in practice
Supports prefix-based queries efficiently
Provides faster exact string lookups
explanation: Tries excel at prefix-based operations like autocomplete and prefix search, which hash tables cannot do efficiently.

text: What does 'union' operation do in Union-Find?
options: Splits a set into two parts
Finds common elements between sets
Merges two disjoint sets into one
Sorts the sets in ascending order
explanation: The union operation merges two disjoint sets into a single set by connecting their root representatives.

text: What is a multiset?
options: A sorted set with unique elements
A collection allowing duplicate elements
A set with multiple data types
A set of sets nested together
explanation: A multiset (bag) is like a set but allows duplicate elements, counting the number of occurrences of each element.

text: What is path compression in Union-Find?
options: Making every node in find path point to root
Compressing the storage used by the structure
Shortening paths between nodes in a graph
Compressing the data stored in each node
explanation: Path compression optimizes find by making every node encountered during a find operation point directly to the root, flattening the tree structure.

text: What is union by rank in Union-Find?
options: Attaching shorter tree under taller tree during union
Sorting all elements by their assigned rank
Ranking all elements before performing union
Using ranked hash functions for set operations
explanation: Union by rank attaches the tree with lower rank (approximate height) under the root of the tree with higher rank, keeping the overall structure balanced.

text: What is the nearly constant amortized time complexity of Union-Find with path compression and union by rank?
options: O(1) constant time
O(log log n) iterated log
O(log n) logarithmic
O(α(n)) inverse Ackermann
explanation: With both path compression and union by rank, each operation takes O(α(n)) amortized time, where α is the inverse Ackermann function, which is effectively constant.

text: What is a Fibonacci heap?
options: A heap with better amortized decrease-key and insert
A Fibonacci sequence number generator structure
A binary heap variant with extra child pointers
A heap that stores Fibonacci numbers only
explanation: A Fibonacci heap supports insert and decrease-key in O(1) amortized time and extract-min in O(log n) amortized time, used to optimize Dijkstra's algorithm.

text: What is a suffix array?
options: An array of string prefixes
A compressed trie structure
A sorted array of all string suffixes
An array suffix pointer
explanation: A suffix array is a sorted array of all suffixes of a string, providing an efficient alternative to suffix trees for many string processing problems.

text: What is a sparse table?
options: A table with many empty cells throughout
A compressed hash table for sparse data
A sparse matrix representation structure
A structure for O(1) static range minimum queries
explanation: A sparse table preprocesses data in O(n log n) time and space to answer range minimum (or maximum) queries in O(1) time for static arrays.

text: What is a k-d tree?
options: A space-partitioning tree for k-dimensional data
A tree with k children per node always
A tree of exactly depth k levels
A k-ary heap data structure
explanation: A k-d tree is a binary tree that partitions k-dimensional space, useful for nearest neighbor searches and range queries in multidimensional data.

text: What is a circular buffer?
options: A buffer shaped like a circle visually
A buffer for circular linked list nodes
A double-ended buffer with two pointers
A fixed-size buffer that wraps around when full
explanation: A circular buffer uses a fixed-size array with wrap-around to efficiently implement a FIFO queue, overwriting the oldest data when full.

text: What is an interval tree?
options: A tree for querying overlapping intervals efficiently
A tree storing time intervals as keys
A segment tree with interval-based updates
A tree with regular spacing between nodes
explanation: An interval tree stores intervals and supports efficient queries for all intervals that overlap with a given point or interval, in O(log n + k) time where k is the number of results.

text: What is the time complexity of inserting into a trie for a word of length L?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
O(L)
explanation: Insertion into a trie takes O(L) time, where L is the length of the word, as each character is processed once.

text: What is a suffix tree?
options: A tree that stores all string prefixes
A compressed trie of all suffixes for O(m) matching
An array of suffix pointers with sorting
A binary tree of sorted suffix indices
explanation: A suffix tree is a compressed trie containing all suffixes of a string. It enables pattern matching in O(m) time and many other string operations.

text: What is a van Emde Boas tree?
options: A tree with O(log log U) operations for integer keys
A tree with variable branching factor per level
A cache-oblivious tree for external memory
A self-balancing BST with color properties
explanation: A van Emde Boas tree supports operations in O(log log U) time for integer keys in the universe [0, U), by recursively partitioning the universe.

text: What is a rope data structure?
options: A linked list variant for characters
A hash map for string key-value pairs
A graph connecting strings with edges
A balanced binary tree for efficient string operations
explanation: A rope is a balanced binary tree where leaves contain short strings. It supports efficient concatenation, insertion, and deletion of substrings.

text: What is a count-min sketch?
options: A data structure for exact element counting
A compressed hash table with min values
A min-heap variant tracking element counts
A probabilistic structure for approximate frequency counts
explanation: A count-min sketch uses multiple hash functions and a 2D array to approximate frequency counts in a data stream with possible overestimates but no underestimates.

text: What is a link-cut tree?
options: A tree where links can be physically cut
A doubly linked tree with removable nodes
A dynamic forest supporting link, cut, and path queries
A tree with removable edges but fixed vertices
explanation: A link-cut tree (by Sleator and Tarjan) maintains a dynamic forest supporting link (add edge), cut (remove edge), and path aggregate queries in O(log n) amortized time.

text: What is a wavelet tree?
options: A tree for signal processing operations
A frequency-based heap for priority ordering
A Fourier transform data structure for arrays
A tree for rank, select, and range queries on sequences
explanation: A wavelet tree recursively partitions the alphabet to support rank, select, quantile, and range frequency queries on a sequence in O(log σ) time, where σ is the alphabet size.

text: What is a persistent data structure?
options: A data structure saved to disk permanently
A data structure that cannot ever be deleted
A data structure that persists across program runs
A structure preserving all previous versions on modification
explanation: A persistent data structure preserves all previous versions of itself when modified, allowing queries on any past version.

text: What is a binomial heap?
options: A heap with binomial probability distribution
A heap used in statistical analysis methods
A binary heap with extra pointer fields added
A collection of binomial trees with O(log n) merge
explanation: A binomial heap is a collection of binomial trees supporting merge, insert, and extract-min in O(log n) time. Merge is its key advantage over binary heaps.

text: What is an order-statistic tree?
options: An augmented BST supporting O(log n) rank and select
A tree for statistical analysis of data sets
A tree that orders elements statistically
A heap that tracks insertion order of elements
explanation: An order-statistic tree is a BST (often Red-Black) augmented with subtree sizes, supporting select (k-th smallest) and rank queries in O(log n) time.

text: What is a succinct data structure?
options: A brief textual description of a data structure
A structure using near information-theoretic minimum space
A small fixed-capacity array with limited size
A compressed hash table designed for very dense data
explanation: A succinct data structure uses space close to the information-theoretic lower bound (within lower-order terms) while still supporting efficient query operations.

text: What is string matching?
options: Comparing two strings for equality only
Finding occurrences of a pattern in text
Sorting strings in alphabetical order
Concatenating two strings end to end
explanation: String matching (pattern matching) is the problem of finding all occurrences of a pattern string within a larger text string.

text: What is the brute force string matching time complexity for text of length n and pattern of length m?
options: O(n)
O(n + m)
O(m)
O(n × m)
explanation: Brute force string matching checks the pattern at every position in the text, giving O(n × m) worst-case time complexity.

text: What is a substring?
options: A rearrangement of string characters
A contiguous sequence of characters within a string
A string of exactly length 1 character
Any characters from a string in any order
explanation: A substring is a contiguous sequence of characters within a string. For example, 'bcd' is a substring of 'abcde'.

text: What is a subsequence of a string?
options: A sequence derived by deleting characters keeping order
A contiguous part of the original string
A sorted version of the original string
A reversed version of the original string
explanation: A subsequence is formed by deleting zero or more characters from a string without changing the relative order of remaining characters.

text: What is a palindrome?
options: A string with only repeated characters throughout
A string with all unique characters
A string with an even number of characters
A string that reads the same forward and backward
explanation: A palindrome is a string that reads the same forward and backward, such as 'racecar' or 'madam'.

text: What is an anagram?
options: A palindrome of another word
A prefix of another given word
A substring of another word
A rearrangement forming another word
explanation: An anagram is formed by rearranging the characters of a word to create a new word, using all original letters exactly once (e.g., 'listen' and 'silent').

text: How can you check if two strings are anagrams?
options: Compare their lengths only for equality
Check if they have the same first character
Reverse one string and compare with the other
Sort both strings and compare, or count frequencies
explanation: Two strings are anagrams if they have the same character frequencies. This can be checked by sorting both (O(n log n)) or counting character frequencies (O(n)).

text: What is a prefix of a string?
options: The middle portion of the string
A reversed version of the string
The last character of the string
A substring starting from the beginning
explanation: A prefix is a substring that starts at the beginning of the string. For 'hello', prefixes include 'h', 'he', 'hel', 'hell', 'hello'.

text: What is a suffix of a string?
options: A substring starting from the beginning
A rotated version of the string
The first character of the string
A substring ending at the last character
explanation: A suffix is a substring that ends at the last character. For 'hello', suffixes include 'o', 'lo', 'llo', 'ello', 'hello'.

text: What is string concatenation?
options: Splitting a string into parts
Reversing a string entirely
Joining two strings end to end
Sorting characters in a string
explanation: String concatenation joins two or more strings end to end to form a new string. For example, 'hello' + ' world' = 'hello world'.

text: What is the KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) algorithm?
options: A string compression algorithm for reducing text size
A hash-based search method for substring matching tasks
A pattern matcher using failure function to skip redundancy
A comparison-based sorting algorithm for string arrays
explanation: KMP preprocesses the pattern to build a failure function (partial match table) that allows it to skip redundant comparisons, achieving O(n + m) time.

text: What is the time complexity of the KMP algorithm?
options: O(n + m)
O(m log n)
O(n × m)
explanation: KMP runs in O(n + m) time: O(m) for preprocessing the pattern and O(n) for searching through the text.

text: What is the Rabin-Karp algorithm?
options: A divide and conquer string matching algorithm
A string matching algorithm using rolling hashes
A string sorting algorithm by character values
A tree-based matching algorithm using tries
explanation: Rabin-Karp uses a rolling hash to compute hash values of text substrings, comparing them with the pattern's hash for quick matching.

text: What is a rolling hash in Rabin-Karp?
options: A hash that updates efficiently as the window slides
A multi-level hash with cascading computation
A hash that changes randomly each call
A hash stored in a circular buffer structure
explanation: A rolling hash efficiently updates the hash value when the window slides by one position, by removing the contribution of the outgoing character and adding the incoming one in O(1).

text: What is the longest palindromic substring problem?
options: Finding the longest string in a collection
Reversing a string to find palindromes
Finding the longest contiguous palindrome substring
Finding all palindromes in a given string
explanation: The problem asks for the longest contiguous substring of a given string that reads the same forwards and backwards.

text: What is the time complexity of finding the longest palindromic substring using dynamic programming?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
explanation: The DP approach fills an n × n table, checking if each substring is a palindrome, taking O(n^2) time.

text: What is string hashing used for?
options: Efficiently comparing strings via hash values
Encrypting strings for secure storage
Compressing strings to save disk space
Sorting strings into alphabetical order
explanation: String hashing computes hash values of strings for efficient comparison. It is used in pattern matching, duplicate detection, and many string problems.

text: What is the Boyer-Moore algorithm?
options: A pattern matcher using bad character and good suffix rules
A brute force matching algorithm for strings
A string sorting algorithm by character frequency
A compression algorithm for text data files
explanation: Boyer-Moore compares the pattern from right to left and uses bad character and good suffix heuristics to skip portions of the text, often achieving sub-linear time in practice.

text: What is the Z-array of a string?
options: An array where Z[i] is longest prefix match starting at i
The reverse of the original string as array
An array of character frequencies for the string
An array of zeros for initialization
explanation: The Z-array for a string S has Z[i] = length of the longest substring starting at position i that is also a prefix of S. It can be computed in O(n) time.

text: How is the Z-algorithm used for pattern matching?
options: By hashing the pattern and comparing values
By using a suffix tree of the text string
By sorting the text characters first
By concatenating pattern and text then computing Z-array
explanation: The Z-algorithm concatenates pattern P, a separator, and text T (P$T), then computes the Z-array. Positions where Z[i] = |P| indicate pattern matches.

text: What is the Aho-Corasick algorithm?
options: A multi-pattern matcher building a trie-based automaton
A compression algorithm for pattern libraries
A single pattern matching algorithm only
A string sorting algorithm for multiple strings
explanation: Aho-Corasick builds a trie-based automaton from multiple patterns and searches for all patterns simultaneously in the text in O(n + m + z) time, where z is the number of matches.

text: What is Manacher's algorithm?
options: A string sorting algorithm for arrays
A pattern matching algorithm for text
A string compression algorithm for storage
An O(n) algorithm finding all palindromic substrings
explanation: Manacher's algorithm finds the longest palindromic substring (and all maximal palindromes) in O(n) time by cleverly reusing previously computed palindrome information.

text: What is the time complexity of building a suffix array using the DC3/skew algorithm?
options: O(n log^2 n)
O(n log n)
O(n)
explanation: The DC3 (difference cover) algorithm constructs a suffix array in O(n) time, which is optimal.

text: What is the LCP (Longest Common Prefix) array?
options: An array storing longest common prefix lengths between consecutive sorted suffixes
An array storing character frequencies found within a given string
An array storing the lengths of all strings within a collection
An array storing prefix sums computed over numeric data elements
explanation: The LCP array stores the length of the longest common prefix between consecutive suffixes in the suffix array, useful for many string problems.

text: What is Burrows-Wheeler Transform (BWT)?
options: A string matching algorithm for patterns
A reversible transformation aiding text compression
A string encryption method for security
A hash function designed for string data
explanation: BWT rearranges characters of a string by sorting all rotations, producing output with more character runs. It is reversible and used in bzip2 compression.

text: What is a suffix automaton?
options: An automaton that generates all suffixes
A pattern matching machine for any text
A minimal DFA recognizing all suffixes of a string
A suffix tree variant with compressed nodes
explanation: A suffix automaton is the smallest DFA that accepts exactly all suffixes of a string. It can be built in O(n) time and has at most 2n-1 states.

text: What is the time complexity of the Boyer-Moore algorithm in the best case?
options: O(n/m)
O(n × m)
O(n)
O(n + m)
explanation: In the best case, Boyer-Moore achieves O(n/m) time by skipping m characters at a time using the bad character heuristic.

text: What is polynomial string hashing?
options: Hashing using polynomial long division
Computing hash as sum of characters times powers of a base
Hashing that produces polynomial-length hash output
Using polynomial time algorithms for hash computation
explanation: Polynomial hashing computes hash(s) = s[0]×p^0 + s[1]×p^1 + ... + s[n-1]×p^(n-1) mod M, where p is a prime base. It supports efficient rolling hash computation.

text: What is the longest repeated substring problem's optimal solution?
options: O(n^3) brute force approach
O(n^2) using dynamic programming
O(n log^2 n) using rolling hashing
O(n) using suffix array with LCP array
explanation: The longest repeated substring can be found in O(n) time by building a suffix array and LCP array, then finding the maximum value in the LCP array.

text: What is the number of distinct substrings of a string of length n?
options: n(n+1)/2 minus sum(LCP)
2^n (exponential count)
n^2 (quadratic count)
n! (factorial of n)
explanation: The number of distinct substrings is n(n+1)/2 - sum(LCP), where LCP is the longest common prefix array of the suffix array. Total substrings minus duplicates.

text: What does P stand for in computational complexity?
options: Problems solvable in polynomial time
Prime number problems class
Probability problems class
Parallel problems class
explanation: P is the class of decision problems that can be solved by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time.

text: What does NP stand for in computational complexity?
options: New computational Problems class
Non-deterministic Polynomial time
Not Polynomial time class
No Proof available for it
explanation: NP stands for Non-deterministic Polynomial time: problems whose solutions can be verified in polynomial time by a deterministic Turing machine.

text: What is the best time complexity achievable for comparison-based sorting?
options: O(log n)
O(n log n)
O(n)
explanation: The lower bound for any comparison-based sorting algorithm is Ω(n log n), proven using the decision tree model.

text: Which is more efficient: O(n) or O(n log n)?
options: Depends on constants
O(n) is more efficient
O(n log n) is better
They are equivalent
explanation: O(n) is more efficient than O(n log n) because n grows slower than n log n for large values of n.

text: What is the time complexity of accessing an element in a hash table on average?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
explanation: Hash tables provide O(1) average-case time for access, insertion, and deletion with a good hash function.

text: What is time-space tradeoff?
options: Time complexity cannot affect space complexity
Time and space are always equal in measure
Using more time to save space or vice versa
A method to reduce both time and space simultaneously
explanation: A time-space tradeoff means that an algorithm can use more memory to run faster, or use less memory at the cost of running slower.

text: Which is faster: O(log n) or O(√n)?
options: They are equivalent
O(log n) is faster
O(√n) is faster
Cannot compare them
explanation: O(log n) grows slower than O(√n) for large n. For example, when n = 1,000,000: log n ≈ 20 while √n = 1,000.

text: What is the significance of O(n!) complexity?
options: It is essentially the same as O(n^2) complexity class
It is classified as a polynomial time complexity class
It is very efficient for all possible input sizes given
It is extremely inefficient, growing super-exponentially
explanation: O(n!) (factorial) is one of the fastest-growing complexities, making algorithms with this complexity practical only for very small inputs.

text: What does 'in-place algorithm' mean?
options: An algorithm that runs at a specific location
An algorithm using only constant extra space
An algorithm that cannot be relocated in memory
An algorithm that only sorts data in place
explanation: An in-place algorithm uses only O(1) or O(log n) extra space beyond the input, modifying the input directly rather than using auxiliary data structures.

text: What is the time complexity of finding the maximum element in an unsorted array?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: Finding the maximum in an unsorted array requires examining every element, taking O(n) time.

text: What is an NP-complete problem?
options: A problem that cannot be solved at all
A problem with no known algorithm existing
A problem in NP as hard as any NP problem
A problem that runs in constant time always
explanation: An NP-complete problem is in NP and is as hard as any NP problem: every problem in NP can be reduced to it in polynomial time.

text: What is an NP-hard problem?
options: A problem with a known polynomial time solution
A problem that is always in the class P
A problem that is easy to solve efficiently
A problem at least as hard as NP-complete problems
explanation: An NP-hard problem is at least as hard as NP-complete problems. It may or may not be in NP (its solution may not be verifiable in polynomial time).

text: What is memoization's space-time tradeoff?
options: It uses no extra space at all
It uses extra space to avoid redundant computation
It increases time to save storage space
It reduces both space and time simultaneously
explanation: Memoization trades extra memory (to store computed results) for faster execution (by looking up previously computed results instead of recomputing them).

text: What is the relationship between P and NP?
options: P and NP are completely disjoint sets
P = NP has been proven true conclusively
NP ⊆ P has been proven true conclusively
P ⊆ NP, but whether P = NP is open
explanation: Every problem in P is also in NP (P ⊆ NP). Whether P = NP (every NP problem has a polynomial solution) is one of the most important open problems in computer science.

text: What is a polynomial reduction?
options: Reducing the degree of a polynomial expression
Transforming one problem into another in polynomial time
Reducing the time complexity of an algorithm
Simplifying an algorithm's code implementation
explanation: A polynomial reduction transforms an instance of problem A into an instance of problem B in polynomial time, used to prove that B is at least as hard as A.

text: What is tail recursion optimization?
options: Adding a tail data structure to the recursion stack
Removing all recursive calls from the entire program
Making recursive call the last operation for iteration optimization
Making the recursion run in reverse backward order
explanation: Tail recursion optimization converts a recursive function where the recursive call is the last operation into an iterative loop, eliminating stack overhead.

text: What is loop unrolling?
options: Removing all loops from the source code base entirely
Making loops run in the reverse backward execution order
Executing multiple iterations per loop cycle to reduce overhead
Converting all iterative loops to recursion-based calls
explanation: Loop unrolling is a compiler optimization that reduces loop overhead by executing the body of the loop multiple times per iteration, reducing the number of branch instructions.

text: What is cache-friendly code?
options: Code with no memory access at all
Code that runs entirely in cache memory
Code that uses caching library APIs
Code that maximizes CPU cache hit rates
explanation: Cache-friendly code accesses memory sequentially or in patterns that maximize spatial and temporal locality, reducing cache misses and improving performance.

text: What is the difference between best case, worst case, and average case complexity?
options: Best is minimum time, worst is maximum, average is expected
Worst case is purely theoretical and never practical
They are always the same value for any algorithm
Best case only applies to small input sizes
explanation: Best case is the minimum time for any input of size n, worst case is the maximum, and average case is the expected time averaged over all possible inputs.

text: Why is O(n log n) considered efficient for sorting?
options: It works only for small input sizes efficiently
It uses the least memory of all algorithms
It matches the comparison-based sorting lower bound
It is the fastest possible for all sorting
explanation: O(n log n) is optimal for comparison-based sorting algorithms, as proven by the Ω(n log n) lower bound from decision tree analysis.

text: What is the complexity class PSPACE?
options: Problems solvable using polynomial space amount
Problems requiring exponential space to solve
Problems solvable in constant space only
Problems with no space requirement at all
explanation: PSPACE is the class of problems solvable by a Turing machine using a polynomial amount of space. P ⊆ NP ⊆ PSPACE.

text: What is Cook's theorem?
options: P equals NP has been conclusively proven
Every NP problem is fundamentally unsolvable
Every problem is solvable in polynomial time
The Boolean satisfiability problem (SAT) is NP-complete
explanation: Cook's theorem (1971) proves that the Boolean satisfiability problem (SAT) is NP-complete, establishing the first known NP-complete problem.

text: What is the difference between a decision problem and an optimization problem?
options: Decision has yes/no answer; optimization seeks best solution
They are exactly the same type of problem
Optimization problems are always solvable in P
Decision problems are always computationally harder
explanation: A decision problem asks a yes/no question (e.g., 'Is there a path shorter than k?'). An optimization problem seeks the optimal solution (e.g., 'What is the shortest path?').

text: What is pseudo-polynomial time?
options: An algorithm that pretends to be efficient
Time polynomial in input value rather than input size
False polynomial time that does not exist
The same as polynomial time in all cases
explanation: A pseudo-polynomial algorithm runs in time polynomial in the value of the input (e.g., O(nW) for knapsack) but not in the size (number of bits) of the input.

text: What is the 3-SAT problem?
options: A Boolean satisfiability problem with 3 literals per clause
A problem with exactly 3 valid solutions
A sorting problem with 3 distinct elements
Satisfying exactly 3 linear equations
explanation: 3-SAT is a special case of SAT where the Boolean formula is in CNF and each clause has exactly 3 literals. It is NP-complete and often used for reductions.

text: What is the vertex cover problem?
options: Coloring all vertices with minimum colors
Finding all connected vertices in a graph
Finding smallest vertex set covering all edges
Covering all vertices with color assignments
explanation: The vertex cover problem asks for the smallest set of vertices that covers all edges (every edge has at least one endpoint in the set). It is NP-complete.

text: What is the approximation ratio of the 2-approximation algorithm for vertex cover?
options: The solution is at most twice the optimal
The solution is at most 1.5 times optimal
The solution is at most 3 times the optimal
The solution always equals the optimal exactly
explanation: The greedy 2-approximation algorithm for vertex cover guarantees a solution at most twice the size of the optimal solution.

text: What is the class co-NP?
options: The class whose complement problems are in NP
The complement of NP problems
Problems that are harder than NP class
Problems that are not in NP at all
explanation: co-NP contains problems whose complement (negation) is in NP. For example, 'is this formula unsatisfiable?' is in co-NP because 'is it satisfiable?' is in NP.

text: What is a parameterized algorithm?
options: An algorithm that dynamically changes its parameters at runtime
An algorithm whose complexity depends on input size and a fixed parameter
A tunable sorting algorithm that uses an adjustable pivot element
An algorithm that has many user-tunable parameter values
explanation: Parameterized algorithms measure complexity as f(k) × n^c, where k is a parameter. A problem is FPT if it can be solved in time f(k) × n^O(1), isolating the exponential part to the parameter k.

text: What is the time hierarchy theorem?
options: All problems can be solved in the same time
Time complexity is always fixed and unchanging
More time allows a Turing machine to solve more problems
There is no hierarchy in time complexity classes
explanation: The time hierarchy theorem states that given more time, Turing machines can solve more problems. Specifically, DTIME(f(n)) is strictly contained in DTIME(f(n) × log f(n)) for reasonable f.

text: What does FIFO stand for in data structures?
options: Fast Input Fast Output
First Index First Order
First In First Out
Fixed In Fixed Out
explanation: FIFO stands for First In First Out, a principle used in queues where the first element added is the first one removed.

text: Which term describes a function calling itself?
options: Abstraction
Iteration
Recursion
Selection
explanation: Recursion is a technique where a function calls itself to solve smaller subproblems of the original problem.

text: What is the base case in a recursive function?
options: The largest subproblem solved by recursion
The first recursive call made by function
The condition that stops the recursion calls
The condition that starts the recursion loop
explanation: The base case is the condition that terminates recursion, preventing infinite calls by returning a result directly.

text: What does LIFO stand for in data structures?
options: Linear Input Fixed Output
Last Index First Output
Least Index First Order
Last In First Out
explanation: LIFO stands for Last In First Out, a principle used in stacks where the most recently added element is removed first.

text: Which complexity class grows the fastest?
options: Linear
Logarithmic
Exponential
Polynomial
explanation: Exponential complexity O(2^n) grows the fastest among these classes, doubling with each additional input element.

text: What is the time complexity of a simple for loop iterating n times?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: A single for loop iterating n times performs n operations, giving a linear time complexity of O(n).

text: What does the term 'traversal' mean in data structures?
options: Copying a structure into another one
Sorting elements in ascending order only
Deleting all elements from a structure
Visiting each element exactly one time
explanation: Traversal means visiting or accessing each element in a data structure exactly once in a systematic manner.

text: Which data structure uses the LIFO principle?
options: Stack
Queue
Graph
Array
explanation: A stack follows the Last In First Out (LIFO) principle where the last element pushed is the first one popped.

text: What is a primitive data type?
options: A complex object-oriented class type
A user-defined composite structure type
A dynamically allocated reference type
A basic built-in type provided by language
explanation: Primitive data types are basic types built into a programming language, such as int, float, char, and boolean.

text: What is the purpose of a flowchart?
options: To encrypt data for secure transmission
To visually represent an algorithm's steps
To compile source code into binary files
To allocate memory for data structures
explanation: A flowchart is a diagrammatic representation of an algorithm showing the sequence of steps using standard symbols.

text: What is the time complexity of T(n) = T(n-1) + n?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
explanation: Expanding the recurrence: T(n) = n + (n-1) + ... + 1 = n(n+1)/2, which is O(n^2).

text: Which notation gives both upper and lower asymptotic bounds?
options: Small-o notation
Big-O notation
Theta notation
Omega notation
explanation: Theta notation provides a tight bound, meaning the function is bounded both above and below by the same growth rate.

text: What is the space complexity of a recursive function with depth n?
options: O(n) due to call stack frames
O(1) constant space always
O(n^2) due to nested recursion
O(log n) due to stack halving
explanation: Each recursive call adds a frame to the call stack, so a recursion depth of n uses O(n) space.

text: Which recurrence relation represents binary search?
explanation: Binary search halves the input each step and does constant work, giving T(n) = T(n/2) + 1 = O(log n).

text: What is amortized analysis used for?
options: Measuring best-case time for a single run
Finding worst case of each single operation
Calculating space used by a single function
Computing the average cost over many operations
explanation: Amortized analysis determines the average time per operation over a sequence of operations, even if some individual operations are expensive.

text: What is tail recursion?
options: Recursion where the call is the last operation
Recursion that uses an explicit stack structure
Recursion with no defined base case at all
Recursion with multiple simultaneous recursive calls
explanation: Tail recursion occurs when the recursive call is the last operation in the function, allowing compiler optimization to reuse the stack frame.

text: Which complexity is better for large inputs: O(n log n) or O(n^2)?
options: O(n^2) is always better for large inputs
O(n log n) is better for large inputs
Neither is better; they alternate in speed
Both have identical growth for large inputs
explanation: O(n log n) grows slower than O(n^2) for large inputs, making it the more efficient complexity class.

text: What is the difference between an algorithm and a heuristic?
options: Algorithms guarantee solutions; heuristics may not
Algorithms use hardware; heuristics use software
Heuristics guarantee correctness; algorithms do not
Heuristics are faster; algorithms are always slower
explanation: An algorithm guarantees a correct solution, while a heuristic is an approximation that may find a good solution faster but without guarantees.

text: What does the substitution method prove in recurrence solving?
options: It proves by guessing and verifying via induction
It proves by converting recurrence to iteration
It proves by drawing a complete recursion tree
It proves by applying the Master Theorem formula
explanation: The substitution method involves guessing the form of the solution and then proving it correct using mathematical induction.

text: What is the recurrence for merge sort?
explanation: Merge sort divides the array into two halves (2T(n/2)) and merges them in O(n) time, giving T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n.

text: What is the time complexity of T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n log n by Master Theorem?
options: O(n log^2 n)
O(n^2 log n)
O(n log n)
explanation: Here a=3, b=4, f(n)=n log n. Since n^(log_4 3) is approximately n^0.79 and f(n) = Omega(n^(log_4 3 + epsilon)), Case 3 applies, giving O(n log n).

text: Which method handles recurrences not solvable by the Master Theorem?
options: Simple iteration counting method
Akra-Bazzi generalized method
Direct formula application method
Basic substitution guess method
explanation: The Akra-Bazzi method generalizes the Master Theorem and handles recurrences with unequal subproblem sizes and non-polynomial driving functions.

text: What is the complexity of T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n log n?
options: O(n^2 log n)
O(n log n)
O(n log^2 n)
explanation: This falls between Cases 2 and 3 of the Master Theorem. Using the extended version, T(n) = O(n log^2 n).

text: In amortized analysis, what is the potential method?
options: Assigning actual cost to each operation directly
Assigning a potential energy function to data structure
Counting total operations divided by total time
Measuring memory usage across all operations run
explanation: The potential method assigns a potential function to the data structure state; amortized cost = actual cost + change in potential.

text: What is the significance of the P vs NP problem?
options: It asks if space complexity equals time complexity always
It asks if all algorithms can run in constant time always
It asks if polynomial verification implies polynomial solving
It asks if every problem has a brute force solution path
explanation: P vs NP asks whether every problem whose solution can be verified in polynomial time can also be solved in polynomial time.

text: What does the recursion tree method visualize?
options: The sorted order of elements after each comparison
The memory allocation pattern of the algorithm run
The branching cost at each level of the recurrence
The input-output mapping of the algorithm function
explanation: The recursion tree method draws a tree where each node represents the cost at that level, and summing all levels gives the total complexity.

text: What is the time complexity of T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + n?
options: O(n)
O(n log^2 n)
O(n log n)
explanation: Using the recursion tree, each level sums to n and the tree height is log_{3/2}(n), giving O(n log n).

text: When does the Master Theorem not apply directly?
options: When b is greater than a in the recurrence
When the recurrence has exactly two subproblems
When subproblems have different unequal sizes
When f(n) is a simple polynomial function
explanation: The Master Theorem requires subproblems of equal size. When subproblems are unequal like T(n/3) + T(2n/3), the Akra-Bazzi method is needed.

text: What is the aggregate method in amortized analysis?
options: Using a potential function to track stored credits
Assigning different costs to different operation types
Counting only the most expensive operation each time
Summing total cost of n operations then dividing by n
explanation: The aggregate method finds the total cost of a sequence of n operations and divides by n to get the amortized cost per operation.

text: What is the difference between deterministic and nondeterministic algorithms?
options: Deterministic ones use randomness; nondeterministic do not
Deterministic ones are slower; nondeterministic are faster
Nondeterministic ones always find the optimal solution path
Nondeterministic ones can explore multiple paths at once
explanation: A nondeterministic algorithm can conceptually explore multiple computation paths simultaneously, which is key to understanding NP complexity class.

text: What is the maximum number of elements a stack can pop at once?
options: Half of them
All elements
Two elements
One element
explanation: A stack can only pop one element at a time — the element currently at the top of the stack.

text: Which operation adds an element to the rear of a queue?
options: Enqueue
Pop
Push
Dequeue
explanation: The enqueue operation adds an element to the rear (back) of a queue.

text: What is a singly linked list?
options: Each node contains two separate data field values
Each node points to both next and previous nodes
Each node points only to the next node in list
Each node has a direct link to every other node
explanation: In a singly linked list, each node contains data and a pointer to the next node only, forming a one-directional chain.

text: What is stored in each node of a linked list?
options: Data and a pointer to the next node
Only the memory address of next node
A pointer to both previous and next nodes
Only the data value without any pointer
explanation: Each node in a basic linked list stores data and a reference (pointer) to the next node in the sequence.

text: Which data structure is used for undo operations in editors?
options: Queue
Graph
Stack
Array
explanation: A stack is used for undo operations because the most recent action (last in) needs to be undone first (first out).

text: What happens when you dequeue from an empty queue?
options: It inserts a default placeholder element
It returns a null or zero value silently
It causes an underflow error condition
It resets the queue to initial capacity
explanation: Attempting to dequeue from an empty queue causes an underflow error because there are no elements to remove.

text: What is the index of the first element in a zero-based array?
options: n
explanation: In zero-based indexing, the first element of an array is stored at index 0.

text: Which linear structure allows insertion at both ends?
options: Deque
Array
Queue
Stack
explanation: A deque (double-ended queue) supports insertion and deletion at both the front and rear ends.

text: What is the time complexity of inserting at the head of a linked list?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: Inserting at the head of a linked list takes O(1) time since we only need to update the head pointer.

text: What is a static array?
options: An array that automatically sorts its elements
An array with a fixed size set at creation
An array whose size can grow dynamically
An array that stores only string data types
explanation: A static array has a fixed size determined at the time of declaration and cannot be resized during execution.

text: What is the advantage of a circular queue over a linear queue?
options: It stores more elements in the same memory space
It allows random access to any element directly
It efficiently reuses vacated front space in array
It automatically sorts elements during insertion
explanation: A circular queue wraps around to reuse space vacated by dequeued elements, solving the problem of wasted space in a linear queue.

text: How does a doubly linked list differ from a singly linked list?
options: Each node has pointers to both next and previous
It stores two data fields in each single node
It uses half the memory of a singly linked list
It can only be traversed in the forward direction
explanation: A doubly linked list has two pointers per node — one to the next node and one to the previous node — allowing bidirectional traversal.

text: What is the time complexity of searching in an unsorted linked list?
options: O(n log n)
O(log n)
O(n)
explanation: Searching in an unsorted linked list requires traversing nodes one by one in the worst case, giving O(n) time.

text: What is a sentinel node in a linked list?
options: A dummy node used to simplify boundary operations
A node that stores the list's total length value
A special node that stores metadata about types
A node that points to the middle of the list
explanation: A sentinel (dummy) node is a placeholder node at the boundary of a list that simplifies insertion and deletion by eliminating null checks.

text: How is a stack implemented using two queues?
options: Push to both queues; pop alternates between the two
Push to Q2 always; pop alternates and merges both queues
Push and pop both operate on Q1; Q2 stores overflow
Push to Q1 always; for pop, move Q1 to Q2 and dequeue
explanation: One approach: always enqueue to Q1. For pop, move all but the last element from Q1 to Q2, then dequeue the remaining element from Q1.

text: What is the time complexity of inserting at a specific position in an array?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: Inserting at a specific position in an array requires shifting all subsequent elements by one position, giving O(n) time.

text: What problem does a circular linked list solve?
options: It automatically maintains elements in sorted order
It reduces memory usage by half compared to singly
It allows continuous traversal without null termination
It enables O(1) search for any element in list
explanation: A circular linked list connects the last node back to the first, allowing continuous traversal without encountering a null pointer.

text: Which data structure is best for implementing a browser's back button?
options: Deque
Array
Stack
Queue
explanation: A stack is ideal for a browser's back button because pages are revisited in reverse order (LIFO), matching the stack's behavior.

text: What is the disadvantage of an array-based stack?
options: It requires O(n) time for every push operation
It cannot store primitive data type values at all
It has a fixed capacity that can cause overflow
It does not support push and pop operations well
explanation: An array-based stack has a fixed maximum size, so pushing beyond capacity causes stack overflow unless resized dynamically.

text: What is the amortized cost of appending to a dynamic array?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
explanation: Although occasional resizing costs O(n), the amortized cost of appending to a dynamic array is O(1) when doubling the capacity.

text: How does the XOR linked list reduce memory usage?
options: It stores XOR of next and previous addresses per node
It stores only even-indexed nodes and skips odd ones
It removes half the nodes and links remaining ones
It compresses data values using XOR encoding scheme
explanation: An XOR linked list stores the XOR of the next and previous node addresses in a single pointer field, halving pointer memory usage.

text: What is the time complexity of reversing a singly linked list iteratively?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
explanation: Reversing a singly linked list iteratively requires one pass through all n nodes, changing each pointer, giving O(n) time.

text: How can you detect a cycle in a linked list in O(1) space?
options: Use Floyd's cycle detection with two pointer speeds
Sort the list and check for duplicate node values
Use a hash set to store all visited node addresses
Store visited flags inside each node data field
explanation: Floyd's tortoise and hare algorithm uses two pointers moving at different speeds; if they meet, a cycle exists, using only O(1) extra space.

text: What is the worst-case time for dynamic array resizing?
options: O(log n) due to binary copy optimization
O(1) per single resize operation performed
O(n) to copy all elements to new array
O(n^2) due to nested copy and shift steps
explanation: When a dynamic array is full, a new larger array is allocated and all n elements are copied over, taking O(n) time for that single resize.

text: How is a min-stack implemented with O(1) getMin?
options: Store min value only at the bottom of the stack
Sort the stack after every push operation call
Scan the entire stack each time getMin is called
Maintain an auxiliary stack tracking current minimums
explanation: A min-stack uses an auxiliary stack that pushes the current minimum alongside each element, enabling O(1) minimum retrieval.

text: What is the skip list and how does it improve linked list search?
options: A multi-level linked list with express lane pointers
A balanced tree structure embedded in a linked list
A sorted array overlay that replaces the linked list
A hash table combined with a singly linked list node
explanation: A skip list maintains multiple levels of linked lists with increasingly sparse nodes, allowing O(log n) expected search time.

text: What is an unrolled linked list?
options: A linked list where each node stores an array of elements
A linked list with randomized pointer assignments only
A linked list where nodes are stored in reverse order
A linked list that has been converted into a flat array
explanation: An unrolled linked list stores multiple elements per node (a small array), reducing pointer overhead and improving cache performance.

text: How do you find the intersection point of two linked lists efficiently?
options: Reverse both lists and compare from the tail forward
Hash all elements of both lists and compare buckets
Sort both lists and perform a binary search on each
Compute lengths, align starts, then traverse together
explanation: Find the length difference, advance the longer list by that difference, then traverse both simultaneously until pointers meet at the intersection.

text: What is the time complexity of merging k sorted linked lists using a min-heap?
options: O(nk log k)
O(nk^2)
O(nk)
O(n log k)
explanation: Using a min-heap of size k, each of the n*k total elements is inserted and extracted once, giving O(nk log k) where n is the average list length.

text: What is a gap buffer and where is it used?
options: An array with a movable gap used in text editors
A circular array used for network packet buffering
A stack-based buffer used for function call storage
A linked list variant used in database indexing
explanation: A gap buffer is an array with a gap (empty space) at the cursor position, making insertions and deletions near the cursor O(1), commonly used in text editors.

text: What is the root node of a tree?
options: Any leaf node at the bottom of the tree
The node with the most children in tree
The node at the deepest level of tree
The topmost node with no parent at all
explanation: The root is the topmost node in a tree that has no parent and serves as the starting point for traversal.

text: What is a leaf node in a tree?
options: A node that connects to the root directly
A node that has no children at all
A node that has exactly two child nodes
The first node added to the tree structure
explanation: A leaf node is a node with no children; it is at the terminal end of a branch in the tree.

text: How many children can a binary tree node have at most?
options: Four
Three
Two
One
explanation: In a binary tree, each node can have at most two children, referred to as the left child and the right child.

text: What is an edge in a graph?
options: A connection between two vertices
A path visiting all graph vertices
An isolated node with no neighbors
The weight assigned to each vertex
explanation: An edge is a connection or link between two vertices (nodes) in a graph.

text: What is an undirected graph?
options: A graph where edges have specific direction only
A graph where edges have no specific direction
A graph where all vertices are fully connected
A graph with no edges between any vertices
explanation: In an undirected graph, edges have no direction — if vertex A connects to B, then B also connects to A.

text: What is the height of a single-node tree?
options: One
Two
Undefined
Zero
explanation: The height of a tree with a single node (the root) is 0, as height counts the number of edges on the longest path from root to a leaf.

text: What is a weighted graph?
options: A graph where all vertices have the same degree
A graph with no cycles between any of its nodes
A graph where every node connects to all others
A graph where edges have assigned numerical values
explanation: A weighted graph assigns a numerical value (weight) to each edge, often representing cost, distance, or capacity.

text: What is the degree of a vertex in an undirected graph?
options: The total weight of all edges from that vertex
The number of edges connected to the vertex
The shortest path from that vertex to root
The number of vertices in the entire graph
explanation: The degree of a vertex in an undirected graph is the number of edges incident to it.

text: What is a complete binary tree?
options: A tree where every internal node has no children
A tree with only leaf nodes and no internal nodes
A tree where every node has exactly one child node
A tree where all levels are fully filled except last
explanation: A complete binary tree has all levels fully filled except possibly the last, which is filled from left to right.

text: What is an adjacency list used for?
options: Finding shortest path in weighted graph directly
Storing tree nodes in level-order arrangement
Storing graph structure as lists of neighbors
Sorting vertices in topological ordering only
explanation: An adjacency list represents a graph by storing a list of neighboring vertices for each vertex, using memory efficiently for sparse graphs.

text: What is the maximum number of nodes in a binary tree of height h?
explanation: A binary tree of height h can have at most 2^(h+1) - 1 nodes when every level is completely filled (perfect binary tree).

text: What is the difference between adjacency list and adjacency matrix?
options: Lists cannot represent weighted graphs but matrices can
Lists use O(V+E) space; matrices use O(V^2) space
Both always use exactly the same amount of space
Lists use O(V^2) space; matrices use O(V+E) space
explanation: Adjacency lists use O(V+E) space, efficient for sparse graphs; adjacency matrices use O(V^2) space, efficient for dense graphs.

text: What is a binary search tree (BST) property?
options: Nodes are arranged in ascending order left to right
Left child is less; right child is greater than parent
All nodes have exactly two children always
Every level must be completely filled with nodes
explanation: In a BST, for every node, all values in the left subtree are less and all values in the right subtree are greater.

text: What is a full binary tree?
options: A tree where each node has exactly one child node
A tree where all leaf nodes are at the same level
A tree where every node has zero or two children
A tree with the maximum possible number of nodes
explanation: A full (or proper) binary tree is one where every node has either zero or two children — no node has only one child.

text: How is a graph different from a tree?
options: Trees have weighted edges; graphs have unweighted ones
Graphs must be connected; trees can be disconnected
Trees can have cycles; graphs cannot have any cycles
Graphs can have cycles; trees are always acyclic
explanation: A tree is a connected acyclic graph. Graphs in general can contain cycles, multiple connected components, and need not have a root.

text: What is a self-loop in a graph?
options: An edge shared between two separate graph components
A path that returns to the starting vertex via others
A cycle that passes through every vertex exactly once
An edge that connects a vertex to itself directly
explanation: A self-loop is an edge that connects a vertex to itself, meaning the edge starts and ends at the same vertex.

text: What is the space complexity of an adjacency matrix for V vertices?
options: O(V)
O(V + E)
O(E)
explanation: An adjacency matrix uses a V x V 2D array to store edge information, requiring O(V^2) space regardless of the number of edges.

text: What is a balanced binary tree?
options: A tree where left and right subtree heights differ by at most one
A tree where all nodes have exactly two children always
A tree where all leaf nodes store the same data value
A tree that contains equal number of left and right nodes
explanation: A balanced binary tree ensures that for every node, the height difference between its left and right subtrees is at most 1.

text: What is a directed acyclic graph (DAG)?
options: An undirected graph with at least one cycle present
A tree with multiple root nodes and directed edges
A graph where all edges are bidirectional and acyclic
A directed graph with no cycles between any vertices
explanation: A DAG is a directed graph with no cycles; it is commonly used for task scheduling, dependency resolution, and topological sorting.

text: What is the depth of a node in a tree?
options: The maximum number of nodes at that particular level
The total number of nodes in the entire tree structure
The number of edges from the root to that specific node
The number of children that the node currently has
explanation: The depth of a node is the number of edges on the path from the root of the tree to that node.

text: What is a bipartite graph?
options: A graph whose vertices can be split into two disjoint independent sets
A directed graph with edges going in exactly two directions only
A graph where every vertex has exactly two edges connected to it
A graph that contains exactly two connected components in total
explanation: A bipartite graph's vertices can be divided into two disjoint sets such that every edge connects a vertex from one set to the other.

text: How many edges does a tree with n vertices have?
options: n
explanation: A tree with n vertices always has exactly n - 1 edges, as it is a connected acyclic graph with the minimum edges needed.

text: What is the worst-case height of an unbalanced BST with n nodes?
options: O(sqrt(n))
O(n log n)
O(log n)
O(n)
explanation: An unbalanced BST can degenerate into a linked list when elements are inserted in sorted order, giving a height of O(n).

text: What is a strongly connected component in a directed graph?
options: A subgraph where every vertex is reachable from every other vertex
A subgraph containing exactly one vertex with no outgoing edges
A subgraph where all vertices have the same in-degree value
A maximal set of vertices with no edges between any of them
explanation: A strongly connected component is a maximal subgraph where there is a directed path from every vertex to every other vertex.

text: What is the Euler's formula for connected planar graphs?
options: V + E - F = 2
V + E + F = 2
V - E - F = 2
V - E + F = 2
explanation: Euler's formula states V - E + F = 2 for connected planar graphs, where V is vertices, E is edges, and F is faces.

text: What is a threaded binary tree?
options: A binary tree where null pointers are replaced with in-order links
A binary tree where each node stores a thread identifier
A binary tree stored across multiple threads of execution
A binary tree that requires multi-threaded access for traversal
explanation: A threaded binary tree replaces null child pointers with pointers to the in-order predecessor or successor, enabling traversal without recursion.

text: What is a hypergraph?
options: A graph where edges can connect any number of vertices
A three-dimensional representation of a standard graph
A graph where vertices are organized in a grid pattern
A graph where each edge connects exactly two vertices
explanation: A hypergraph generalizes a graph by allowing edges (hyperedges) to connect any number of vertices, not just two.

text: What is the chromatic number of a graph?
options: The maximum degree of any single vertex in the graph
The minimum number of colors needed to color all vertices
The number of connected components in the given graph
The total count of edges present in the entire graph
explanation: The chromatic number is the minimum number of colors needed to color the graph's vertices such that no two adjacent vertices share the same color.

text: What is the maximum number of edges in a simple undirected graph with n vertices?
explanation: A simple undirected graph with n vertices can have at most n(n-1)/2 edges, achieved when every pair of vertices is connected (complete graph).

text: What is a k-ary tree?
options: A tree with k root nodes and shared leaf structure
A tree where each node has exactly k children always
A tree where each node has at most k children nodes
A tree that has exactly k levels from root to leaves
explanation: A k-ary tree is a rooted tree where each node has at most k children. A binary tree is a special case where k=2.

text: What does linear search do?
options: Uses hashing to find the target element value
Sorts elements first then searches for target
Checks elements sequentially from start to end
Divides the array in half at each comparison
explanation: Linear search examines each element sequentially from the beginning until the target is found or the end is reached.

text: What is the best-case time complexity of linear search?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: The best case of linear search is O(1), which occurs when the target element is found at the first position.

text: What is required for binary search to work correctly?
options: The array must be sorted beforehand
The array must have an even number of items
The array must contain unique elements only
The array must be stored in a linked list
explanation: Binary search requires the array to be sorted so it can eliminate half of the remaining elements at each comparison step.

text: What is the worst-case time complexity of binary search?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
explanation: Binary search halves the search space at each step, giving a worst-case time complexity of O(log n).

text: In binary search, what happens if the target is less than the middle element?
options: Search continues in the right half only
The search terminates with element not found
Search continues in the left half only
Search restarts from the beginning again
explanation: If the target is less than the middle element, binary search continues in the left half since the array is sorted.

text: Which search algorithm does not require sorted data?
options: Binary search
Fibonacci search
Interpolation search
Linear search
explanation: Linear search works on unsorted data by checking each element one by one without requiring any ordering.

text: What is the space complexity of iterative binary search?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: Iterative binary search uses only a constant amount of extra space for variables like low, high, and mid.

text: What is the worst-case time complexity of linear search?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: In the worst case, linear search must examine all n elements before finding the target or determining it is absent.

text: What value does a search algorithm return if the element is not found?
options: Typically negative one or a sentinel value
The last index of the array searched
The middle index of the array searched
The first index of the array searched
explanation: Most search implementations return -1 or a special sentinel value to indicate the target element was not found.

text: How many comparisons does binary search make on 1024 sorted elements at most?
explanation: Binary search on 1024 elements makes at most log2(1024) = 10 comparisons.

text: How does interpolation search improve over binary search?
options: It estimates position based on value distribution
It uses extra memory to cache previous search results
It works on unsorted arrays unlike binary search
It always guarantees O(1) time for any input data
explanation: Interpolation search estimates the target's position based on the value distribution, performing better on uniformly distributed sorted data.

text: What is the average-case time complexity of interpolation search on uniform data?
options: O(log n)
O(log log n)
O(sqrt(n))
O(n)
explanation: On uniformly distributed sorted data, interpolation search has an average-case time complexity of O(log log n).

text: What is exponential search used for?
options: Finding range for binary search on unbounded arrays
Searching in a hash table with open addressing
Finding an element in an unsorted linked list
Searching in a balanced binary search tree structure
explanation: Exponential search finds the range where the target may exist by doubling the index, then applies binary search within that range.

text: What is the time complexity of searching in a balanced BST?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: A balanced BST maintains O(log n) height, so searching follows a path from root to leaf in O(log n) time.

text: What is ternary search?
options: A search that compares three elements at each single step
A search that uses three different search algorithms together
A search that requires three sorted arrays simultaneously
A search that divides the search space into three equal parts
explanation: Ternary search divides the sorted search space into three equal parts using two midpoints, recursively narrowing down to the target.

text: What is jump search and what is its optimal block size?
options: Search every other element; block size is always two
Jump to random positions; block size is chosen randomly
Skip logarithmic blocks; block size is always log of n
Skip fixed blocks then linear search; block size is sqrt(n)
explanation: Jump search skips ahead by fixed blocks of size sqrt(n), then performs linear search within the block where the target may exist.

text: What is the space complexity of recursive binary search?
options: O(n log n)
O(log n)
O(n)
explanation: Recursive binary search uses O(log n) space due to the call stack, as each recursive call halves the problem size.

text: How does Fibonacci search determine the split point?
options: It always splits the array exactly at the golden ratio
It uses Fibonacci numbers to divide the search space
It uses random Fibonacci-like sequences for splitting
It hashes elements using Fibonacci number as the key
explanation: Fibonacci search uses Fibonacci numbers to determine split points, dividing the array into unequal parts based on consecutive Fibonacci numbers.

text: What is the key advantage of binary search over linear search?
options: It can find multiple elements in a single search
It has O(log n) time versus O(n) on sorted data
It uses less memory than linear search algorithm
It works on any type of data without restrictions
explanation: Binary search's O(log n) time complexity is significantly faster than linear search's O(n) for large sorted datasets.

text: What happens if binary search is applied to an unsorted array?
options: It will automatically sort the array first then search
It will always return the correct result anyway
It will convert to linear search as a fallback mode
It may skip the target and return incorrect result
explanation: Binary search assumes sorted order to eliminate halves; on unsorted data, it may skip the target element entirely, producing incorrect results.

text: What is the worst-case time complexity of interpolation search?
options: O(n)
O(sqrt(n))
O(log log n)
O(log n)
explanation: In the worst case (e.g., exponentially distributed data), interpolation search degrades to O(n) as it may scan nearly all elements.

text: How does exponential search combine with binary search?
options: It uses exponentiation to compute hash for direct access
It finds a range by doubling then binary searches within
It applies binary search on exponentially growing subarrays
It sorts the array exponentially then searches linearly
explanation: Exponential search first finds the range [i/2, i] where the target lies by doubling i, then applies binary search within that range, achieving O(log n).

text: What is the time complexity of search in a skip list?
options: O(log n) expected with O(n) worst case
O(n log n) expected and O(n^2) worst case
O(n) in both average and worst cases
O(1) in all cases due to direct access
explanation: A skip list provides O(log n) expected search time due to multiple levels of pointers, but worst case is O(n) if levels are poorly distributed.

text: What is the lower bound for comparison-based searching in sorted arrays?
options: O(log n)
O(sqrt(n))
O(n)
explanation: Any comparison-based search on a sorted array requires at least Omega(log n) comparisons in the worst case, proven by information-theoretic arguments.

text: How does search work in a van Emde Boas tree?
options: It exploits universe size for O(log log u) operations
It uses comparison-based binary search on tree nodes
It performs breadth-first search on a balanced binary tree
It hashes each element for constant time access always
explanation: A van Emde Boas tree supports search, insert, and delete in O(log log u) time where u is the universe size, using recursive decomposition.

text: What is a fractional cascading technique?
options: Dividing search space into fractional geometric sections
Cascading hash functions for multi-level collision handling
Sharing elements between catalogs to speed up searches
Splitting arrays into fractions for parallel search
explanation: Fractional cascading propagates a fraction of elements from one sorted list to the next, reducing k binary searches from O(k log n) to O(k + log n).

text: What is the time complexity of searching in an optimal binary search tree?
options: O(1) since the tree is precomputed optimally
O(n) for every possible search query
O(n^2) due to the overhead of optimal structure
O(log n) or better based on access frequencies
explanation: An optimal BST minimizes expected search time based on known access frequencies, achieving O(log n) or better weighted average search time.

text: What is the two-pointer search technique?
options: Using two separate arrays for parallel search
Using two recursive calls to divide the search space
Using two hash tables to resolve search collisions
Using two indices moving toward each other or same direction
explanation: The two-pointer technique uses two indices that move toward each other or in the same direction to solve problems like pair-sum in O(n) time on sorted arrays.

text: What is the complexity of searching in a perfectly balanced k-d tree?
options: O(k * log n)
O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: Range search in a d-dimensional k-d tree takes O(n^(1-1/d) + k) time where k is the number of reported points, due to the spatial partitioning structure.

text: What is the sentinel linear search optimization?
options: Adding a sentinel at the end to eliminate bound checking
Using multiple sentinels to divide array into segments
Replacing all elements with sentinels then binary search
Sorting the array first and placing sentinel in middle
explanation: Sentinel linear search places the target at the end of the array, eliminating the need for a separate bounds check in each iteration, roughly halving comparisons.

text: What is the basic idea behind bubble sort?
options: Insert each element into its correct sorted position
Select the minimum element and place it at start
Repeatedly swap adjacent elements if they are wrong
Divide array into halves and merge them sorted
