explanation: Since larger values are always stored in the right subtree of a BST, following right child pointers until there is no right child leads to the maximum element.

text: Which tree traversal is commonly used to delete a binary tree safely (deleting children before the parent)?
options: Pre-order traversal
In-order traversal
Post-order traversal
Level-order traversal
explanation: Post-order traversal (Left, Right, Root) processes children before the parent, making it suitable for safely deleting a tree since child nodes are freed before their parent.

text: How many leaf nodes does a full binary tree with n internal nodes have?
options: n
2n
explanation: A full binary tree (every internal node has exactly 2 children) with n internal nodes has exactly n + 1 leaf nodes.

text: What is the balance factor in an AVL tree?
options: The number of nodes in the tree
The difference in heights of the left and right subtrees of a node
The total height of the tree
The number of rotations performed
explanation: The balance factor of a node in an AVL tree is the height of its left subtree minus the height of its right subtree. It must be -1, 0, or 1 for the tree to be balanced.

text: When is a right-left (RL) rotation performed in an AVL tree?
options: When a node is inserted in the left subtree of the left child
When a node is inserted in the right subtree of the left child
When a node is inserted in the left subtree of the right child
When a node is inserted in the right subtree of the right child
explanation: An RL rotation is needed when the imbalance is caused by inserting into the left subtree of the right child. A right rotation on the right child is followed by a left rotation on the node.

text: How is a node with two children deleted from a BST?
options: Simply remove the node
Replace it with its in-order successor or predecessor, then delete that replacement node
Swap it with the root and remove the root
Delete both children first, then remove the node
explanation: To delete a node with two children, replace its value with its in-order successor (smallest in right subtree) or predecessor (largest in left subtree), then delete that replacement node.

text: What is Morris traversal and what is its space complexity?
options: A traversal using two stacks with O(n) space
An in-order traversal using threading to achieve O(1) space without a stack or recursion
A traversal that visits nodes randomly in O(n) space
A BFS traversal using O(n) space
explanation: Morris traversal achieves O(1) space in-order traversal by temporarily creating threaded links (predecessor's right pointer to the current node) instead of using a stack.

text: What is a Red-Black tree's maximum height in terms of the number of nodes n?
options: Exactly log2(n)
At most 2 * log2(n + 1)
Exactly n
At most n/2
explanation: A Red-Black tree has a maximum height of 2 * log2(n + 1) because the black-height constraints ensure the tree stays approximately balanced.

text: What is a segment tree's time complexity for a range sum query after O(n) construction?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
O(√n)
explanation: A segment tree answers range queries (sum, min, max) in O(log n) time after O(n) construction, by combining precomputed results for segments that cover the query range.

text: How can you verify if a given binary tree is a valid BST?
options: Check if each node is greater than its children
Perform in-order traversal and verify the result is strictly increasing
Check if the root is the median value
Count the number of nodes and compare with height
explanation: A valid BST produces a strictly increasing sequence when traversed in-order. Alternatively, recursively check that each node's value falls within valid min/max bounds.

text: Why is bottom-up heap construction O(n) rather than O(n log n)?
options: Because it skips half the elements
Because most nodes are near the leaves and require very few sift-down operations, forming a convergent series
Because it uses insertion sort internally
Because it only processes the root node
explanation: In bottom-up construction, nodes near the leaves (the majority) need very little sifting. The total work sums to O(n) because the series 1*n/2 + 2*n/4 + 3*n/8 + ... converges to O(n).

text: What is a B-tree primarily used for?
options: In-memory sorting
Maintaining sorted data in database and file systems with minimal disk reads
Network routing
String pattern matching
explanation: B-trees are designed for storage systems that read/write large blocks of data. Their wide branching factor minimizes the number of disk accesses for search, insert, and delete.

text: What is the LCA (Lowest Common Ancestor) of two nodes in a binary tree?
options: The root of the tree
The deepest node that is an ancestor of both given nodes
The parent of the deeper node
The node at the midpoint between the two nodes
explanation: The LCA of two nodes is the deepest (lowest) node in the tree that has both given nodes as descendants, where a node can be a descendant of itself.

text: What is the time complexity of LCA computation using Euler tour and sparse table after O(n log n) preprocessing?
options: O(log n) per query
O(1) per query
O(n) per query
O(√n) per query
explanation: After O(n log n) preprocessing with Euler tour + sparse table for range minimum query, each LCA query is answered in O(1) time.

text: What is Heavy-Light Decomposition (HLD) used for?
options: Balancing binary trees
Decomposing a tree into chains so that any root-to-leaf path crosses O(log n) chains, enabling efficient path queries
Finding the heaviest subtree
Removing heavy nodes from the tree
explanation: HLD decomposes a tree into heavy and light chains. Any path from root to a leaf crosses at most O(log n) chains, allowing path queries and updates in O(log^2 n) with a segment tree.

text: Without lazy propagation, what is the time complexity of a range update on a segment tree, and how does lazy propagation improve it?
options: O(n) without, O(log n) with lazy propagation
O(n log n) without, O(n) with lazy propagation
O(log n) without, O(1) with lazy propagation
Both are O(n)
explanation: Without lazy propagation, updating all elements in a range requires O(n) individual updates. Lazy propagation defers updates to children, reducing range updates to O(log n) by pushing changes down only when needed.

text: What is the amortized time complexity of splay tree operations?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
O(√n)
explanation: Splay trees have O(log n) amortized time for search, insert, and delete operations. Individual operations may take O(n) but the splaying operation ensures overall amortized efficiency.

text: What is a Fenwick tree's advantage over a segment tree?
options: Fenwick trees support more operations
Fenwick trees use less memory (n elements vs 4n) and have smaller constant factors while supporting prefix operations
Fenwick trees have better worst-case complexity
Fenwick trees support range updates natively
explanation: A Fenwick tree (Binary Indexed Tree) uses only an array of size n and has very small constants, making it faster and more memory-efficient than a segment tree for prefix sum/update operations.

text: How much extra space does each update operation use in a persistent segment tree?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
O(n log n)
explanation: Each update in a persistent segment tree creates O(log n) new nodes (one per level on the update path) while sharing all unchanged subtrees with the previous version.

text: What is the Centroid Decomposition of a tree used for?
options: Finding the center of mass of weighted nodes
Recursively finding and removing centroids to create a decomposition tree enabling efficient path-based queries
Balancing unbalanced trees
Finding the median node
explanation: Centroid decomposition recursively finds the centroid, removes it, and decomposes subtrees. The resulting decomposition tree has O(log n) depth and enables efficient path queries.

text: What is the time complexity of range update and point query in a Fenwick tree?
options: O(n) for each
O(log n) for each
O(1) for update, O(n) for query
O(n log n) for each
explanation: Using a difference array technique with a Fenwick tree, both range updates and point queries can be performed in O(log n) time.

text: What is the time complexity of constructing an optimal BST given n keys with known access probabilities?
options: O(n)
explanation: The optimal BST problem (Knuth's algorithm) uses dynamic programming with O(n^2) subproblems, each taking O(n) time to compute, giving O(n^3) overall. Knuth's optimization reduces it to O(n^2).

text: How does a treap maintain both BST and heap properties simultaneously?
options: By using two separate trees
Each node has a key (BST-ordered) and a random priority (heap-ordered); rotations maintain both properties
By sorting elements twice
By storing elements in two arrays
explanation: A treap assigns each node a key and a random priority. The keys satisfy BST order and priorities satisfy heap order. Rotations upon insertion/deletion maintain both properties, giving expected O(log n) height.

text: What is the difference between BFS and DFS?
options: BFS uses a stack and DFS uses a queue
BFS explores neighbors level by level using a queue, while DFS goes deep using a stack
BFS is faster than DFS
DFS finds shortest paths but BFS does not
explanation: BFS uses a queue to explore all neighbors at the current depth before moving deeper. DFS uses a stack (or recursion) to go as deep as possible before backtracking.

text: What does DFS stand for?
options: Direct First Search
Depth First Search
Double Find Search
Data Flow System
explanation: DFS stands for Depth-First Search, a graph traversal algorithm that explores as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking.

text: How many edges does a spanning tree of a connected graph with V vertices contain?
options: V
2V
explanation: A spanning tree includes all V vertices and exactly V-1 edges, forming a connected acyclic subgraph (tree) that spans the entire graph.

text: Can BFS be used to find the shortest path in an unweighted graph?
options: No, only Dijkstra's algorithm can find shortest paths
Yes, BFS finds the shortest path in terms of number of edges in an unweighted graph
Only if the graph is a tree
Only if the graph has no cycles
explanation: In an unweighted graph, BFS guarantees finding the shortest path (fewest edges) from the source to every reachable vertex because it explores vertices in order of distance.

text: What is the space complexity of BFS on a graph with V vertices?
options: O(V)
O(E)
explanation: BFS requires O(V) space for the queue and the visited array, as in the worst case all vertices might be in the queue simultaneously.

text: What is an acyclic graph?
options: A graph with exactly one cycle
A graph that contains no cycles
A graph with all vertices connected
A graph with no edges
explanation: An acyclic graph is a graph that has no cycles. A directed acyclic graph (DAG) is a directed graph with no directed cycles.

text: In a weighted graph, what do the edge weights typically represent?
options: The number of vertices
Costs, distances, or capacities associated with traveling along that edge
The color of the edge
The degree of adjacent vertices
explanation: Edge weights in a weighted graph represent numerical values such as cost, distance, bandwidth, or capacity, and are used in algorithms like Dijkstra's and Kruskal's.

text: How is a graph typically represented in code using an adjacency list?
options: As a 2D array of size V×V
As an array of linked lists (or arrays), where each vertex has a list of its neighbors
As a single sorted array
As a hash table mapping edges to vertices
explanation: An adjacency list stores a list of neighboring vertices for each vertex. This is memory-efficient for sparse graphs, using O(V + E) space.

text: What happens if DFS visits a vertex that has already been visited in a directed graph?
options: It always means a cycle exists
It depends on whether the previously visited vertex is still on the current DFS path
DFS stops immediately
The graph is invalid
explanation: In a directed graph, revisiting a vertex only indicates a cycle if that vertex is still on the current recursion stack (back edge). Cross edges and forward edges also revisit vertices but don't form cycles.

text: What is the minimum number of edges in a connected undirected graph with V vertices?
options: V
explanation: A connected undirected graph with V vertices needs at least V-1 edges, forming a tree. Removing any edge would disconnect the graph.

text: What is the time complexity of Dijkstra's algorithm using a Fibonacci heap?
options: O(V log V + E)
O(E log V)
O(V * E)
explanation: With a Fibonacci heap, Dijkstra's algorithm achieves O(V log V + E) because decrease-key operations take O(1) amortized time and extract-min takes O(log V) amortized.

text: How does Kruskal's algorithm find a minimum spanning tree?
options: By starting from a vertex and adding the cheapest edge to a neighbor
By sorting all edges by weight and adding them if they don't create a cycle, using Union-Find
By finding the shortest path from source to all vertices
By removing the most expensive edges
explanation: Kruskal's algorithm sorts all edges by weight and greedily adds the cheapest edge that doesn't form a cycle, using Union-Find to detect cycles. It runs in O(E log E) time.

text: What is topological sorting and on which type of graph is it performed?
options: Sorting vertices by degree on any graph
A linear ordering of vertices such that for every directed edge (u,v), u comes before v; only on DAGs
Sorting edges by weight in an undirected graph
Ordering vertices alphabetically
explanation: Topological sort produces a linear ordering of vertices in a DAG such that every edge goes from an earlier vertex to a later one. It is undefined for graphs with cycles.

text: How does the Bellman-Ford algorithm differ from Dijkstra's algorithm?
options: Bellman-Ford is always faster
Bellman-Ford can handle negative edge weights and detect negative cycles, while Dijkstra cannot
Dijkstra handles negative weights but Bellman-Ford does not
They produce different results on the same graph
explanation: Bellman-Ford works with negative edge weights (O(VE) time) and can detect negative-weight cycles. Dijkstra requires non-negative weights but is faster with O((V+E) log V).

text: What is the time complexity of Prim's algorithm using a binary heap?
options: O(E log V)
O(V * E)
O(V + E)
explanation: Prim's algorithm with a binary heap processes each edge once and performs O(E) decrease-key operations (each O(log V)) plus O(V) extract-min operations, giving O(E log V).

text: How can you detect a cycle in an undirected graph using DFS?
options: Check if any vertex has degree > 2
If DFS visits an already-visited vertex that is not the parent of the current vertex, a cycle exists
Count the number of edges; a cycle exists if E > V
Use topological sort
explanation: During DFS in an undirected graph, if you encounter an already-visited vertex that is not the parent of the current vertex, you have found a back edge indicating a cycle.

text: What does the Union-Find path compression optimization do?
options: It compresses the data stored at each node
It flattens the tree by making every node point directly to the root during find operations
It removes unused paths from the graph
It compresses the graph into fewer vertices
explanation: Path compression makes each node encountered during a find operation point directly to the root, flattening the tree structure and speeding up future operations.

text: What is the Floyd-Warshall algorithm used for?
options: Finding the minimum spanning tree
Finding shortest paths between all pairs of vertices
Topological sorting
Detecting bipartite graphs
explanation: Floyd-Warshall computes shortest paths between every pair of vertices in O(V^3) time and O(V^2) space. It works with negative weights but not negative cycles.

text: What is the time complexity of finding connected components in an undirected graph?
options: O(V + E)
O(V * E)
explanation: Connected components can be found using BFS or DFS starting from each unvisited vertex. The total time is O(V + E) since each vertex and edge is processed at most once.

text: What is a back edge in DFS and what does it indicate?
options: An edge going from a node to its parent
An edge from a node to an ancestor in the DFS tree, indicating a cycle in directed graphs
An edge that was removed during DFS
An edge connecting two different components
explanation: A back edge connects a vertex to one of its ancestors in the DFS tree. In directed graphs, back edges indicate cycles. In undirected graphs, any edge to a visited non-parent vertex forms a back edge.

text: What is the time complexity of Tarjan's algorithm for finding strongly connected components?
options: O(V + E)
O(V * E)
explanation: Tarjan's algorithm uses a single DFS pass with a stack to find all SCCs in O(V + E) time, making it optimal for this problem.

text: According to the duality principle in network flow, what is equal to the capacity of the minimum s-t cut?
options: The number of edges in the graph
The maximum flow from source s to sink t
The minimum spanning tree weight
The number of vertices on the shortest path
explanation: The Max-Flow Min-Cut duality states that the value of the maximum flow from source to sink exactly equals the capacity of the minimum cut separating them, a fundamental result in combinatorial optimization.

text: What is the time complexity of the Edmonds-Karp algorithm for maximum flow?
options: O(V * E)
explanation: Edmonds-Karp is a BFS-based implementation of Ford-Fulkerson that finds augmenting paths using BFS. It runs in O(V * E^2) time.

text: What is an Euler path in a graph?
options: A path that visits every vertex exactly once
A path that traverses every edge exactly once
The shortest path between two vertices
A path with minimum total weight
explanation: An Euler path traverses every edge of the graph exactly once (vertices may be revisited). An undirected graph has an Euler path if and only if it has exactly 0 or 2 vertices with odd degree.

text: What is the time complexity of finding all articulation points in an undirected graph?
options: O(V + E)
O(V * E)
explanation: Articulation points can be found using a modified DFS in O(V + E) time by tracking discovery times and low values for each vertex.

text: What is Johnson's algorithm for all-pairs shortest paths?
options: Running Dijkstra from every vertex without preprocessing
Reweighting edges using Bellman-Ford to eliminate negative weights, then running Dijkstra from every vertex
Using Floyd-Warshall on a reduced graph
A divide-and-conquer approach for shortest paths
explanation: Johnson's algorithm uses Bellman-Ford to compute a potential function that reweights edges to be non-negative, then runs Dijkstra from each vertex. Total time is O(V^2 log V + VE).

text: What is a Hamiltonian path and why is finding one computationally hard?
options: A shortest path in a weighted graph, solvable in polynomial time
A path visiting every vertex exactly once; the decision problem is NP-complete
A path traversing every edge exactly once; solvable in O(V + E)
The longest path in a tree
explanation: A Hamiltonian path visits every vertex exactly once. Determining whether such a path exists is NP-complete, meaning no known polynomial-time algorithm exists for general graphs.

text: What is the Dinic's algorithm time complexity for maximum flow?
options: O(V * E)
explanation: Dinic's algorithm achieves O(V^2 * E) time by using BFS to build level graphs and DFS for blocking flows. For unit-capacity graphs, it runs in O(E√V).

text: What is the 2-SAT problem and how is it related to graph algorithms?
options: Finding two satisfying assignments using brute force
A satisfiability problem solvable in polynomial time by building an implication graph and finding SCCs
A graph coloring problem
An NP-complete problem with no efficient solution
explanation: 2-SAT determines satisfiability of CNF formulas with at most 2 literals per clause. It is solvable in O(V + E) by constructing an implication graph and checking if any variable and its negation are in the same SCC.

text: What is the time complexity of finding a minimum spanning tree using Borůvka's algorithm?
options: O(E log V)
O(V + E)
O(E log E)
explanation: Borůvka's algorithm finds the MST in O(E log V) time by repeatedly finding the minimum outgoing edge for each component and merging components, halving the number of components each round.

text: What is the divide and conquer approach?
options: Solving a problem by trying all possible solutions
Breaking a problem into smaller subproblems, solving them, and combining the solutions
Using a greedy choice at each step
Storing solutions to avoid recomputation
explanation: Divide and conquer breaks a problem into smaller independent subproblems, solves them recursively, and then combines the results to form the final solution.

text: What are the two key properties required for a dynamic programming solution?
options: Greedy choice and local optima
Optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems
Random choices and large inputs
Divide and conquer and backtracking
explanation: Dynamic programming requires optimal substructure (an optimal solution contains optimal solutions to subproblems) and overlapping subproblems (the same subproblems are solved multiple times).

text: What is an example of a problem solved by the greedy approach?
options: 0/1 Knapsack problem
Making change with the fewest coins (when denominations are canonical)
Traveling Salesman Problem
Matrix chain multiplication
explanation: The coin change problem (with standard denominations like 1, 5, 10, 25) can be solved greedily by always choosing the largest denomination that fits.

text: What is the key difference between greedy algorithms and dynamic programming?
options: Greedy algorithms are always slower
Greedy algorithms make locally optimal choices without reconsidering, while DP considers all subproblem solutions
Dynamic programming never gives optimal solutions
They are the same approach
explanation: Greedy algorithms make the locally optimal choice at each step and never reconsider. DP exhaustively evaluates all subproblem solutions to guarantee a globally optimal result.

text: What is the bottom-up approach in dynamic programming?
options: Solving the problem using recursion from the top
Solving smaller subproblems first and building up to the original problem using a table
Randomly solving subproblems
Starting from the solution and working backwards
explanation: The bottom-up (tabulation) approach solves the smallest subproblems first, stores their results in a table, and iteratively builds up to solve the original problem.

text: How does backtracking reduce the search space compared to brute force?
options: It sorts the candidates first
It abandons partial solutions as soon as they are determined to be invalid, avoiding exploration of dead-end branches
It uses hashing to skip solutions
It randomly selects solutions to test
explanation: Backtracking prunes the search tree by abandoning a partial solution as soon as it violates the problem constraints, avoiding the exploration of all solutions derived from that invalid partial solution.

text: What is the time complexity of computing Fibonacci numbers using memoization?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: With memoization, each Fibonacci value from F(0) to F(n) is computed exactly once and stored, giving O(n) total time compared to O(2^n) for naive recursion.

text: Which algorithm design paradigm does merge sort belong to?
options: Greedy
Dynamic programming
Divide and conquer
Backtracking
explanation: Merge sort is a classic divide and conquer algorithm: it divides the array in half, recursively sorts each half, and then merges the sorted halves.

text: What is the purpose of the top-down approach in dynamic programming?
options: To sort elements from top to bottom
To solve the original problem recursively and cache results to avoid redundant computation
To iterate through the array from beginning to end
To find the maximum element first
explanation: The top-down approach (memoization) starts solving the original problem, recursively solves subproblems, and caches their results in a table to avoid recomputation.

text: What is a brute force approach to problem solving?
options: An optimized algorithm
An approach that tries every possible solution and checks which one is correct
An approach that uses heuristics
An approach based on random sampling
explanation: Brute force systematically enumerates all possible solutions to find the correct one. It is straightforward but often inefficient for large inputs.

text: What is the time complexity of the dynamic programming solution for the Longest Common Subsequence?
options: O(n)
O(n * m) where n and m are the lengths of the two sequences
explanation: The LCS DP solution fills an (n+1) × (m+1) table, computing each cell in O(1). The total time is O(n * m) where n and m are the string lengths.

text: Why does the greedy approach fail for the 0/1 Knapsack problem but work for the fractional Knapsack?
options: The greedy approach is always incorrect
In 0/1 Knapsack, items cannot be divided, so a locally optimal choice may block a better global solution
The fractional Knapsack has fewer items
Both can be solved greedily
explanation: In fractional Knapsack, you can take fractions of items by value/weight ratio, and the greedy choice property holds. In 0/1 Knapsack, taking an entire high-ratio item may prevent fitting a better combination.

text: How does the N-Queens backtracking solution work?
options: It places all queens randomly and checks if the placement is valid
It places queens one row at a time, backtracking when a placement conflicts with existing queens
It sorts the board positions and assigns queens greedily
It uses dynamic programming to find all placements
explanation: The N-Queens backtracking solution places one queen per row, checking column and diagonal conflicts. If no valid position exists in a row, it backtracks to try a different position in the previous row.

text: What is the Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS) problem's optimal time complexity?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
explanation: The LIS can be solved in O(n log n) using patience sorting or binary search with a dynamic approach, improving upon the O(n^2) basic DP solution.

text: What is Huffman coding and which algorithm design paradigm does it use?
options: A sorting algorithm using divide and conquer
An optimal prefix-free encoding algorithm using the greedy approach
A string matching algorithm using dynamic programming
A graph algorithm using backtracking
explanation: Huffman coding is a greedy algorithm that builds an optimal prefix-free binary code by repeatedly merging the two lowest-frequency symbols.

text: What is the difference between the 0/1 Knapsack and the fractional Knapsack problem?
options: 0/1 Knapsack allows fractions while fractional does not
In 0/1 Knapsack items are taken whole or not at all; in fractional Knapsack items can be divided
They are the same problem
Fractional Knapsack is always harder
explanation: In 0/1 Knapsack, each item is either taken completely or left (DP solution). In fractional Knapsack, items can be partially taken (greedy solution by value-to-weight ratio).

text: What is the rod cutting problem in dynamic programming?
options: Cutting a graph into components
Finding the maximum revenue by cutting a rod of length n into pieces with given prices for each length
Dividing an array into equal parts
Cutting a string at specific positions
explanation: The rod cutting problem seeks to maximize revenue by cutting a rod of length n into pieces, where each length has a given price. DP solves it in O(n^2) time.

text: What is branch and bound and how does it differ from backtracking?
options: They are the same algorithm
Branch and bound uses bounds (upper/lower) to prune branches that cannot yield better solutions than the current best
Branch and bound only works on trees
Backtracking uses bounds while branch and bound does not
explanation: Branch and bound extends backtracking by computing bounds on partial solutions. If a bound shows a branch cannot improve the best-known solution, that branch is pruned.

text: What is the space optimization technique for the 0/1 Knapsack DP solution?
options: Use a 3D array instead of 2D
Use only two 1D arrays (or one array traversed in reverse) since each row only depends on the previous row
Use a hash table
Use a linked list
explanation: Since each row of the DP table depends only on the previous row, space can be reduced from O(nW) to O(W) by using a single 1D array and filling it in reverse order.

text: What is the activity selection problem and which approach solves it optimally?
options: Selecting the minimum number of activities; solved by DP
Selecting the maximum number of non-overlapping activities; solved by a greedy algorithm that sorts by finish time
Selecting activities with maximum total weight; solved by brute force
Assigning activities to workers; solved by backtracking
explanation: The activity selection problem maximizes the number of non-overlapping activities. The greedy approach sorts by finish time and always picks the earliest-finishing compatible activity.

text: What is the time complexity of the matrix chain multiplication DP solution?
options: O(n log n)
explanation: Matrix chain multiplication uses DP with O(n^2) subproblems, each taking O(n) time to solve (trying all split points), giving O(n^3) total time.

text: How does the meet-in-the-middle technique reduce the Subset Sum time from O(2^n) to approximately O(2^(n/2))?
options: By sorting the subsets before checking
By splitting elements into two halves, enumerating all sums in each half, then combining using binary search or hashing
By using dynamic programming instead of brute force
By reducing the problem to a graph problem
explanation: Meet-in-the-middle splits n elements into two halves of n/2, generates all 2^(n/2) sums for each, then uses sorting and binary search (or hashing) to find complementary pairs that sum to the target.

text: What is the difference between Las Vegas and Monte Carlo randomized algorithms?
options: Las Vegas algorithms are deterministic
Las Vegas algorithms always produce correct results (with variable runtime), while Monte Carlo may produce incorrect results (with bounded runtime)
Monte Carlo algorithms are always faster
They are the same type of algorithm
explanation: Las Vegas algorithms always give correct results but their running time is a random variable (e.g., randomized quicksort). Monte Carlo algorithms have bounded time but may give incorrect results with some probability.

text: What is the time complexity of solving the edit distance problem using DP?
options: O(n)
O(n * m)
explanation: The edit distance (Levenshtein distance) between strings of length n and m is computed using a DP table of size (n+1) × (m+1), filled in O(nm) time.

text: Which of the following problems violates Bellman's principle of optimality and therefore cannot be solved by standard DP?
options: Shortest path problem
Longest simple path in a general graph
Matrix chain multiplication
Edit distance
explanation: The longest simple path problem in a general graph lacks optimal substructure because a longest simple path between two vertices may not contain the longest simple path between intermediate vertices (due to the no-repeat constraint).

text: What is the time complexity of the Held-Karp algorithm for the Traveling Salesman Problem?
options: O(n!)
explanation: The Held-Karp algorithm uses bitmask DP to solve TSP in O(n^2 * 2^n) time and O(n * 2^n) space, exponentially faster than the O(n!) brute-force approach.

text: What is the Knuth optimization for DP?
options: Using hash tables for memoization
An optimization for DP problems with the property that the optimal split point is monotone, reducing O(n^3) to O(n^2)
Parallelizing DP computations
Converting recursion to iteration
explanation: Knuth's optimization applies to interval DP problems where the optimal split point k for dp[i][j] is between k[i][j-1] and k[i+1][j]. This monotonicity reduces the time from O(n^3) to O(n^2).

text: What is the Convex Hull Trick optimization in dynamic programming?
options: Finding the convex hull of points
An optimization for DP transitions of the form dp[i] = min(dp[j] + b[j] * a[i]) using a set of lines
Computing convex combinations of solutions
A geometric algorithm for nearest neighbors
explanation: The Convex Hull Trick optimizes DP transitions where the recurrence has the form dp[i] = min/max over j of (dp[j] + b[j]*a[i]). It maintains a set of lines and queries in O(n) or O(n log n) total.

text: What is the difference between online and offline algorithms?
options: Online algorithms require internet access
Online algorithms process input piece by piece as it arrives, while offline algorithms have access to the entire input upfront
Offline algorithms are always slower
Online algorithms cannot be analyzed
explanation: Online algorithms must make decisions as each input element arrives without knowledge of future inputs. Offline algorithms can see the entire input before making any decisions. The competitive ratio measures an online algorithm's quality.

text: What is the time complexity of solving the Subset Sum problem using DP?
options: O(n * S) where S is the target sum (pseudo-polynomial)
O(n log n)
explanation: The DP solution for Subset Sum runs in O(n * S) time where n is the number of elements and S is the target sum. This is pseudo-polynomial because S is not polynomial in the input size.

text: What is the time complexity of inserting a word of length L into a trie?
options: O(L)
O(n)
O(L * n)
explanation: Inserting a word of length L into a trie requires traversing or creating L nodes, one for each character, giving O(L) time.

text: What is the advantage of a priority queue over a sorted array for maintaining a dynamic set?
options: Priority queues use less memory
Priority queues support O(log n) insertion while sorted arrays require O(n) for insertion
Priority queues allow O(1) random access
Sorted arrays cannot store integers
explanation: A priority queue (heap) supports O(log n) insertion and extract-min, while inserting into a sorted array requires shifting elements, taking O(n).

text: What does the find operation do in a Union-Find data structure?
options: Finds the minimum element in a set
Returns the representative (root) of the set containing a given element
Finds the union of two sets
Searches for an element in a sorted array
explanation: The find operation returns the representative (root) of the set that contains the given element, used to determine if two elements are in the same set.

text: What is a min-heap's property regarding the root element?
options: The root is the largest element
The root is the median element
The root is the smallest element
The root has no special property
explanation: In a min-heap, every parent is smaller than or equal to its children, so the root always holds the smallest element in the heap.

text: What is the time complexity of the extract-min operation in a binary min-heap?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
O(n log n)
explanation: Extract-min removes the root (minimum element), replaces it with the last element, and sifts down to restore the heap property, taking O(log n) time.

text: What is a trie primarily used for?
options: Sorting numbers
Efficient storage and retrieval of strings, supporting prefix-based operations
Graph traversal
Matrix operations
explanation: A trie (prefix tree) stores strings character by character, enabling efficient prefix searches, autocomplete, and dictionary operations.

text: What is the union operation in Union-Find?
options: Finding all elements in a set
Merging two disjoint sets into one
Splitting a set into two parts
Sorting elements within a set
explanation: The union operation merges the sets containing two given elements into a single set by connecting the root of one to the root of the other.

text: For a node at index i in a binary heap stored in an array (0-indexed), what is the index of its left child?
options: 2i
explanation: In a 0-indexed array representation of a binary heap, the left child of node at index i is at index 2i + 1, and the right child is at 2i + 2.

text: What is a multimap data structure?
options: A map that can store multiple values for the same key
A map that uses multiple hash functions
A map with multiple levels
A map that can only store maps
explanation: A multimap is a data structure similar to a map but allows multiple values to be associated with the same key, unlike a standard map which has unique keys.

text: What is the delete operation in a binary heap called?
options: Remove
Extract
Pop
Decrease-key followed by extract-min
explanation: To delete an arbitrary element from a binary heap, decrease its key to negative infinity (making it the minimum), then extract-min to remove it.

text: What is the amortized time complexity of Union-Find operations with both path compression and union by rank?
options: O(log n)
O(α(n)), where α is the inverse Ackermann function
O(n)
explanation: With both path compression and union by rank, the amortized time per operation is O(α(n)), where α is the inverse Ackermann function which grows incredibly slowly (≤ 4 for all practical n).

text: What is a Fibonacci heap's key advantage over a binary heap?
options: Simpler implementation
O(1) amortized decrease-key operation instead of O(log n)
Less memory usage
Faster extract-min
explanation: Fibonacci heaps support O(1) amortized decrease-key (vs O(log n) in binary heaps), which improves algorithms like Dijkstra's to O(V log V + E) from O((V+E) log V).

text: What is a suffix array and what problem does it help solve?
options: An array of suffix sums for range queries
A sorted array of all suffixes of a string, enabling efficient pattern matching and string analysis
An array storing the suffixes of Fibonacci numbers
A compressed version of a string
explanation: A suffix array is a sorted array of all suffixes of a string (stored as starting indices). It enables O(m log n) pattern matching and is used in many string algorithms.

text: What is a sparse table used for?
options: Storing sparse matrices
Answering static range minimum/maximum queries in O(1) after O(n log n) preprocessing
Building sparse graphs
Compressing data
explanation: A sparse table preprocesses an array in O(n log n) time and space to answer range minimum (or maximum) queries in O(1) time, suitable for static data.

text: How does a k-d tree determine which dimension to split on at each level?
options: It always splits on the first dimension
It cycles through the dimensions at each level (depth mod k)
It randomly selects a dimension
It splits on the dimension with the most variance
explanation: A standard k-d tree cycles through dimensions at each level: at depth d, it splits on dimension d mod k. This ensures all dimensions are used for partitioning the space.

text: What is the time complexity of merge operation in a binomial heap?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
O(n log n)
explanation: Merging two binomial heaps takes O(log n) time because each has at most O(log n) binomial trees, and the merge works like binary addition of the tree lists.

text: What is an interval tree used for?
options: Storing time intervals for scheduling
Finding all intervals that overlap with a given query interval efficiently
Measuring time intervals
Sorting intervals by length
explanation: An interval tree stores intervals and supports efficient queries for finding all intervals that overlap with a given point or interval, typically in O(log n + k) time where k is the output size.

text: What is the space complexity of a trie storing n strings with average length m over an alphabet of size σ?
options: O(n)
O(n * m * σ)
O(n * m)
O(σ)
explanation: In the worst case, a trie has up to n*m nodes, each with an array of σ pointers (one per alphabet character), giving O(n * m * σ) space. Compressed tries reduce this.

text: What is a balanced BST's advantage over a hash table for ordered operations?
options: Balanced BSTs have O(1) lookup
Balanced BSTs support ordered operations like range queries, in-order traversal, and predecessor/successor in O(log n)
Hash tables cannot store strings
Balanced BSTs use less memory
explanation: While hash tables offer O(1) average lookup, balanced BSTs support ordered operations such as range queries, finding min/max, and in-order traversal, all in O(log n) time.

text: What is a circular buffer and where is it commonly used?
options: A buffer that stores data in a circle shape on disk
A fixed-size buffer that wraps around, commonly used in streaming, producer-consumer patterns, and I/O buffering
A buffer that can grow infinitely
A buffer used only for circular linked lists
explanation: A circular buffer uses a fixed-size array with wrap-around. When it's full, new data overwrites the oldest. It's widely used in streaming, audio, and producer-consumer scenarios.

text: What is a van Emde Boas tree's space complexity?
options: O(n)
O(U) where U is the universe size
O(log U)
O(n log n)
explanation: A van Emde Boas tree requires O(U) space where U is the universe size. It supports insert, delete, search, predecessor, and successor all in O(log log U) time.

text: What is a rope data structure and what advantage does it offer over arrays for string operations?
options: A compressed string format
A balanced binary tree of strings that supports O(log n) concatenation, split, and insertion instead of O(n) for arrays
A linked list of characters
An encrypted string storage
explanation: A rope is a balanced binary tree where leaves hold short strings. It supports O(log n) concatenation, split, and insertion at arbitrary positions, much better than O(n) for array-based strings.

text: What is the order-statistic tree and what additional information does it maintain?
options: A tree that only stores statistics
An augmented BST that stores subtree sizes to support O(log n) rank and select queries
A tree that orders nodes by creation time
A tree that counts the number of queries
explanation: An order-statistic tree augments a balanced BST with subtree size at each node, enabling O(log n) rank (find rank of element) and select (find k-th smallest element) operations.

text: What is a wavelet tree used for?
options: Audio signal processing
Answering rank, select, and range frequency queries on sequences in O(log σ) time where σ is the alphabet size
Compressing images
Sorting wavelets
explanation: A wavelet tree is built over a sequence and supports rank, select, quantile, and range frequency queries in O(log σ) time using O(n log σ) space.

text: Which underlying data structure does the link-cut tree use internally to maintain preferred paths?
options: Red-Black trees
AVL trees
Splay trees
B-trees
explanation: Link-cut trees use splay trees to represent preferred paths (access paths). The splay tree's amortized O(log n) operations enable the link-cut tree to support link, cut, and path queries efficiently.

text: How much space does a succinct data structure use relative to the information-theoretic minimum Z?
options: O(Z) bits
Z + o(Z) bits, meaning within a lower-order additive term of the minimum
Exactly Z bits
2Z bits
explanation: A succinct data structure uses Z + o(Z) bits where Z is the information-theoretic minimum. The o(Z) term is asymptotically negligible compared to Z, making the space near-optimal.

text: What is a suffix tree and how does it relate to a suffix array?
options: They are completely unrelated data structures
A suffix tree is a compressed trie of all suffixes; a suffix array stores the same suffixes sorted, and they can be converted between in O(n) time
A suffix array is always preferred over a suffix tree
A suffix tree stores prefix information while a suffix array stores suffix information
explanation: A suffix tree is a compressed trie of all suffixes of a string, built in O(n) time. A suffix array is the sorted order of suffix starting positions. They are interconvertible in O(n) time.

text: What is the time complexity of the decrease-key operation in a Fibonacci heap?
options: O(log n) amortized
O(1) amortized
O(n)
O(log log n)
explanation: The decrease-key operation in a Fibonacci heap takes O(1) amortized time by cutting the node from its parent and adding it to the root list, with cascading cuts maintaining structural properties.

text: What is an x-fast trie and what time complexity does it achieve for predecessor queries?
options: O(log n)
O(log log U) using hash tables at each level of a binary trie over the universe
O(√n)
explanation: An x-fast trie stores a binary trie over the universe of size U with hash tables at each level, achieving O(log log U) predecessor/successor queries using O(n log U) space.

text: What is the weight-balanced tree's balance condition?
options: Left and right subtrees have equal height
The ratio of sizes of left and right subtrees is bounded by a constant α, where 1/4 ≤ α ≤ 1-1/√2
Each node has the same weight
The total weight equals n
explanation: A weight-balanced tree (BB[α] tree) ensures that for every node, both subtrees have at least α fraction of the total nodes. This guarantees O(log n) height and efficient rebalancing.

text: What is the time complexity of comparing two strings of length n for equality?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: Comparing two strings for equality requires checking each character pair, taking O(n) time in the worst case where all characters match except possibly the last.

text: Is 'ace' a valid subsequence of 'abcde'?
options: No, because the characters are not contiguous
Yes, because the characters appear in the same relative order in the original string
Only if we rearrange the original string
No, because 'ace' is not a word
explanation: Yes, 'ace' is a subsequence of 'abcde' because characters a, c, e appear in the original string in that same relative order, even though they are not contiguous.

text: How many total substrings does a string of length n have (including the empty string)?
options: n
explanation: A string of length n has n(n+1)/2 non-empty substrings (choosing start and end positions) plus the empty string, totaling n(n+1)/2 + 1.

text: In lexicographic order, which string comes first: 'apple' or 'application'?
options: 'application' because it is longer
'apple' because at the first differing position, 'e' < 'i'
They are considered equal
'apple' because shorter strings always come first
explanation: Lexicographic comparison goes character by character. Both share 'appl', but then 'e' (from 'apple') comes before 'i' (from 'application') in the alphabet, so 'apple' comes first.

text: What is the time complexity of reversing a string of length n?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
explanation: Reversing a string requires swapping characters from both ends toward the middle, touching each character once, taking O(n) time.

text: What is the time complexity of the naive (brute force) pattern matching algorithm?
options: O(n + m)
O(n * m) in the worst case
O(n)
O(m)
explanation: The naive approach tries the pattern at every position in the text, comparing up to m characters at each position. The worst case is O(n * m) for text length n and pattern length m.

text: How can you check if a string is a palindrome?
options: Sort the string and check if it's the same
Compare the string with its reverse
Check if all characters are the same
Count the number of vowels
explanation: A string is a palindrome if it reads the same forwards and backwards. Comparing the string with its reverse (or using two pointers from both ends) checks this in O(n) time.

text: What is the difference between a substring and a subsequence?
options: They are the same thing
A substring is contiguous characters; a subsequence can skip characters but maintains order
A subsequence is always longer
A substring can skip characters
explanation: A substring consists of consecutive characters from the original string, while a subsequence can skip characters but must maintain the relative order.

text: What does the term 'pattern matching' mean in the context of strings?
options: Creating patterns from strings
Finding occurrences of a pattern string within a text string
Matching two strings for equality
Sorting strings by pattern
explanation: Pattern matching (string searching) finds all occurrences of a shorter pattern string within a longer text string.

text: What is string immutability?
options: Strings that can be modified in place
Strings that cannot be changed after creation; any modification creates a new string
Strings stored in read-only memory
Strings with fixed length
explanation: In many languages (Java, Python, JavaScript), strings are immutable — once created, their characters cannot be changed. Any 'modification' produces a new string object.

text: What is the failure function (partial match table) in the KMP algorithm?
options: A table indicating which characters to skip in the text
A table storing the length of the longest proper prefix that is also a suffix for each prefix of the pattern
A table of hash values for the pattern
A table mapping characters to their positions
explanation: The KMP failure function for position i stores the length of the longest proper prefix of pattern[0..i] that is also a suffix. This determines where to continue matching after a mismatch.

text: How does the Rabin-Karp algorithm handle spurious matches?
options: It ignores them completely
It performs a character-by-character comparison when hash values match to verify the actual match
It uses a different hash function each time
It rehashes the entire text
explanation: When the rolling hash of a text window matches the pattern's hash, Rabin-Karp performs a full character comparison to confirm the match, since different strings can have the same hash.

text: What is the Z-array for a string S?
options: An array of zeros
Z[i] = length of the longest substring starting at i that matches a prefix of S
An array sorting characters alphabetically
The reverse of the string stored as an array
explanation: The Z-array stores Z[i] = the length of the longest substring starting at position i that matches a prefix of S. It can be computed in O(n) time and is used for pattern matching.

text: What is the key idea behind the Boyer-Moore algorithm?
options: Searching from left to right in the pattern
Comparing the pattern from right to left and using bad character and good suffix heuristics to skip positions
Using hash values for comparison
Building a trie of all substrings
explanation: Boyer-Moore compares the pattern from right to left. On a mismatch, it uses the bad character rule and good suffix rule to skip ahead, often achieving sub-linear time in practice.

text: What is the time complexity of computing the longest palindromic substring using dynamic programming?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
explanation: The DP approach uses a 2D table where dp[i][j] indicates if the substring from i to j is a palindrome. Filling this table takes O(n^2) time and space.

text: What is a rolling hash and how does it enable efficient string matching?
options: A hash that rolls dice for randomness
A hash function that can compute the hash of the next window in O(1) by removing the leading character and adding the trailing character
A hash that is computed once and never changes
A hash function specific to number sequences
explanation: A rolling hash updates the hash value incrementally when the window slides by one position: remove the contribution of the outgoing character and add the incoming character, both in O(1).

text: How does the KMP algorithm achieve O(n + m) time complexity?
options: By sorting the text first
By never re-comparing characters that have already been matched, using the failure function to resume matching
By using hash values instead of character comparison
By dividing the text into blocks
explanation: KMP uses the failure function to skip over previously matched characters on a mismatch. The text pointer never goes backward, ensuring O(n + m) time for text length n and pattern length m.

text: What is the time complexity of checking if one string is a rotation of another?
options: O(n) by concatenating the string with itself and searching for the other string
O(n log n)
explanation: String B is a rotation of string A if and only if B appears as a substring of A+A (A concatenated with itself). This can be checked in O(n) using KMP or similar.

text: In polynomial string hashing, what values are typically chosen for the base p and modulus M?
options: p = 1 and M = 10
p greater than the alphabet size and M a large prime to minimize collisions
Both should be powers of 2
p = 0 and M = the string length
explanation: For polynomial hashing, the base p should be greater than the alphabet size (e.g., 31 or 37 for lowercase letters) and M should be a large prime (e.g., 10^9 + 7) to minimize collision probability.

text: What is the best-case time complexity of the Boyer-Moore algorithm for pattern matching?
options: O(n * m)
O(n + m)
O(n / m)
O(n)
explanation: In the best case, Boyer-Moore can skip m characters at a time, achieving O(n/m) comparisons. This happens when the last character of the pattern doesn't appear in the text.

text: What is the time complexity of Manacher's algorithm for finding the longest palindromic substring?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
explanation: Manacher's algorithm finds the longest palindromic substring in O(n) time by exploiting symmetry properties of palindromes to avoid redundant comparisons.

text: What is the Aho-Corasick algorithm's time complexity for matching k patterns of total length m against a text of length n?
options: O(n * k)
O(n + m + z) where z is the number of matches
O(n * m)
O(k * m * n)
explanation: Aho-Corasick builds an automaton from all patterns in O(m) time and processes the text in O(n) time, reporting each match in O(1). Total time is O(n + m + z) where z is the number of matches.

text: What is the suffix automaton of a string?
options: An automaton that accepts only the suffixes of the string
A minimal DFA that accepts exactly all substrings of the string, built in O(n) time and space
An automaton that generates all permutations
A finite automaton for sorting strings
explanation: A suffix automaton (DAWG) is the minimal DFA accepting all substrings of a string. It has at most 2n-1 states and 3n-4 transitions and can be built in O(n) time.

text: What is the Burrows-Wheeler Transform (BWT) primarily used for?
options: Encrypting strings for security
Rearranging a string to group similar characters together, enabling better compression
Sorting strings in reverse order
Finding palindromes in strings
explanation: BWT rearranges the characters of a string so that similar characters cluster together, making the result highly compressible. It is the basis of bzip2 compression and is used in bioinformatics.

text: What key optimization technique does Ukkonen's suffix tree algorithm use to achieve linear time?
options: Hash tables for each node
Suffix links, implicit extensions, and the active point trick to avoid redundant traversal
Divide and conquer on the string
Binary search on suffix positions
explanation: Ukkonen's algorithm achieves O(n) by using suffix links to jump between insertion points, implicit extensions to avoid explicit leaf updates, and the active point trick to track the current position efficiently.

text: How can the number of distinct substrings of a string be computed using a suffix array and LCP array?
options: Count = n(n+1)/2
Count = n(n+1)/2 - sum of all LCP values
Count = sum of all LCP values
Count = n^2
explanation: The total number of distinct substrings is n(n+1)/2 (all possible substrings) minus the sum of LCP values (which count duplicate prefixes between consecutive sorted suffixes).

text: What is the worst-case time complexity of the Boyer-Moore algorithm?
options: O(n/m)
O(n + m)
O(n * m)
O(n)
explanation: The original Boyer-Moore algorithm has O(n * m) worst-case time when all characters in text and pattern are the same. The Galil rule or turbo-BM variant achieves O(n) worst case.

text: What is the LCP (Longest Common Prefix) array and how is it used with suffix arrays?
options: An array of string lengths
LCP[i] stores the length of the longest common prefix between the i-th and (i-1)-th sorted suffixes; it enables efficient pattern matching and string analysis
An array of character frequencies
An array mapping characters to positions
explanation: The LCP array stores the length of the longest common prefix between consecutive suffixes in sorted order. Combined with the suffix array, it enables O(m + log n) pattern matching and efficient computation of repeated substrings.

text: What is the time complexity of the Kasai algorithm for constructing the LCP array from a suffix array?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
O(n * m)
explanation: Kasai's algorithm computes the LCP array from the suffix array and inverse suffix array in O(n) time by exploiting the property that LCP values decrease by at most 1 between consecutive positions.

text: What is the palindromic tree (Eertree) data structure?
options: A binary tree of palindrome lengths
A data structure that stores all distinct palindromic substrings of a string in O(n) time and space
A trie of reversed strings
A balanced BST of palindromes
explanation: The palindromic tree (Eertree) represents all distinct palindromic substrings of a string using at most n+2 nodes. It can be built online in O(n) time and supports various palindrome queries.

text: What is the Big-O complexity of an algorithm with running time 3n + 5?
options: O(3n)
O(n)
explanation: Big-O ignores constant factors and lower-order terms. 3n + 5 simplifies to O(n) because the growth is linear.

text: Which grows faster: O(n^2) or O(2^n)?
options: O(n^2) grows faster
They grow at the same rate
O(2^n) grows faster
It depends on n
explanation: Exponential growth O(2^n) eventually dominates any polynomial growth O(n^2). For large n, 2^n is astronomically larger than n^2.

text: What is the time complexity of a loop that runs from 1 to n and increments by 2 each time?
options: O(n)
O(n/2), which is O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: A loop from 1 to n incrementing by 2 runs n/2 times. In Big-O notation, O(n/2) = O(n) since constant factors are ignored.

text: What does it mean to optimize an algorithm?
options: To make the code longer
To improve the algorithm's time or space efficiency
To add more features
To translate it to a faster programming language
explanation: Optimizing an algorithm means reducing its time complexity, space complexity, or both, to make it run faster or use less memory.

text: What is the time complexity of checking if a number n is even or odd?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: Checking if a number is even or odd requires a single modulo or bitwise AND operation, which is O(1) regardless of the number's magnitude.

text: Why is O(1) the most efficient time complexity?
options: Because it means the algorithm does nothing
Because the execution time does not increase with input size
Because it uses no memory
Because it always runs in exactly 1 millisecond
explanation: O(1) or constant time means the algorithm takes the same amount of time regardless of input size, making it the most efficient complexity class.

text: What is the space complexity of an algorithm that uses only a fixed number of variables?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: An algorithm using only a fixed (constant) number of variables regardless of input size has O(1) space complexity.

text: What does it mean when we say an algorithm runs in 'polynomial time'?
options: It always finishes in under a second
Its running time is bounded by n^k for some constant k
It uses polynomial equations
It can only process polynomials
explanation: An algorithm runs in polynomial time if its time complexity is O(n^k) for some constant k. Examples include O(n), O(n^2), and O(n^3). Polynomial-time algorithms are considered efficient.

text: If an algorithm has two independent steps with complexities O(n) and O(m), what is the overall complexity?
options: O(n * m)
O(n + m)
O(max(n, m))
explanation: When two independent steps are performed sequentially, their complexities are added: O(n) + O(m) = O(n + m).

text: Why do we use Big-O notation instead of measuring exact execution time?
options: Because exact time is always the same
Because Big-O describes how the algorithm scales with input size, independent of hardware and implementation
Because Big-O is more precise
Because exact time cannot be measured
explanation: Big-O notation abstracts away hardware-specific constants and focuses on how the algorithm's performance scales with input size, enabling meaningful comparisons across systems.

text: What is the relationship between the complexity classes P and NP?
options: P is a strict subset of NP and P ≠ NP is proven
P ⊆ NP (P is contained in NP), but whether P = NP is unknown
NP ⊂ P (NP is a strict subset of P)
P and NP are disjoint sets
explanation: Every problem in P is also in NP (since a polynomial-time algorithm is also a polynomial-time verifier). Whether P = NP or P ≠ NP is one of the most important open problems in computer science.

text: What is the time complexity of Strassen's matrix multiplication algorithm?
options: O(n^2 log n)
explanation: Strassen's algorithm multiplies two n×n matrices in O(n^(log2 7)) ≈ O(n^2.807) time by reducing 8 recursive multiplications to 7, improving over the O(n^3) naive method.

text: Which of the following is a well-known NP-complete problem?
options: Binary search
Sorting an array
The Boolean Satisfiability Problem (SAT)
Finding the minimum in an array
explanation: SAT (Boolean Satisfiability) was the first problem proven NP-complete by Cook's theorem. Sorting and searching are polynomial-time problems, not NP-complete.

text: What is the difference between time complexity and space complexity?
options: They always have the same value
Time complexity measures the number of operations as a function of input size; space complexity measures the memory used
Space complexity is always larger
Time complexity measures clock time in seconds
explanation: Time complexity counts the number of basic operations relative to input size. Space complexity measures the amount of additional memory used relative to input size. They are independent metrics.

text: What is the significance of the O(n log n) barrier for comparison-based sorting?
options: It can be beaten by any sorting algorithm
No comparison-based sorting algorithm can have a worst case better than Ω(n log n)
O(n log n) is the average case but O(n) is achievable in the worst case
Only merge sort achieves this bound
explanation: The Ω(n log n) lower bound is information-theoretic: with n! possible permutations and each comparison yielding 1 bit of information, at least log2(n!) = Θ(n log n) comparisons are needed.

text: How does loop unrolling improve performance at the hardware level?
options: By reducing the total number of operations
By reducing branch overhead and enabling better instruction pipelining and cache utilization
By converting the loop to recursion
By eliminating the need for variables
explanation: Loop unrolling reduces the frequency of branch instructions (loop condition checks), allowing the CPU pipeline to execute more useful work per cycle and improving instruction-level parallelism.

text: What is the time-space tradeoff in memoization?
options: Memoization uses more time to save space
Memoization uses extra space to store computed results, reducing time by avoiding redundant computation
Memoization has no tradeoff
Memoization reduces both time and space
explanation: Memoization trades space (storing computed results in a table) for time (avoiding redundant recursive computations). This often reduces exponential time to polynomial time at the cost of polynomial space.

text: What is the complexity of adding two n-digit numbers?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: Adding two n-digit numbers requires processing each digit once (with carry), taking O(n) time. This is a fundamental operation in arbitrary-precision arithmetic.

text: What is cache-oblivious algorithm design?
options: Algorithms that don't use any cache
Algorithms designed to efficiently use the memory hierarchy without knowing cache parameters
Algorithms that ignore memory constraints
Algorithms that clear the cache before running
explanation: Cache-oblivious algorithms achieve near-optimal cache performance without knowing cache size or line size. They work well on any level of the memory hierarchy through recursive divide-and-conquer patterns.

text: What is the difference between worst-case and average-case complexity?
options: They are always the same
Worst-case gives the maximum time over all inputs of size n; average-case gives the expected time over a distribution of inputs
Average-case is always worse than worst-case
Worst-case only considers the best input
explanation: Worst-case complexity is the maximum time for any input of size n, providing an upper-bound guarantee. Average-case complexity is the expected time assuming a probability distribution over inputs.

text: What is Cook's theorem and its significance?
options: It proves P = NP
It proves that the Boolean satisfiability problem (SAT) is NP-complete, establishing the first NP-complete problem
It proves that all NP problems are unsolvable
It provides a polynomial algorithm for SAT
explanation: Cook's theorem (1971) proved SAT is NP-complete by showing every NP problem can be reduced to SAT in polynomial time. This established the framework for proving other problems NP-complete.

text: What is pseudo-polynomial time and why is it misleading?
options: It is polynomial time disguised as exponential
An algorithm runs in time polynomial in the numeric value of the input but exponential in the input size (number of bits)
It is always faster than polynomial time
It means the algorithm is approximately polynomial
explanation: Pseudo-polynomial time means the runtime is polynomial in the value of the input (e.g., O(nW) for knapsack) but exponential in the input's bit-length (log W bits to represent W). Such problems are weakly NP-hard.

text: What is the complexity class co-NP?
options: Problems whose complements are in P
Problems whose complements are in NP; equivalently, problems with polynomial-time disproofs
Problems harder than NP
Problems that are not in NP
explanation: co-NP contains decision problems whose complement (negation) is in NP. A problem is in co-NP if 'no' instances have efficiently verifiable proofs. Whether NP = co-NP is unknown.

text: What fundamental consequence does the time hierarchy theorem have for the P vs NP question?
options: It proves P = NP
It proves that strictly more time allows solving strictly more problems, implying P ≠ EXPTIME
It shows all problems are equally hard
It proves NP = PSPACE
explanation: The time hierarchy theorem shows P ⊊ EXPTIME (there are problems solvable in exponential time but not polynomial time). However, it does not resolve whether P = NP directly.

text: What is the best known time complexity for matrix multiplication of two n×n matrices?
options: O(n^2.807) (Strassen)
O(n^2.371) approximately (current best)
explanation: The current best known algorithm for matrix multiplication achieves approximately O(n^2.371) (due to recent advances by Williams, Alman, and others). The ω = 2 lower bound has not been achieved.

text: What is a Fixed-Parameter Tractable (FPT) algorithm?
options: An algorithm with fixed running time
An algorithm with time O(f(k) * n^c) where k is a parameter, f is any computable function, and c is a constant independent of k
An algorithm that fixes errors in parameters
An algorithm with O(n) time for any parameters
explanation: FPT algorithms have time complexity O(f(k) * n^c) where the exponential blowup is confined to a parameter k (not the input size n). When k is small, these run efficiently even if n is large.

text: What is the role of polynomial-time reductions in proving NP-completeness?
options: They simplify the problem to make it solvable
They show that if problem A reduces to problem B in polynomial time and A is NP-hard, then B is also NP-hard
They reduce the time complexity of algorithms
They convert NP problems to P problems
explanation: A polynomial reduction from A to B transforms any instance of A into an instance of B in polynomial time. If A is NP-hard and reduces to B, then B is at least as hard, proving B is NP-hard.

text: What is the PSPACE complexity class?
options: Problems solvable in polynomial time
Problems solvable using polynomial space on a deterministic Turing machine
Problems that use no space at all
Problems solvable on parallel computers
explanation: PSPACE contains problems decidable using polynomial space. It includes both P and NP (since polynomial time implies polynomial space). Whether PSPACE = NP is unknown.

text: What is the approximation ratio and when is it used?
options: The ratio of input size to output size
For NP-hard optimization problems, the ratio of the algorithm's solution value to the optimal solution value, guaranteed to be within a bound
The ratio of time complexity to space complexity
The ratio of average case to worst case complexity
explanation: For NP-hard problems where exact solutions are impractical, approximation algorithms provide solutions within a guaranteed factor (approximation ratio) of optimal. E.g., a 2-approximation is at most twice the optimal.

text: What is the Exponential Time Hypothesis (ETH) and what does it imply?
options: All problems require exponential time
3-SAT cannot be solved in 2^(o(n)) time, implying many NP-hard problems have no sub-exponential algorithms
P = NP is true
Exponential algorithms are always impractical
explanation: ETH conjectures that 3-SAT has no 2^(o(n)) algorithm. This implies tight lower bounds for many NP-hard problems and is a stronger assumption than P ≠ NP.

text: What does an Abstract Data Type (ADT) define?
options: The memory layout of data in RAM
The programming language used to build a structure
The behavior and operations without specifying implementation
Only the implementation details of a data structure
explanation: An ADT defines the behavior (operations) of a data structure without specifying how it is implemented internally.

text: What does Big-O notation represent?
options: The upper bound on growth rate of an algorithm
The best-case time complexity
The exact running time in seconds
The average-case time complexity
explanation: Big-O notation describes the upper bound (worst-case) growth rate of an algorithm's time or space complexity.

text: Which of the following is O(1) time complexity?
options: Traversing a linked list
Binary search in a sorted array
Accessing an array element by index
Sorting an array using merge sort
explanation: Accessing an array element by index takes constant time O(1) because arrays support direct access via memory address calculation.

text: What is the base case in recursion?
options: The largest input size
The recursive step that reduces the problem
The first recursive call
The condition that stops the recursion
explanation: The base case is the condition that terminates recursion, preventing infinite recursive calls.

text: Which notation describes the lower bound of an algorithm's growth rate?
options: Big-Omega
Big-O
Big-Theta
Little-o
explanation: Big-Omega notation provides the lower bound on the growth rate, representing the best-case scenario.

text: What is the time complexity of a simple for loop that iterates n times?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
O(n²)
explanation: A single loop iterating n times performs n operations, giving O(n) time complexity.

text: What is space complexity?
options: The number of variables declared in a program
The amount of memory an algorithm uses relative to input size
The disk space required to store the source code
The number of lines of code in an algorithm
explanation: Space complexity measures how much additional memory an algorithm needs as the input size grows.

text: Which of the following uses iteration instead of recursion?
options: A while loop that repeats until a condition is false
A function that calls itself with a smaller input
A function with no base case
A divide-and-conquer approach that splits problems
explanation: Iteration uses loop constructs like while or for to repeat operations, unlike recursion which uses self-calling functions.

text: What does Big-Theta (Θ) notation represent?
options: The average of best and worst cases
Only the lower bound
Only the upper bound
Both upper and lower bounds (tight bound)
explanation: Big-Theta provides a tight bound, meaning the algorithm's growth rate is bounded both above and below by the given function.

text: What is pseudocode primarily used for?
options: Optimizing code execution speed
Compiling programs into machine code
Debugging runtime errors
Describing algorithms in a human-readable format
explanation: Pseudocode is a human-readable, language-independent way to describe the logic of an algorithm before actual implementation.

text: What is the time complexity of two nested loops, each iterating n times?
options: O(n log n)
O(n²)
O(2n)
O(n)
explanation: Two nested loops each running n times result in n × n = n² operations, giving O(n²) complexity.

text: Which recurrence relation represents the time complexity of binary search?
explanation: Binary search halves the input each step and does O(1) work per step, giving T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1), which solves to O(log n).

text: What problem can occur if a recursive function lacks a proper base case?
options: Compilation error
Stack overflow due to infinite recursion
The function runs in constant time
The function returns 0
explanation: Without a base case, the function calls itself indefinitely, consuming stack memory until a stack overflow occurs.

text: What is the amortized time complexity of inserting n elements into a dynamic array that doubles its size when full?
options: O(1) per insertion
O(n) per insertion
O(n²) total
O(log n) per insertion
explanation: Although occasional resizing costs O(n), spreading the cost over all insertions gives O(1) amortized time per insertion.

text: Which of the following growth rates is the fastest as n approaches infinity?
options: O(2ⁿ)
O(n log n)
O(n³)
O(n²)
explanation: Exponential growth O(2ⁿ) outpaces all polynomial growth rates like O(n²) and O(n³) as n approaches infinity.

text: What is tail recursion?
options: Recursion that uses a queue instead of a stack
Recursion with multiple base cases
Recursion where the recursive call is the last operation in the function
Recursion where the function calls itself at the beginning
explanation: In tail recursion, the recursive call is the last operation, allowing compilers to optimize it into iteration (tail-call optimization).

text: If f(n) = 3n² + 5n + 2, what is the Big-O complexity?
options: O(n² + n)
O(n²)
O(3n²)
O(n)
explanation: Big-O drops constants and lower-order terms, so 3n² + 5n + 2 simplifies to O(n²).

text: What is the space complexity of a recursive function that makes n recursive calls with no additional data structures?
options: O(n²)
O(log n)
O(n)
explanation: Each recursive call adds a frame to the call stack, so n calls use O(n) space on the stack.

text: According to the Master Theorem, what is the solution to T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n)?
options: O(n log n)
O(log n)
O(n²)
O(n)
explanation: This recurrence falls under Case 2 of the Master Theorem where a = b^k, yielding O(n log n).

text: Which statement correctly compares iteration and recursion?
options: Recursive solutions are always faster than iterative ones
Iteration cannot solve problems that recursion can
Recursion always uses less memory than iteration
Every recursive solution can be converted to an iterative one
explanation: Any recursive algorithm can be rewritten iteratively using an explicit stack, though the code may be more complex.

text: What is the time complexity of the recursive Fibonacci function without memoization?
options: O(2ⁿ)
O(n log n)
O(n)
O(n²)
explanation: The naive recursive Fibonacci has exponential O(2ⁿ) complexity because it recomputes overlapping subproblems.

text: Which of the following is true about little-o notation?
options: f(n) = o(g(n)) means f grows strictly slower than g
It represents a tight bound like Big-Theta
It represents the lower bound of an algorithm
f(n) = o(g(n)) means f grows at the same rate as g
explanation: Little-o notation means f(n) grows strictly slower than g(n); the ratio f(n)/g(n) approaches 0 as n approaches infinity.

text: What is the time complexity of computing the power x^n using the divide-and-conquer exponentiation method?
options: O(√n)
O(log n)
O(n log n)
O(n)
explanation: Fast exponentiation squares x and halves n at each step, resulting in O(log n) multiplications.

text: Which recurrence relation does the Tower of Hanoi problem follow?
explanation: Tower of Hanoi moves n-1 disks twice (to auxiliary and back) plus one move for the largest disk, giving T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 1.

text: If an algorithm has complexity O(n!) , for n=20, approximately how many operations are performed?
options: About 1 million
20 million
explanation: 20! equals approximately 2.43 × 10^18, demonstrating why factorial-time algorithms are impractical for even moderate inputs.

text: What does the substitution method involve when solving recurrences?
options: Converting Big-O to Big-Theta automatically
Replacing the algorithm with a faster one
Guessing the form of the solution and proving it by induction
Substituting iterative code for recursive code
explanation: The substitution method involves guessing the solution form and then using mathematical induction to verify correctness.

text: What is the asymptotic complexity of log(n!) ?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
O(n log n)
O(n²)
explanation: By Stirling's approximation, log(n!) ≈ n log n - n, so log(n!) is Θ(n log n).

text: In asymptotic analysis, which relationship is correct?
options: O(n) ⊂ O(log n)
O(n log n) ⊂ O(n²)
O(2ⁿ) ⊂ O(n³)
O(n²) ⊂ O(n)
explanation: O(n log n) is a subset of O(n²) because n log n grows slower than n² for large n.

text: What is the space complexity of merge sort?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
O(log n)
explanation: Merge sort requires O(n) auxiliary space for the temporary arrays used during merging.

text: Which case of the Master Theorem applies to T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n?
options: None of the cases apply
Case 1: work dominated by leaves, O(n²)
Case 3: work dominated by root, O(n)
Case 2: work evenly split, O(n log n)
explanation: Here a=4, b=2, log_b(a)=2, and f(n)=n is O(n^(2-ε)), so Case 1 applies giving O(n²).

text: What is the time complexity of accessing an element at a given index in an array?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
O(n²)
explanation: Arrays provide constant-time O(1) access because the memory address is computed directly from the base address and index.

text: Which data structure follows the Last In, First Out (LIFO) principle?
options: Queue
Stack
Linked List
Array
explanation: A stack follows LIFO, meaning the most recently added element is the first one to be removed.

text: Which data structure follows the First In, First Out (FIFO) principle?
options: Queue
Stack
Priority Queue
Deque
