explanation: The edit distance table is (m+1) × (n+1) to include base cases for empty string prefixes.

text: A suffix array for a string of length n stores the sorted order of all n suffixes and can be constructed in:
options: O(n^2 log n)
O(n log n) or O(n)
explanation: Efficient suffix array construction algorithms run in O(n log n) or even O(n) time.

text: Boyer-Moore's algorithm is generally faster in practice because it skips characters using:
options: The failure function
Bad character and good suffix heuristics
Rolling hash
Breadth-first search
explanation: Boyer-Moore uses bad character and good suffix rules to skip large portions of text, often achieving sublinear time.

text: The edit distance between 'kitten' and 'sitting' is:
explanation: Three operations: substitute k→s, substitute e→i, insert g at end. So the edit distance is 3.

text: A compressed trie (Patricia trie/radix tree) reduces space by:
options: Using hash tables instead of arrays
Merging chains of single-child nodes into one edge
Storing only leaf nodes
Using bit manipulation
explanation: A compressed trie merges edges along paths with single children, reducing the number of nodes significantly.

text: The LCS of two strings of lengths m and n can be computed in O(m * n) time and optimized to use space:
options: O(m * n)
O(min(m, n))
O(m + n)
explanation: LCS space can be optimized to O(min(m,n)) by keeping only two rows of the DP table at a time.

text: In Rabin-Karp, a rolling hash allows updating the hash of a sliding window in:
options: O(m)
O(log m)
O(n)
explanation: The rolling hash removes the contribution of the outgoing character and adds the incoming one in O(1) time.

text: Boyer-Moore compares the pattern to the text starting from which end of the pattern?
options: Left to right
Right to left
Middle outward
Random positions
explanation: Boyer-Moore matches the pattern from right to left, enabling larger shifts on mismatches.

text: Aho-Corasick algorithm extends KMP to match multiple patterns simultaneously and runs in time:
options: O(n * k) where k is number of patterns
O(n + m + z) where z is total matches
O(n * m)
explanation: Aho-Corasick processes the text in O(n + m + z) time where m is total pattern length and z is the number of matches.

text: A suffix tree for a string of length n can be built in O(n) time using:
options: KMP algorithm
Ukkonen's algorithm
Rabin-Karp
Boyer-Moore
explanation: Ukkonen's algorithm constructs a suffix tree in O(n) time using an online, incremental approach.

text: The Z-algorithm computes for each position i the length of the longest substring starting at i that matches a prefix of the string, in time:
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
O(n * m)
explanation: The Z-algorithm runs in O(n) time using a window technique to avoid redundant comparisons.

text: The LCP (Longest Common Prefix) array combined with a suffix array can simulate suffix tree queries. Building the LCP array from a suffix array takes:
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
O(n * log^2 n)
explanation: Kasai's algorithm computes the LCP array from the suffix array in O(n) time.

text: Manacher's algorithm finds all palindromic substrings (or the longest palindromic substring) in:
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
explanation: Manacher's algorithm exploits palindrome symmetry to find all palindromic substrings in O(n) time.

text: The suffix automaton (DAWG) of a string of length n has at most how many states?
options: n
explanation: A suffix automaton has at most 2n - 1 states, making it a compact representation of all substrings.

text: String hashing with double hashing (two different hash functions) is used to reduce:
options: Time complexity
Space usage
Hash collision probability
Pattern length
explanation: Double hashing uses two independent hash functions, making the probability of both colliding simultaneously very low.

text: The Burrows-Wheeler Transform (BWT) is useful for text compression because it groups:
options: Vowels together
Similar characters together, increasing runs
Characters alphabetically
Digits separately
explanation: BWT rearranges characters so that similar characters cluster together, creating longer runs for better compression.

text: The longest palindromic subsequence of a string of length n can be found in O(n^2) by reducing it to:
options: Edit distance problem
LCS of the string and its reverse
Suffix array construction
KMP matching
explanation: The longest palindromic subsequence equals the LCS of the string and its reverse, solvable in O(n²).

text: In the Boyer-Moore algorithm, the good suffix rule precomputes shifts based on:
options: Character frequencies in the text
Occurrences of the matched suffix elsewhere in the pattern
Hash values of substrings
The failure function
explanation: The good suffix rule finds where the matched suffix reappears in the pattern to determine the optimal shift.

text: A problem is in class P if it can be solved in:
options: Exponential time
Polynomial time by a deterministic Turing machine
Constant time
Logarithmic time only
explanation: Class P contains problems solvable in polynomial time (O(n^k) for some constant k) by a deterministic Turing machine.

text: A problem is in NP if a given solution can be verified in:
options: Exponential time
Polynomial time
Constant time
Non-deterministic time
explanation: NP problems have solutions that can be verified in polynomial time by a deterministic Turing machine.

text: The Master Theorem is used to solve recurrences of the form:
options: T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)
T(n) = f(n) + g(n)
explanation: The Master Theorem applies to recurrences T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n) where a ≥ 1 and b > 1.

text: Which of the following is a known NP-complete problem?
options: Sorting
Binary search
Satisfiability (SAT)
Finding minimum in an array
explanation: SAT (Boolean satisfiability) was the first problem proven NP-complete by Cook's theorem.

text: Amortized analysis gives the average time per operation over:
options: Random inputs
A worst-case sequence of operations
Only best-case inputs
A single operation
explanation: Amortized analysis computes the average cost per operation over a worst-case sequence of operations.

text: If P = NP, it would mean that every problem whose solution can be verified quickly can also be:
options: Solved in exponential time
Solved in polynomial time
Solved in constant time
Not solved at all
explanation: P = NP would mean every problem with polynomial-time verification also has a polynomial-time solution.

text: The recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n describes the time complexity of which algorithm?
options: Binary search
Merge sort
Linear search
Insertion sort
explanation: Merge sort splits the array into two halves (2T(n/2)) and merges in O(n), giving T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n.

text: A polynomial-time reduction from problem A to problem B means:
options: A is harder than B
B is at least as hard as A
A and B are identical
B can be solved in constant time
explanation: A reduction from A to B shows that solving B solves A, meaning B is at least as hard as A.

text: Big-O notation O(f(n)) provides an upper bound on the:
options: Best-case time
Average-case time
Growth rate of a function
Exact running time
explanation: Big-O notation describes an asymptotic upper bound on the growth rate of a function.

text: The recurrence T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) solves to:
options: O(n)
O(log n)
O(n log n)
explanation: This recurrence halves the problem size with constant work per level, resulting in O(log n) — like binary search.

text: Using the Master Theorem, what is the solution to T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n?
options: O(n log n)
O(n^2 log n)
O(n)
explanation: Here a=4, b=2, f(n)=n. log_b(a)=2, and n < n^2, so by Case 1: T(n) = O(n^2).

text: A problem is NP-hard if:
options: It is in NP
Every NP problem reduces to it in polynomial time
It can be solved in polynomial time
It has no solution
explanation: A problem is NP-hard if every problem in NP can be reduced to it in polynomial time; it need not be in NP itself.

text: A problem is NP-complete if and only if it is:
options: In P and NP-hard
In NP and NP-hard
In NP but not in P
Unsolvable
explanation: NP-complete problems are both in NP (verifiable in polynomial time) and NP-hard (as hard as any NP problem).

text: The aggregate method of amortized analysis works by:
options: Assigning credits to operations
Computing total cost of n operations and dividing by n
Using a potential function
Counting recursive calls
explanation: The aggregate method computes the total cost of a sequence of n operations and divides by n for the amortized cost.

text: Using the Master Theorem, T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n log n falls into which case?
options: Case 1
Case 2 (with k=1)
Case 3
Master Theorem does not apply directly
explanation: f(n) = n log n vs n^(log_2 2) = n. Since n log n / n = log n which is not polynomial, the basic Master Theorem does not directly apply.

text: The Clique problem can be reduced to the Vertex Cover problem, proving Vertex Cover is:
options: In P
NP-complete
Unsolvable
O(n) solvable
explanation: Since Clique is NP-complete and reduces to Vertex Cover (which is in NP), Vertex Cover is also NP-complete.

text: In the accounting (banker's) method of amortized analysis, some operations are charged:
options: Their exact cost
More than their actual cost to save credit for expensive operations
Nothing at all
Random amounts
explanation: The accounting method overcharges cheap operations to bank credit, which pays for occasional expensive operations.

text: The recurrence T(n) = T(n-1) + n solves to:
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
explanation: Expanding: T(n) = n + (n-1) + ... + 1 = n(n+1)/2 = O(n²).

text: Which of the following is NOT known to be NP-complete?
options: Hamiltonian cycle
Graph coloring with 3 colors
Finding shortest path in a graph
Subset sum
explanation: Shortest path (e.g., Dijkstra's) is solvable in polynomial time, so it is in P, not NP-complete.

text: The potential method of amortized analysis defines the amortized cost as:
options: Actual cost - potential change
Actual cost + change in potential function
Potential function only
Actual cost / n
explanation: Amortized cost = actual cost + (Φ_after - Φ_before), where Φ is the potential function.

text: Cook's theorem (Cook-Levin theorem) states that which problem is NP-complete?
options: Traveling Salesman
Graph Coloring
Boolean Satisfiability (SAT)
Knapsack
explanation: Cook-Levin theorem proved SAT is NP-complete, the first problem shown to be NP-complete.

text: To prove a new problem X is NP-complete, you must show X is in NP and reduce:
options: X to a known NP-complete problem
A known NP-complete problem to X
X to P
P to X
explanation: You reduce a known NP-complete problem to X in polynomial time, showing X is at least as hard.

text: The Master Theorem Case 2 states that if f(n) = Θ(n^(log_b a)), then T(n) =:
options: Θ(n^(log_b a))
Θ(n^(log_b a) * log n)
Θ(f(n))
Θ(n * log n)
explanation: In Case 2, when f(n) matches n^(log_b a), an extra log factor appears: T(n) = Θ(n^(log_b a) · log n).

text: The amortized cost of dynamic array doubling (push_back) using the aggregate method is:
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: Total cost of n insertions with doubling is O(n), so amortized cost per insertion is O(n)/n = O(1).

text: 3-SAT is NP-complete. Which of the following is in P?
options: 3-SAT
2-SAT
Hamiltonian Path
Subset Sum
explanation: 2-SAT can be solved in polynomial time using implication graphs and SCC detection, unlike 3-SAT.

text: The Akra-Bazzi method generalizes the Master Theorem to handle recurrences where:
options: Subproblems have equal sizes
Subproblems have unequal sizes
There are no subproblems
Only linear recurrences
explanation: Akra-Bazzi extends the Master Theorem to recurrences with subproblems of different sizes, like T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + n.

text: The complexity class co-NP contains problems whose complements are in NP. Which is true about P?
options: P is a subset of co-NP only
P is a subset of NP only
P is a subset of both NP and co-NP
P and co-NP are disjoint
explanation: P ⊆ NP ∩ co-NP because any polynomial-time algorithm can both accept and reject, handling complements.

text: In the potential method, for a sequence of operations to have valid amortized analysis, the potential function must satisfy:
options: Φ_final < Φ_initial
Φ_final ≥ Φ_initial (potential never drops below initial)
Φ is always zero
Φ is always negative
explanation: The potential function must satisfy Φ_final ≥ Φ_initial so the total amortized cost is an upper bound on total actual cost.

text: The recurrence T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 1 solves to:
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
explanation: Each level doubles the subproblems, giving T(n) = 2^n + 2^(n-1) + ... = O(2^n).

text: A problem is in PSPACE if it can be solved using polynomial space. What is the relationship between NP and PSPACE?
options: NP = PSPACE
NP ⊆ PSPACE
PSPACE ⊆ NP
They are disjoint
explanation: NP ⊆ PSPACE because any NP problem can be solved by trying all certificates using polynomial space.

text: What is a primitive data type?
options: A basic data type provided by the language
A data type defined by the user
A data type for storing collections
A data type used only in databases
explanation: A primitive data type is a basic data type provided directly by the programming language, such as int, float, char, and boolean.

text: What does FIFO stand for?
options: Fast Input Fast Output
First In First Out
Final In Final Out
First Index First Order
explanation: FIFO stands for First In First Out, which is the ordering principle used by queues.

text: What is O(n) complexity called?
options: Logarithmic
Constant
Linear
Quadratic
explanation: O(n) is called linear time complexity because the execution time grows proportionally with the input size.

text: What is the purpose of an algorithm's termination condition?
options: To ensure the algorithm runs infinitely
To reduce the algorithm's memory usage
To increase the algorithm's speed
To guarantee the algorithm eventually stops
explanation: A termination condition ensures that an algorithm will eventually stop after a finite number of steps, preventing infinite loops.

text: Which of the following best describes a recursive algorithm?
options: An algorithm that calls itself directly or indirectly
An algorithm that uses only loops
An algorithm that runs in constant time
An algorithm that sorts data in place
explanation: A recursive algorithm is one that solves a problem by calling itself with smaller subproblems until reaching a base case.

text: What is O(log n) complexity called?
options: Exponential
Logarithmic
Linear
Polynomial
explanation: O(log n) is called logarithmic time complexity because the number of operations grows as the logarithm of the input size.

text: What does LIFO stand for?
options: Last Index First Order
Least In First Out
Last In First Out
Linear Input Final Output
explanation: LIFO stands for Last In First Out, which is the ordering principle used by stacks.

text: What is the base case in recursion?
options: The most complex case that takes longest
The case that triggers an infinite loop
The first recursive call made by the function
The condition that stops recursive calls
explanation: The base case is the condition in a recursive function that stops further recursive calls and returns a direct result.

text: Which of the following is O(n^2) complexity called?
options: Quadratic
Cubic
Logarithmic
Exponential
explanation: O(n^2) is called quadratic time complexity because the number of operations grows as the square of the input size.

text: What is a flowchart used for in algorithm design?
options: To compile source code into executables
To visually represent the steps of an algorithm
To measure the runtime of a program
To allocate memory for data structures
explanation: A flowchart is a diagrammatic representation that uses shapes and arrows to visually illustrate the steps and flow of an algorithm.

text: What is the time complexity of the recurrence T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
explanation: By the Master Theorem case 1: log_2(4) = 2, so n^(log_b(a)) = n^2 dominates f(n) = n, giving T(n) = Θ(n^2).

text: What is the difference between a greedy algorithm and a dynamic programming approach?
options: Greedy always gives optimal solutions unlike dynamic programming
Dynamic programming is always faster than greedy algorithms
Greedy uses more memory than dynamic programming
Dynamic programming considers all subproblems while greedy makes locally optimal choices
explanation: Dynamic programming solves all subproblems and combines their solutions, while a greedy algorithm makes the locally optimal choice at each step without reconsidering.

text: What is the space complexity of a recursive function with depth d and constant space per call?
options: O(d)
explanation: Each recursive call adds a constant-sized frame to the call stack, so the total space used is proportional to the recursion depth d.

text: Which technique converts a recursive algorithm to an iterative one?
options: Memoization with hash tables
Using an explicit stack data structure
Applying the Master Theorem directly
Replacing all variables with constants
explanation: An explicit stack can simulate the call stack used by recursion, allowing any recursive algorithm to be converted to an iterative one.

text: What is tail recursion?
options: Recursion that uses two base cases
Recursion that always runs in O(1) space
Recursion where the recursive call is the last operation
Recursion that processes the tail of an array
explanation: Tail recursion occurs when the recursive call is the last operation in the function, allowing compilers to optimize it into iteration.

text: If f(n) = 3n^2 + 5n + 7, what is f(n) in Big-O notation?
options: O(n)
explanation: In Big-O notation, we keep only the highest-order term and drop constants, so 3n^2 + 5n + 7 = O(n^2).

text: What is the time complexity of T(n) = T(n/2) + n?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
O(n log n)
explanation: By the Master Theorem case 3: log_2(1) = 0, and f(n) = n = Ω(n^(0+ε)), so T(n) = Θ(n).

text: What is the difference between best case and average case complexity?
options: Best case is always O(1) while average case is always O(n)
Best case is the minimum operations needed while average case considers all inputs
Best case and average case are always identical
Average case is always worse than worst case
explanation: Best case measures the minimum number of operations for the most favorable input, while average case considers the expected performance over all possible inputs.

text: What is the time complexity of three nested loops each running n times?
options: O(n log n)
O(3n)
explanation: Three nested loops each running n times result in n × n × n = n^3 iterations, giving O(n^3).

text: What is the purpose of the recurrence tree method?
options: To sort elements using a tree structure
To convert iterative code into recursive code
To build a binary search tree from recurrences
To visualize the cost at each level of recursion
explanation: The recurrence tree method visualizes the work done at each level of recursion to sum up total cost and determine the overall complexity.

text: What is the Akra-Bazzi method used for?
options: Solving divide-and-conquer recurrences more general than the Master Theorem
Solving linear recurrences with constant coefficients
Computing the exact running time of any algorithm
Optimizing tail-recursive functions automatically
explanation: The Akra-Bazzi method generalizes the Master Theorem to handle recurrences where subproblems may have unequal sizes.

text: What is the potential method in amortized analysis?
options: Assigning credits to individual operations for future use
Using a potential function to account for stored work in the data structure
Dividing total cost equally among all operations
Measuring the worst-case cost of every single operation
explanation: The potential method defines a potential function over the data structure's state, where the amortized cost of an operation equals its actual cost plus the change in potential.

text: What is the time complexity of the recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n/log n?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
O(n log log n)
explanation: This recurrence falls in the gap between Master Theorem cases 2 and 3. Using the Akra-Bazzi method or direct analysis, T(n) = O(n log log n).

text: In the accounting method of amortized analysis, what are credits used for?
options: Tracking the number of recursive calls at each depth
Measuring the maximum memory usage of each operation
Computing the average time across all different algorithms
Paying for future expensive operations with overcharges from cheap ones
explanation: In the accounting method, cheap operations are overcharged to build up credits that pay for occasional expensive operations, ensuring a low amortized cost.

text: What is the significance of the decision tree model in lower bound proofs?
options: It proves that certain problems require at least a minimum number of comparisons
It shows the maximum possible speedup from parallelism
It demonstrates that all algorithms have the same complexity
It provides an upper bound on memory usage
explanation: The decision tree model represents all possible comparison sequences, and its height gives a lower bound on the number of comparisons needed in the worst case.

text: What does it mean for a problem to be in the complexity class P?
options: It can be solved in exponential time on a deterministic machine
It can be solved in polynomial time on a deterministic Turing machine
It can only be solved in polynomial time on a nondeterministic machine
It can be verified but not solved in polynomial time
explanation: A problem is in class P if there exists a deterministic algorithm that solves it in time polynomial in the size of the input.

text: What is the relationship between little-o and Big-O notation?
options: Little-o is identical to Big-O in all cases
f(n) = o(g(n)) means f(n) grows at the same rate as g(n)
f(n) = o(g(n)) means f(n) grows strictly slower than g(n)
Little-o provides a lower bound while Big-O provides an upper bound
explanation: Little-o notation f(n) = o(g(n)) means f(n) grows strictly slower than g(n), i.e., lim f(n)/g(n) = 0, which is a stricter condition than Big-O.

text: What is the aggregate method of amortized analysis?
options: Analyzing each operation independently and taking the maximum
Using a potential function over the data structure state
Assigning different costs to different types of operations
Computing total cost for n operations and dividing by n
explanation: The aggregate method computes the total cost of a sequence of n operations and divides by n to get the amortized cost per operation.

text: What is the time complexity of computing the nth Fibonacci number using naive recursion?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
explanation: Naive recursive Fibonacci has exponential time complexity O(2^n) because it recomputes the same subproblems exponentially many times.

text: What is the Master Theorem's regularity condition?
options: The subproblems must all be of equal size
In case 3, af(n/b) <= cf(n) for some c < 1 and large n
The function f(n) must be strictly increasing
The number of subproblems must be a power of two
explanation: The regularity condition in case 3 of the Master Theorem requires af(n/b) <= cf(n) for some constant c < 1 and all sufficiently large n.

text: What is a singly linked list?
options: A list where each node has two data fields
A list stored in contiguous memory locations
A list where each node points to the next node
A list that can only store integer values
explanation: A singly linked list is a data structure where each node contains data and a pointer to the next node in the sequence.

text: What operation does 'peek' perform on a stack?
options: Removes and returns the top element
Checks if the stack is empty or not
Adds an element to the top of the stack
Returns the top element without removing it
explanation: The peek operation returns the top element of the stack without removing it, allowing inspection without modification.

text: What is the time complexity of inserting at the beginning of a singly linked list?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: Inserting at the beginning of a singly linked list takes O(1) time because it only requires updating the head pointer.

text: Which data structure is used to implement undo functionality?
options: Queue
Stack
Array
Linked list
explanation: A stack is used for undo operations because the most recent action (last in) should be undone first (first out), following LIFO order.

text: What is a circular queue?
options: A queue implemented using a circular linked list only
A queue that sorts elements in circular order
A queue where the last position connects back to the first
A queue that can only store a fixed number of elements
explanation: A circular queue treats the underlying array as circular, so the last position wraps around to the first, efficiently utilizing space.

text: What is the time complexity of the dequeue operation?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
explanation: The dequeue operation removes the front element in O(1) time using a front pointer or index.

text: What is the main advantage of a linked list over an array?
options: Dynamic size without pre-allocation
Faster access to elements by index
Less memory usage per element
Better cache performance overall
explanation: Linked lists can grow and shrink dynamically without needing to pre-allocate a fixed size, unlike arrays.

text: What does the 'enqueue' operation do?
options: Removes an element from the front
Adds an element to the rear of the queue
Returns the front element without removing
Checks if the queue is full or not
explanation: The enqueue operation inserts an element at the rear of the queue, following the FIFO principle.

text: What is a doubly linked list?
options: A list with two head pointers
A list where each node stores two data values
A list where each node has next and previous pointers
A list that can be traversed only forward
explanation: A doubly linked list has nodes with pointers to both the next and previous nodes, enabling traversal in both directions.

text: What is the time complexity of accessing the kth element in a singly linked list?
options: O(log k)
O(k)
explanation: Accessing the kth element requires traversing k nodes from the head, giving O(k) time complexity.

text: How can you detect a cycle in a linked list efficiently?
options: By using Floyd's cycle detection with slow and fast pointers
By using two nested loops to compare all node pairs
By sorting the linked list and checking for duplicates
By converting the linked list to an array first
explanation: Floyd's cycle detection algorithm uses two pointers moving at different speeds; if they meet, a cycle exists, achieving O(n) time and O(1) space.

text: What is the time complexity of deleting a node from the middle of a doubly linked list given a pointer to it?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: With a direct pointer to the node, deletion in a doubly linked list takes O(1) because both the previous and next pointers are accessible.

text: How is a deque different from a regular queue?
options: A deque is always implemented using a linked list
A deque can store only primitive data types
A deque allows insertion and deletion at both ends
A deque has O(log n) insertion complexity
explanation: A deque (double-ended queue) supports insertion and deletion at both the front and rear, unlike a regular queue which only allows enqueue at rear and dequeue at front.

text: What is the advantage of a circular linked list over a singly linked list?
options: It uses less memory per node than singly linked lists
It supports random access to any element in O(1)
It automatically sorts elements upon insertion
It allows traversal from any node back to itself
explanation: In a circular linked list, the last node points back to the first, allowing continuous traversal from any starting node back to itself.

text: How can two stacks be used to implement a queue?
options: Push to stack1 for enqueue; move all to stack2 for dequeue when stack2 is empty
Use both stacks simultaneously for every operation
Push to both stacks at the same time for every insert
Use stack1 for even elements and stack2 for odd elements
explanation: Enqueue pushes to stack1; dequeue pops from stack2, transferring all elements from stack1 to stack2 when stack2 is empty, giving amortized O(1).

text: What is the worst-case time complexity of resizing a dynamic array?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: When a dynamic array is full and needs resizing, all n elements must be copied to a new larger array, taking O(n) time.

text: How do you find the middle element of a linked list in one pass?
options: Count all nodes first then traverse to the middle
Reverse the list and pick the first element
Use two pointers where one moves twice as fast as the other
Use binary search on the linked list nodes
explanation: Using slow and fast pointers, where fast moves two steps and slow moves one step, slow will be at the middle when fast reaches the end.

text: What is a priority queue?
options: A queue where elements are served in insertion order
A queue that reverses the order of all elements
A queue that stores only numeric priority values
A queue where each element has a priority and higher priority elements are served first
explanation: A priority queue serves elements based on their priority rather than insertion order, with higher priority elements dequeued first.

text: What is the time complexity of reversing a singly linked list iteratively?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
explanation: Reversing a singly linked list iteratively requires traversing all n nodes once while changing pointers, giving O(n) time.

text: What problem does the circular queue solve compared to a linear queue using an array?
options: It eliminates the need for a front pointer entirely
It prevents wasted space from dequeued positions at the front
It allows the queue to store unlimited elements
It reduces the time complexity of all operations
explanation: A circular queue reuses space freed by dequeued elements at the front by wrapping around, preventing the false overflow problem of linear queues.

text: What is the amortized time complexity of push and pop operations on a stack implemented with a dynamic array?
options: O(n) for push and O(1) for pop
O(log n) for both push and pop
O(1) for both push and pop
O(n) for both push and pop
explanation: Using a dynamic array that doubles in size, push has O(1) amortized cost via aggregate analysis, and pop is always O(1).

text: What is the XOR linked list?
options: A linked list where node data is XORed for encryption
A linked list where nodes are connected using bitwise OR
A linked list that uses XOR to detect and correct errors
A doubly linked list that stores XOR of next and previous addresses in one pointer
explanation: An XOR linked list stores the XOR of the previous and next node addresses in a single pointer field, reducing memory to that of a singly linked list while enabling bidirectional traversal.

text: What is the time complexity of the k-stacks in one array problem using an auxiliary array approach?
options: O(1) for both push and pop operations
O(n) for push and O(n) for pop
O(k) for push and O(1) for pop
O(log k) for push and O(log k) for pop
explanation: Using auxiliary arrays to track tops and free slots, k stacks can be implemented in a single array with O(1) push and pop for any stack.

text: What is the maximum number of elements in a circular queue of size n?
options: n
2n
explanation: In a standard circular queue implementation, one slot is kept empty to distinguish between full and empty states, so the maximum capacity is n - 1.

text: How does a lock-free concurrent queue typically achieve thread safety?
options: By using global mutex locks on all operations
By copying the entire queue for each thread
By using compare-and-swap (CAS) atomic operations
By restricting access to a single thread at a time
explanation: Lock-free concurrent queues use atomic compare-and-swap (CAS) operations to update pointers without locks, avoiding blocking and enabling concurrent access.

text: What is the space overhead of a skip list compared to a sorted linked list?
options: O(1) additional space
O(n log n) additional space
O(n^2) additional space
O(n) expected additional space
explanation: A skip list uses O(n) expected additional space for the extra pointers across all levels, as each level has roughly half the elements of the level below.

text: What is the worst-case time for dequeue when implementing a queue with two stacks?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: In the worst case, all n elements must be transferred from stack1 to stack2 before dequeue, taking O(n) time, though the amortized cost is O(1).

text: What is an unrolled linked list?
options: A linked list where nodes are flattened into a single array
A linked list where each node stores an array of elements instead of one
A linked list that unrolls recursive calls into iteration
A linked list that removes all duplicate elements automatically
explanation: An unrolled linked list stores multiple elements per node in a small array, improving cache performance and reducing pointer overhead compared to a standard linked list.

text: What is the sentinel node technique in linked list implementations?
options: A node that stores the count of all elements in the list
A node that monitors the list for memory leaks
A dummy node used to simplify boundary conditions at the head or tail
A node that encrypts the data stored in the linked list
explanation: A sentinel node is a dummy node placed at the boundaries of a linked list to eliminate special-case checks for empty lists or head/tail operations.

text: What is the cache performance advantage of arrays over linked lists?
options: Arrays use less total memory than linked lists
Arrays automatically prefetch data from the disk
Arrays have faster pointer dereferencing than linked lists
Arrays store elements contiguously enabling spatial locality
explanation: Arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, exploiting spatial locality in CPU caches, while linked list nodes may be scattered in memory causing cache misses.

text: What is the maximum number of children a node can have in a binary tree?
options: Two
Three
One
Unlimited
explanation: In a binary tree, each node can have at most two children, referred to as the left child and the right child.

text: What is the root of a tree?
options: The node with the most children
The topmost node with no parent
The node at the deepest level
The node with the smallest value
explanation: The root is the topmost node in a tree that has no parent and from which all other nodes descend.

text: What is a leaf node in a tree?
options: A node that has exactly one child
A node that is the root of a subtree
A node that has no children
A node that stores the largest value
explanation: A leaf node is a node that has no children, meaning it is at the end of a branch in the tree.

text: What is a complete binary tree?
options: A tree where every node has exactly two children
A tree that has exactly n nodes at level n
A tree where all leaves are at different levels
A tree where all levels are fully filled except possibly the last
explanation: A complete binary tree has all levels fully filled except possibly the last level, which is filled from left to right.

text: What is the degree of a node in a tree?
options: The number of children the node has
The height of the subtree rooted at the node
The depth of the node from the root
The total number of nodes in the tree
explanation: The degree of a node is the number of children it has in the tree.

text: What is a full binary tree?
options: A tree where every level is completely filled
A tree where every node has zero or two children
A tree where all nodes have exactly one child
A tree where the depth equals the number of nodes
explanation: A full binary tree is one in which every node has either zero children (leaf) or exactly two children.

text: What is a heap data structure?
options: A sorted array of unique elements
A balanced binary search tree
A complete binary tree satisfying the heap property
A graph with no cycles at all
explanation: A heap is a complete binary tree where each parent node satisfies the heap property (min-heap or max-heap) with respect to its children.

text: What is the height of a tree with a single node?
explanation: A tree with a single node (root only) has a height of 0, as height is defined as the number of edges on the longest path from root to a leaf.

text: What is the difference between a tree and a graph?
options: A graph can have cycles but a tree cannot
A tree can have cycles but a graph cannot
Trees can only store numbers while graphs store strings
There is no difference between a tree and a graph
explanation: A tree is a connected acyclic graph, meaning it has no cycles, while a general graph may contain cycles.

text: How many edges does a tree with n nodes have?
options: n
2n
explanation: A tree with n nodes always has exactly n - 1 edges, since each non-root node has exactly one parent.

text: What is the maximum number of nodes at level k in a binary tree?
options: k
2k
explanation: At level k of a binary tree, the maximum number of nodes is 2^k, since each node can have at most two children.

text: What is the maximum number of nodes in a binary tree of height h?
options: 2h
explanation: A binary tree of height h can have at most 2^(h+1) - 1 nodes when every level is completely filled (perfect binary tree).

text: How is a binary tree different from a binary search tree?
options: A binary tree can have at most two children; a BST also enforces an ordering property
A BST can have more than two children per node
A binary tree stores only integers while a BST stores strings
There is no difference between them at all
explanation: A BST is a binary tree with the additional constraint that left child values are less than the parent and right child values are greater.

text: What is the time complexity of insertion in a min-heap?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
O(n log n)
explanation: Insertion in a min-heap places the element at the end and bubbles it up, requiring at most O(log n) comparisons along the height of the tree.

text: What is a trie primarily used for?
options: Implementing a balanced binary search tree
Sorting integers in linear time
Storing and searching strings efficiently by prefix
Representing weighted graph adjacency lists
explanation: A trie (prefix tree) stores strings character by character, enabling efficient prefix-based searching and retrieval in O(m) time where m is the string length.

text: In a max-heap, where is the largest element located?
options: At a random position in the heap
At the deepest leaf node of the heap
At the last position of the array
At the root of the heap
explanation: In a max-heap, the heap property ensures that every parent is greater than or equal to its children, so the largest element is always at the root.

text: What is the difference between a min-heap and a max-heap?
options: Min-heap has the smallest element at root while max-heap has the largest
Min-heap is always balanced but max-heap is not
Max-heap can only store positive numbers
Min-heap uses more memory than max-heap
explanation: In a min-heap, the parent is less than or equal to its children (smallest at root), while in a max-heap, the parent is greater than or equal to its children (largest at root).

text: What is the time complexity of building a heap from an unsorted array?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: Building a heap using the bottom-up heapify approach takes O(n) time, as proven by summing the work at each level.

text: What is an adjacency matrix?
options: A list of all edges stored as pairs of vertices
A tree representation of a graph's connected components
A 2D matrix where entry (i,j) indicates an edge between vertices i and j
A hash table mapping vertices to their edge weights
explanation: An adjacency matrix is a V×V 2D array where the entry at row i, column j indicates whether an edge exists between vertices i and j.

text: What is the space complexity of an adjacency matrix for a graph with V vertices?
options: O(V)
O(V + E)
O(E)
explanation: An adjacency matrix requires V×V entries regardless of the number of edges, giving O(V^2) space complexity.

text: What is a B-tree primarily designed for?
options: Efficient disk-based storage and retrieval with minimal I/O
In-memory sorting of small datasets
Implementing hash tables with chaining
Performing graph traversals on sparse graphs
explanation: B-trees are self-balancing trees designed for systems that read and write large blocks of data, minimizing disk I/O by having high branching factor.

text: What is the minimum number of keys in a non-root internal node of a B-tree of order m?
options: m
explanation: In a B-tree of order m, every non-root internal node must have at least ⌈m/2⌉ children, and therefore at least ⌈m/2⌉ - 1 keys.

text: What is a Fibonacci heap's advantage over a binary heap?
options: It always maintains a perfectly balanced tree structure
It uses less memory than a binary heap
It has O(1) amortized insert and decrease-key operations
It supports O(1) worst-case extract-min operation
explanation: A Fibonacci heap supports O(1) amortized time for insert, find-min, decrease-key, and merge, making it ideal for algorithms like Dijkstra's.

text: What is the amortized time complexity of delete-min in a Fibonacci heap?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
O(log n)
explanation: Delete-min in a Fibonacci heap takes O(log n) amortized time due to the consolidation step that merges trees of the same degree.

text: In a red-black tree, what is the maximum ratio of the longest path to the shortest path from root to any leaf?
options: log n : 1
explanation: In a red-black tree, the longest path (alternating red and black) is at most twice the shortest path (all black), maintaining a 2:1 ratio.

text: What is a treap?
options: A tree that uses trapezoidal decomposition for range queries
A binary search tree where each node also has a random priority maintaining heap property
A trie that stores elements in heap order by frequency
A tree that combines three different balancing strategies
explanation: A treap combines a BST (by keys) and a heap (by random priorities), providing expected O(log n) operations due to the random structure.

text: What is the space complexity of a trie storing n strings of maximum length m over an alphabet of size k?
options: O(n * m)
O(n * k)
O(n * m * k)
O(n + m)
explanation: In the worst case, a trie can have up to n*m nodes, and each node stores k pointers (one per alphabet character), giving O(n * m * k) space.

text: What is a van Emde Boas tree used for?
options: Sorting strings in lexicographic order in linear time
Implementing hash tables with perfect hashing
Building minimum spanning trees of dense graphs
Supporting predecessor, successor, insert, and delete in O(log log u) time
explanation: A van Emde Boas tree supports operations like insert, delete, predecessor, and successor in O(log log u) time where u is the universe size.

text: What is the difference between a B-tree and a B+ tree?
options: B+ tree stores data only in leaves and links all leaves for sequential access
B+ tree stores data in all nodes while B-tree stores only in leaves
B-tree is always taller than a B+ tree with the same data
B-tree supports range queries but B+ tree does not
explanation: In a B+ tree, all data records are stored in leaf nodes which are linked together, while internal nodes only store keys for navigation, enabling efficient range queries.

text: What is the height of a red-black tree with n internal nodes?
options: Exactly log n
At most 2 log(n + 1)
Exactly n - 1
At most n / 2
explanation: A red-black tree with n internal nodes has a height of at most 2 log(n + 1), ensuring O(log n) operations in the worst case.

text: What is linear search?
options: A search that divides the array in half each step
A search that uses hashing to find elements
A search that checks each element sequentially
A search that requires the array to be sorted
explanation: Linear search checks each element in the array one by one from the beginning until the target is found or the end is reached.

text: What is the best-case time complexity of linear search?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: The best case occurs when the target element is at the first position, requiring only one comparison, giving O(1).

text: What is the prerequisite for binary search?
options: The array must be sorted
The array must contain unique elements
The array must have an even number of elements
The array must contain only positive numbers
explanation: Binary search requires the array to be sorted so it can eliminate half of the remaining elements at each step.

text: What is the worst-case time complexity of binary search?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: Binary search halves the search space in each step, so the worst case requires log n comparisons.

text: In binary search, what happens when the target is less than the middle element?
options: The search continues in the right half
The search restarts from the beginning
The search continues in the left half
The search terminates immediately
explanation: When the target is less than the middle element in a sorted array, it must be in the left half, so the search continues there.

text: What does linear search return if the element is not found?
options: The last index of the array
The size of the array
The index of the closest element
A special value indicating not found such as -1
explanation: Linear search typically returns -1 or a similar sentinel value to indicate that the target element was not found in the array.

text: Which searching algorithm works on unsorted arrays?
options: Linear search
Interpolation search
Binary search
Fibonacci search
explanation: Linear search does not require any ordering of elements and works on both sorted and unsorted arrays.

text: What is the space complexity of iterative binary search?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: Iterative binary search uses only a constant number of variables (low, high, mid), giving O(1) space complexity.

text: What is the average-case time complexity of linear search?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
O(n log n)
explanation: On average, linear search examines about half the elements before finding the target, giving O(n/2) which simplifies to O(n).

text: How does binary search determine the middle index?
options: mid = low + high
mid = low * high
mid = high - low
mid = (low + high) / 2
explanation: Binary search computes the middle index as mid = (low + high) / 2, or equivalently low + (high - low) / 2 to avoid overflow.

text: What is interpolation search's average-case complexity on uniformly distributed data?
options: O(log log n)
O(log n)
O(n)
O(sqrt(n))
explanation: On uniformly distributed data, interpolation search achieves O(log log n) average time by estimating the target's position proportionally.

text: What is jump search's optimal block size for an array of n elements?
options: sqrt(n)
log n
explanation: The optimal block size for jump search is sqrt(n), which minimizes the total number of comparisons to O(sqrt(n)).

text: How does ternary search differ from binary search?
options: Ternary search has O(1) time complexity
Ternary search only works on unsorted arrays
Ternary search divides the array into three parts instead of two
Ternary search uses hashing to locate elements
explanation: Ternary search divides the search space into three equal parts using two midpoints, comparing the target with both to eliminate two-thirds.

text: Why is binary search preferred over ternary search despite both being O(log n)?
options: Binary search uses less memory overall
Binary search works on unsorted arrays as well
Ternary search cannot handle duplicate elements
Binary search makes fewer comparisons per step on average
explanation: Binary search makes one comparison per step while ternary search makes two, and log_2(n) < 2*log_3(n), making binary search more efficient in practice.

text: What is the time complexity of searching in a balanced BST?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
O(n log n)
explanation: In a balanced BST, the height is O(log n), so searching requires at most O(log n) comparisons following a root-to-leaf path.

text: What is exponential search primarily useful for?
options: Searching in completely unsorted arrays
Searching in sorted unbounded or infinite arrays
Finding the kth smallest element in an array
Searching in hash tables with collision chains
explanation: Exponential search is useful for unbounded or infinite sorted arrays as it first finds a range by doubling, then binary searches within that range.

text: What is the time complexity of jump search?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
O(sqrt(n))
O(n log n)
explanation: Jump search jumps in blocks of sqrt(n) and then performs linear search within the block, giving O(sqrt(n)) total comparisons.

text: How does sentinel linear search improve over standard linear search?
options: It reduces the time complexity from O(n) to O(log n)
It processes two elements per iteration instead of one
It uses binary search as a fallback when elements are sorted
It eliminates the end-of-array boundary check in each iteration
explanation: Sentinel linear search places the target at the end of the array, eliminating the need to check for array bounds in each iteration, reducing comparisons.

text: What is the worst-case time complexity of searching in a skewed BST?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
O(n log n)
explanation: A skewed BST degenerates into a linked list with height n, so searching takes O(n) time in the worst case.

text: How does binary search handle duplicate elements?
options: It always finds the first occurrence of the target
It finds some occurrence but not necessarily the first or last
It always finds the last occurrence of the target
It fails and returns an error when duplicates exist
explanation: Standard binary search finds an arbitrary occurrence of the target; modifications like lower_bound or upper_bound are needed to find the first or last occurrence.

text: What is the time complexity of the optimal algorithm for finding the kth smallest element?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
O(k log n)
explanation: The median-of-medians algorithm guarantees O(n) worst-case time for selecting the kth smallest element by choosing a good pivot.

text: What is the lower bound for comparison-based searching in a sorted array?
options: O(sqrt(n))
O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: Any comparison-based search on a sorted array of n elements requires Ω(log n) comparisons in the worst case, proven via decision tree arguments.

text: What is fractional cascading used for?
options: Performing binary search across multiple sorted arrays more efficiently
Cascading hash functions for better collision resolution
Splitting arrays into fractional parts for parallel processing
Converting fractional numbers to integers for fast search
explanation: Fractional cascading speeds up binary search across k sorted arrays from O(k log n) to O(k + log n) by threading elements between arrays.

text: What is the expected time complexity of randomized QuickSelect?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: Randomized QuickSelect has O(n) expected time because a random pivot gives a balanced partition on average, reducing the problem size by a constant fraction.

text: What is the purpose of the median-of-medians algorithm?
options: To sort an array in guaranteed O(n log n) time
To compute the exact median in O(log n) time
To find a pivot that guarantees worst-case O(n) selection
To merge two sorted arrays in linear time
explanation: The median-of-medians algorithm selects a pivot that ensures at least 30% of elements are on each side, guaranteeing O(n) worst-case time for selection.

text: What is the time complexity of searching in a van Emde Boas tree?
options: O(log n)
O(sqrt(u))
O(log log u)
explanation: A van Emde Boas tree supports search, predecessor, and successor operations in O(log log u) time where u is the universe size.

text: What is the adversary argument in lower bound proofs for searching?
options: A technique where an adversary forces the algorithm to make maximum comparisons
An argument showing that the best algorithm uses divide and conquer
A proof that all search algorithms have the same complexity
An argument that random algorithms are always suboptimal
explanation: An adversary argument constructs worst-case inputs adaptively, forcing any algorithm to make the maximum number of comparisons to solve the problem.

text: What is the time complexity of finding an element in a sorted matrix where rows and columns are sorted?
options: O(n + m)
O(n)
O(log(n * m))
explanation: Starting from the top-right corner, each comparison eliminates either a row or column, giving O(n + m) time for an n×m sorted matrix.

text: What is the worst-case complexity of QuickSelect without randomization?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
O(log n)
explanation: Without randomization and without median-of-medians, QuickSelect can degrade to O(n^2) if the pivot consistently produces highly unbalanced partitions.

text: How does exponential search achieve O(log i) time where i is the position of the target?
options: It uses interpolation to estimate the exact position directly
It sorts only the relevant portion of the array before searching
It uses a hash table to narrow down the search range first
It doubles the search index until exceeding the target then binary searches the range
explanation: Exponential search doubles the index (1, 2, 4, 8, ...) until it exceeds the target, then binary searches the range [i/2, i], taking O(log i) time.

text: What is the time complexity of bubble sort in the worst case?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: Bubble sort compares and swaps adjacent elements in nested loops, giving O(n^2) time complexity in the worst case.

text: Which sorting algorithm selects the minimum element and places it at the beginning?
options: Merge sort
Selection sort
Quick sort
Insertion sort
explanation: Selection sort repeatedly finds the minimum element from the unsorted portion and places it at the beginning of the sorted portion.

text: What is the best-case time complexity of insertion sort?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: Insertion sort achieves O(n) in the best case when the array is already sorted, as each element requires only one comparison.

text: Is bubble sort a stable sorting algorithm?
options: No, it never preserves relative order
Only when all elements are distinct
Only when the input is already sorted
Yes, it preserves relative order of equal elements
explanation: Bubble sort is stable because it only swaps adjacent elements when they are out of order, preserving the relative order of equal elements.

text: What does it mean for a sorting algorithm to be 'in-place'?
options: It uses only a constant amount of extra memory
It sorts elements without using any variables at all
It sorts elements in their original order always
It can only sort arrays stored on disk
explanation: An in-place sorting algorithm uses only O(1) extra memory beyond the input array, sorting by rearranging elements within the same array.

text: What is the time complexity of merge sort?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: Merge sort divides the array in half recursively and merges sorted halves, giving O(n log n) time in all cases.

text: Which sorting algorithm works by inserting each element into its correct position?
options: Quick sort
Merge sort
Insertion sort
Heap sort
explanation: Insertion sort builds the sorted array one element at a time by inserting each new element into its correct position among the already sorted elements.

text: Is selection sort a stable sorting algorithm?
options: Yes, it always preserves relative order
Only when implemented with linked lists
Only when the input contains no duplicates
No, it may change relative order of equal elements
explanation: Selection sort is not stable because swapping the minimum element with the current position can change the relative order of equal elements.

text: What is the space complexity of merge sort?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: Merge sort requires O(n) extra space to store the temporary arrays during the merge step.

text: What is the average-case time complexity of quick sort?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
O(log n)
explanation: Quick sort has O(n log n) average-case time complexity when the pivot divides the array into roughly equal halves on average.

text: What is the worst-case time complexity of quick sort and when does it occur?
options: O(n log n) when the array has many duplicates
O(n) when the array is already sorted
O(n^2) when the pivot is always the smallest or largest element
O(n^2) only when the array contains negative numbers
explanation: Quick sort degrades to O(n^2) when the pivot is consistently the minimum or maximum, creating maximally unbalanced partitions.

text: How does counting sort achieve linear time complexity?
options: By comparing elements in pairs to reduce comparisons
By merging sorted subarrays in reverse order
By partitioning elements around a pivot value
By counting occurrences of each value and using arithmetic to place elements
explanation: Counting sort counts the frequency of each distinct value, then uses prefix sums to determine each element's final position, achieving O(n + k) time.

text: What is the time complexity of heap sort?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: Heap sort builds a heap in O(n) and then extracts the max/min n times, each taking O(log n), giving O(n log n) total.

text: What is the key idea behind radix sort?
options: Comparing pairs of elements and swapping them
Sorting by individual digits from least significant to most significant
Dividing the array into buckets based on hash values
Recursively splitting the array into halves
explanation: Radix sort processes elements digit by digit using a stable sort (like counting sort) at each digit position, from least significant to most significant.

text: Why is merge sort preferred over quick sort for linked lists?
options: Merge sort has better time complexity on linked lists
Quick sort cannot partition linked list elements
Merge sort needs no random access and can merge without extra space for linked lists
Merge sort uses less stack space than quick sort
explanation: Merge sort is preferred for linked lists because it does not require random access and the merge step can be done in O(1) extra space by adjusting pointers.

text: What is the time complexity of radix sort for n numbers with d digits?
options: O(n + d)
O(n log n)
O(n * d)
explanation: Radix sort runs a stable sort for each of d digit positions, each taking O(n + k) time, giving O(d * (n + k)) which simplifies to O(n * d) for fixed k.

text: What is the advantage of quick sort over merge sort in practice?
options: Quick sort has better cache performance and lower constant factors
Quick sort is always stable while merge sort is not
Quick sort has better worst-case complexity
Quick sort uses O(n) extra space while merge sort uses O(n^2)
explanation: Quick sort works in-place with good cache locality, and its constant factors are smaller than merge sort, making it faster in practice despite worse worst-case bounds.

text: What is bucket sort's average-case time complexity when input is uniformly distributed?
options: O(n + k)
O(n log n)
O(k * n)
explanation: When elements are uniformly distributed, bucket sort distributes them evenly into k buckets and sorts each small bucket, achieving O(n + k) average time.

text: What is a stable sorting algorithm?
options: An algorithm that always runs in the same time regardless of input
An algorithm that uses no extra memory at all
An algorithm that preserves the relative order of equal elements
An algorithm that works correctly on all data types
explanation: A stable sorting algorithm maintains the relative order of records with equal keys, which is important when sorting by multiple criteria.

text: What is the three-way partitioning scheme used in quick sort?
options: Dividing elements into three sorted subarrays
Splitting the array into three random groups
Using three different pivot elements simultaneously
Partitioning into elements less than, equal to, and greater than the pivot
explanation: Three-way partitioning (Dutch National Flag) divides elements into three groups: less than, equal to, and greater than the pivot, handling duplicates efficiently.

text: What is the lower bound for comparison-based sorting proven by the decision tree argument?
options: Ω(n log n)
Ω(n)
Ω(log n)
explanation: The decision tree for n elements has n! leaves, requiring height at least log(n!) = Ω(n log n), proving no comparison sort can be faster.

text: What is the worst-case time complexity of Timsort?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
O(n * sqrt(n))
explanation: Timsort is a hybrid sorting algorithm combining merge sort and insertion sort, guaranteeing O(n log n) worst-case time complexity.

text: What is introsort and why was it designed?
options: A sort that introspects data types to choose the comparison function
An external sorting algorithm that introspects disk access patterns
A hybrid of quicksort, heapsort, and insertion sort to guarantee O(n log n) worst case
A parallel sorting algorithm that distributes work across processors
explanation: Introsort begins with quicksort, switches to heapsort when recursion depth exceeds O(log n) to avoid O(n^2) worst case, and uses insertion sort for small subarrays.

text: What is the time complexity of sorting n strings of maximum length m using radix sort?
options: O(n * log m)
O(n * m * log n)
O(n * m)
explanation: Radix sort processes each of the m character positions once across all n strings, giving O(n * m) time for string sorting.

text: What is the optimal number of comparisons needed to sort 5 elements?
explanation: It has been proven that 7 comparisons are both necessary and sufficient to sort 5 elements, as ⌈log_2(5!)⌉ = ⌈log_2(120)⌉ = 7.

text: What is the worst-case number of comparisons in the Ford-Johnson (merge-insertion) sort for n elements?
options: Exactly n log n
Close to the information-theoretic lower bound ⌈log_2(n!)⌉
Exactly n^2 / 2
Exactly 2n - 1
explanation: The Ford-Johnson algorithm achieves a number of comparisons very close to the information-theoretic lower bound ⌈log_2(n!)⌉ for all n.

text: Why is counting sort not a comparison-based sort?
options: It only works on floating point numbers
It uses random sampling instead of direct comparisons
It determines element positions using value counts instead of comparisons
It processes elements in parallel without comparing them
explanation: Counting sort uses the numeric values directly to index into a count array, bypassing element comparisons entirely and breaking the Ω(n log n) lower bound.

text: What is the external merge sort algorithm used for?
options: Sorting data that fits entirely in main memory
Sorting data stored in compressed format
Sorting elements across multiple networked computers
Sorting data that is too large to fit in main memory using disk I/O
explanation: External merge sort divides data into chunks that fit in memory, sorts each chunk, then merges them using a k-way merge, minimizing disk I/O operations.

text: What is the time complexity of patience sorting?
options: O(n log n)
O(n * sqrt(n))
O(n)
explanation: Patience sorting creates piles in O(n log n) time using binary search, and can also find the longest increasing subsequence length.

text: What is the significance of adaptive sorting algorithms?
options: They always achieve O(n) time regardless of input order
They exploit existing order in the input to sort faster
They adapt to different data types automatically
They adjust memory allocation based on array size
explanation: Adaptive algorithms like Timsort detect and exploit presorted runs in the input, achieving near-linear time on partially sorted data while maintaining O(n log n) worst case.

text: What is a hash function?
options: A function that encrypts data for security
A function that sorts data in hash order
A function that maps data to a fixed-size value
A function that compresses data for storage
explanation: A hash function takes an input (key) and maps it to a fixed-size value (hash code) used as an index in a hash table.

text: What is a hash collision?
options: When a key cannot be hashed at all
When a hash function runs out of memory
When a hash table is completely empty
When two keys produce the same hash value
explanation: A hash collision occurs when two different keys are mapped to the same index in the hash table by the hash function.

text: What is the average-case time complexity of search in a hash table?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
explanation: With a good hash function and low load factor, hash table lookups achieve O(1) average-case time complexity.

text: What is chaining in hash tables?
options: Linking multiple hash tables together sequentially
Storing colliding elements in a linked list at each slot
Using multiple hash functions in sequence for lookup
Connecting hash values in a chain for faster access
explanation: Chaining resolves collisions by maintaining a linked list at each hash table slot, where all elements with the same hash are stored.

text: What is the load factor of a hash table?
options: The maximum key value stored in the table
The total memory used by the hash table
The ratio of stored elements to table size
The number of collisions that have occurred
explanation: The load factor is defined as n/m where n is the number of stored elements and m is the table size, indicating how full the table is.

text: What is open addressing in hash tables?
options: Leaving the hash table open for public access
Allowing the hash table to grow without any limit
Using an open-ended linked list for each bucket
Storing all elements directly in the table and probing for empty slots on collision
explanation: In open addressing, all elements are stored within the hash table itself, and collisions are resolved by probing for the next available slot.

text: What is linear probing?
options: Checking the next consecutive slot when a collision occurs
Probing every other slot in the hash table
Using a linear function to generate hash values
Probing slots in a random linear sequence
explanation: Linear probing resolves collisions by sequentially checking the next slot (index + 1, index + 2, ...) until an empty slot is found.

text: What is the purpose of a hash table?
options: To sort data in ascending order efficiently
To store key-value pairs for fast lookup
To compress large datasets for storage
To encrypt data using reversible functions
explanation: A hash table stores key-value pairs and provides fast insertion, deletion, and lookup operations using hash functions.

text: What is the division method for hashing?
options: Dividing the key by the number of elements stored
Dividing the table into equal-sized partitions
Computing hash as key mod table_size
Splitting the key into two halves and adding them
explanation: The division method computes the hash value as h(key) = key mod m, where m is the table size, typically chosen as a prime number.

text: What happens when a hash table's load factor gets too high?
options: The hash function becomes invalid
The table switches to a binary search tree
The table automatically sorts all elements
Performance degrades due to more collisions
explanation: As the load factor increases, collisions become more frequent, degrading the performance of insertion, deletion, and search operations.

text: What is quadratic probing?
options: Probing slots at quadratically increasing intervals from the original hash
Using a quadratic function as the hash function itself
Probing four slots simultaneously for each collision
Squaring the key before applying the hash function
explanation: Quadratic probing resolves collisions by checking slots at positions h(k)+1^2, h(k)+2^2, h(k)+3^2, etc., reducing primary clustering.

text: What is double hashing?
options: Using two hash tables and storing each element in both
Using a second hash function to determine the probe step size
Applying the hash function twice to the same key
Hashing both the key and the value for storage
explanation: Double hashing uses a second hash function h2(k) to determine the step size for probing: h(k, i) = (h1(k) + i * h2(k)) mod m, reducing clustering.

text: What is primary clustering in hash tables?
options: When the hash function distributes elements too evenly
When all elements hash to prime-numbered indices
When elements form long contiguous runs in linear probing
When multiple hash tables are linked into a single cluster
explanation: Primary clustering occurs in linear probing when occupied slots form long contiguous blocks, increasing the probability of further collisions in that area.

text: What is rehashing?
options: Recomputing hash values using a different hash function
Combining two hash tables into a single larger one
Removing all elements and starting with an empty table
Creating a larger table and reinserting all existing elements
explanation: Rehashing creates a new, larger hash table and reinserts all existing elements using the hash function on the new table size to reduce the load factor.

text: What is the multiplication method for hashing?
options: Multiplying the key by a constant and extracting fractional part times table size
Multiplying two keys together to resolve collisions
Using matrix multiplication to compute hash values
Multiplying the table size by the load factor
explanation: The multiplication method computes h(k) = ⌊m * (k * A mod 1)⌋ where A is a constant between 0 and 1, often chosen as (√5 - 1)/2.

text: Why is the table size often chosen as a prime number in hash tables?
options: Prime numbers make the hash table use less memory
Prime sizes help distribute keys more uniformly and reduce clustering
Prime numbers prevent the table from being resized
Prime sizes make deletion operations faster
explanation: A prime table size helps ensure that the hash function distributes keys more uniformly, as it avoids patterns that can arise from common factors.

text: What is the expected chain length in a hash table with chaining when the load factor is α?
options: α
log α
explanation: With simple uniform hashing and chaining, the expected length of each chain is equal to the load factor α = n/m.

text: What is a Bloom filter?
options: A hash table that automatically removes old entries
A hash function that produces uniformly distributed values
A filter that removes duplicate hash values from a table
A space-efficient probabilistic data structure for set membership testing
explanation: A Bloom filter uses multiple hash functions and a bit array to test set membership, allowing false positives but never false negatives.

text: What is the worst-case time complexity of operations in a hash table with chaining?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: In the worst case, all n elements hash to the same slot, creating a single chain of length n, making search O(n).

text: How does separate chaining handle deletion compared to open addressing?
options: Both handle deletion identically in all cases
Chaining simply removes the node while open addressing needs special markers
Open addressing deletes faster than chaining
Chaining cannot support deletion at all
explanation: In chaining, deletion just removes a node from the linked list. In open addressing, deleted slots need tombstone markers to avoid breaking probe sequences.

text: What is universal hashing?
options: A single hash function that works optimally for all inputs
Using the same hash function across all programming languages
Randomly selecting a hash function from a family to guarantee expected O(1) performance
A hash function based on universal mathematical constants
explanation: Universal hashing randomly selects a hash function from a carefully designed family, ensuring that for any input, the expected number of collisions is low.

text: What is perfect hashing?
options: A hash function that never produces any collisions for any input set
A hashing method that uses zero extra memory beyond the keys
A hash function that perfectly distributes elements in sorted order
A two-level hashing scheme that guarantees O(1) worst-case lookup for a static set
explanation: Perfect hashing uses a two-level scheme: a first-level hash table with second-level tables sized to avoid collisions, achieving O(1) worst-case lookup for static sets.

text: What is cuckoo hashing?
options: A hashing scheme that uses two hash functions and two tables, displacing elements on collision
A hashing method where elements are sorted like a cuckoo clock mechanism
A scheme that hashes elements into a tree structure instead of an array
A method that removes the oldest element when a collision occurs
explanation: Cuckoo hashing uses two hash functions and two tables; on collision, the existing element is displaced to its alternate location, achieving O(1) worst-case lookup.

text: What is the false positive rate of a Bloom filter with k hash functions, m bits, and n elements?
options: k / (m * n)
(1 - e^(-kn/m))^k approximately
n / m
explanation: The false positive probability of a Bloom filter is approximately (1 - e^(-kn/m))^k, minimized when k = (m/n) * ln 2.

text: What is consistent hashing primarily used for?
options: Sorting elements consistently across multiple hash tables
Ensuring that the same key always gets the same hash value
Distributing data across servers so that adding or removing a server affects minimal keys
Making hash functions produce consistent output lengths
explanation: Consistent hashing maps both keys and servers onto a ring, so adding or removing a server only requires remapping O(n/k) keys on average.

text: What is the expected space complexity of perfect hashing for n keys?
options: O(n * sqrt(n))
O(n log n)
O(n)
explanation: Using the FKS (Fredman-Komlós-Szemerédi) scheme, perfect hashing achieves O(n) expected space by carefully sizing second-level tables.

text: What is Robin Hood hashing?
options: A scheme where rich (short probe) elements give their slot to poor (long probe) elements
A scheme that steals hash values from neighboring tables
A method that redistributes all elements equally on each insertion
A parallel hashing method that divides work among multiple threads
explanation: Robin Hood hashing is an open addressing variant where elements with shorter probe distances are displaced by elements with longer probe distances, reducing variance.

text: What is the amortized cost of insertion in a hash table that doubles in size when the load factor exceeds a threshold?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
O(sqrt(n))
explanation: Although resizing costs O(n), it happens infrequently enough that the amortized cost of each insertion remains O(1) by the doubling strategy.

text: What is tabulation hashing?
options: A scheme that tabulates all possible hash collisions beforehand
A hashing technique that organizes data in tabular format
A method that uses lookup tables of random values XORed together to compute the hash
A method that uses multiplication tables for hash computation
explanation: Tabulation hashing splits the key into bytes, looks up a random value for each byte from precomputed tables, and XORs them together, providing strong independence properties.

text: What is the advantage of hopscotch hashing over linear probing?
options: It uses less memory per element than linear probing
It does not require a hash function at all
It eliminates all hash collisions completely
It bounds the maximum probe distance to a small neighborhood
explanation: Hopscotch hashing constrains each element to be within a fixed-size neighborhood of its home bucket, bounding probe distances and improving cache performance.

text: What is an inorder traversal of a binary search tree?
options: Visit left subtree, then root, then right subtree
Visit left subtree, then right subtree, then root
Visit root, then left subtree, then right subtree
Visit nodes level by level from top to bottom
explanation: Inorder traversal visits the left subtree, then the root, then the right subtree, producing elements in sorted order for a BST.

text: What is a preorder traversal?
options: Visit left subtree, then root, then right subtree
Visit root, then left subtree, then right subtree
Visit left subtree, then right subtree, then root
Visit nodes in reverse level order
explanation: Preorder traversal visits the root first, then recursively visits the left subtree, followed by the right subtree.

text: What is a postorder traversal?
options: Visit root, then left subtree, then right subtree
Visit nodes level by level from left to right
Visit left subtree, then right subtree, then root
Visit left subtree, then root, then right subtree
explanation: Postorder traversal visits the left subtree, then the right subtree, and finally the root node.

text: What is the time complexity of searching in a balanced BST?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: A balanced BST has height O(log n), so searching requires traversing at most O(log n) nodes from root to leaf.

text: What is level-order traversal also known as?
options: Breadth-first traversal
Inorder traversal
Depth-first traversal
Postorder traversal
explanation: Level-order traversal visits nodes level by level from top to bottom, which is also called breadth-first traversal, typically implemented using a queue.

text: What is the BST property?
options: Every node must have exactly two children
Left child values are less than parent and right child values are greater
All leaf nodes must be at the same level
Parent values must equal the sum of child values
explanation: The BST property requires that for every node, all values in the left subtree are less and all values in the right subtree are greater.

text: How is a BST node with two children deleted?
options: By simply removing the node and its children
By swapping it with the root node first
By replacing it with its inorder successor or predecessor
By converting the tree to an array and rebuilding
explanation: When deleting a node with two children from a BST, it is replaced with either its inorder successor (smallest in right subtree) or inorder predecessor (largest in left subtree).

text: Where is the minimum element located in a BST?
options: At the root of the tree
At the rightmost node of the tree
At any leaf node of the tree
At the leftmost node of the tree
explanation: Due to the BST property, the minimum element is always at the leftmost node, found by repeatedly following left child pointers.

text: What data structure is used to implement level-order traversal?
options: Queue
Stack
Hash table
Priority queue
explanation: Level-order traversal uses a queue to process nodes level by level, enqueueing children of each visited node.

text: What is the time complexity of inserting a node into a balanced BST?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
O(n log n)
explanation: Insertion in a balanced BST traverses from root to a leaf, taking O(log n) time proportional to the tree height.

text: What is an AVL tree?
options: A binary tree where all nodes have exactly two children
A tree where nodes are sorted by insertion order
A self-balancing BST where heights of subtrees differ by at most 1
