To prevent unauthorized access to list contents
explanation: A sentinel simplifies insertion and deletion at boundaries by eliminating special cases.

text: What is the time complexity of reversing a singly linked list iteratively?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
explanation: Iteratively reversing requires one pass through all n nodes, adjusting pointers for O(n) time.

text: What data structure is commonly used to check balanced parentheses?
options: Queue
Array
Stack
Linked list
explanation: A stack matches brackets: push opening brackets and pop when a matching closing bracket appears.

text: What is a deque (double-ended queue)?
options: A queue storing elements in sorted order
A queue allowing insertion and deletion at both ends
A queue with two priority levels
A queue using two arrays for storage
explanation: A deque supports insertion and deletion at both front and rear ends.

text: What advantage does a linked list stack have over an array stack?
options: Linked lists provide faster push and pop operations
Linked lists do not have a fixed capacity limit
Linked lists use less memory per element
Linked lists allow random access to stack elements
explanation: A linked list stack grows dynamically without a predetermined size limit.

text: What is the XOR linked list technique?
options: Nodes connected using XOR of adjacent addresses
Data stored in XOR-encrypted format
XOR operations used for sorting elements
Even and odd nodes linked separately
explanation: An XOR linked list stores the XOR of previous and next addresses, achieving doubly-linked traversal with single-pointer memory.

text: What is the amortized time of appending to a dynamic array that doubles?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
O(sqrt(n))
explanation: Although individual resizes cost O(n), the total cost of n insertions is O(n), giving O(1) amortized.

text: How can you detect a cycle in a linked list using constant space?
options: Store visited addresses in a hash set
Use two pointers moving at different speeds
Reverse the list and check for duplicates
Count nodes and compare with expected size
explanation: Floyd's algorithm uses slow (1 step) and fast (2 steps) pointers; they meet inside the cycle if one exists.

text: What is the time complexity of finding a cycle's start using Floyd's algorithm?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
explanation: After detection, reset one pointer to head and move both at same speed to find the cycle start in O(n).

text: What is a skip list's expected search time complexity?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
O(n log n)
explanation: A skip list uses multiple levels with probabilistic balancing, achieving expected O(log n) search.

text: What is the cache performance difference between arrays and linked lists?
options: Linked lists have better cache performance due to pointer locality
Arrays have better cache performance due to contiguous memory layout
Both have identical cache performance in modern processors
Cache performance depends only on stored data size
explanation: Arrays benefit from spatial locality and CPU cache prefetching due to contiguous storage.

text: How does an unrolled linked list improve performance?
options: By storing multiple elements per node to reduce pointer overhead
By removing all pointers and using index-based access
By compressing node data using run-length encoding
By splitting into sorted sublists for fast merging
explanation: An unrolled linked list stores arrays in each node, reducing pointer overhead and improving cache utilization.

text: What problem does the lock-free queue solve?
options: Preventing memory leaks in garbage-collected environments
Allowing multiple threads to enqueue and dequeue without mutex locks
Ensuring elements are processed in priority order
Compressing queue data for network transmission
explanation: Lock-free queues use atomic operations (CAS) for concurrent access without blocking.

text: What is the worst-case space overhead of a skip list?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
O(n log n)
explanation: A skip list uses O(n) expected extra space, with geometrically fewer nodes at each level.

text: What is the significance of the gap buffer data structure?
options: It provides O(1) insertions at a movable cursor position in text editors
It ensures even distribution across hash table buckets
It bridges gap between stack and queue operations
It buffers network data gaps during packet loss
explanation: A gap buffer maintains a gap at the cursor position, allowing O(1) local insertions and deletions.

text: What is a binary tree?
options: A tree where each node has exactly two children
A tree where each node has at most two children
A tree storing only binary data values
A tree with exactly two levels of depth
explanation: A binary tree allows each node to have at most two children, called left and right.

text: What is the root of a tree?
options: The node with the most children
The topmost node that has no parent
The deepest node in the leftmost branch
The node with the smallest data value
explanation: The root is the topmost node with no parent, serving as the starting point for traversals.

text: What are leaf nodes in a tree?
options: Nodes with exactly one child each
Nodes that have no children at all
Nodes at the second level of the tree
Nodes storing the largest values
explanation: Leaf (terminal) nodes have no children, located at the ends of branches.

text: What is a complete binary tree?
options: A tree where every node has exactly two children
A tree where all levels are full except possibly the last filling left to right
A tree where all leaves are at the same level
A tree containing the maximum possible nodes
explanation: A complete binary tree fills all levels except possibly the last, which fills left to right.

text: What is the maximum number of nodes in a binary tree of height h?
options: 2h
explanation: A binary tree of height h can have at most 2^(h+1) - 1 nodes when fully filled.

text: What is a graph in data structures?
options: A visual chart showing statistics
A collection of vertices connected by edges
A type of balanced binary search tree
A linear sequence of connected nodes
explanation: A graph is a non-linear structure of vertices (nodes) and edges connecting pairs of vertices.

text: What is the degree of a node in a tree?
options: The level where the node is located
The number of children that node has
The total number of edges in the tree
The depth of the longest path from it
explanation: The degree of a node is the number of children (subtrees) attached to it.

text: What is a min-heap?
options: A heap where parent is smaller than its children
A heap where parent is larger than its children
A heap with the minimum possible nodes
A heap storing only the smallest half of elements
explanation: In a min-heap, every parent is less than or equal to its children, with minimum at the root.

text: What is an undirected graph?
options: A graph with only one-way edges
A graph where edges have no directional association
A graph containing no cycles at all
A graph with edges carrying weight values
explanation: In an undirected graph, edges have no direction; connections go both ways.

text: What is the height of a single-node tree?
explanation: A single-node tree has height 0: no edges on the path from root to deepest leaf.

text: What differentiates a full binary tree from a complete binary tree?
options: Full trees have more nodes than complete trees
Full tree nodes have 0 or 2 children; complete fills levels left to right
Complete trees are always full trees
Full trees allow one-child nodes but complete does not
explanation: Full: every node has 0 or 2 children. Complete: all levels full except last, filled left to right.

text: How is a binary heap typically stored in memory?
options: As a linked list with parent pointers
As a hash table with priority keys
As an array where children of index i are at 2i+1 and 2i+2
As a doubly linked list sorted by priority
explanation: Binary heaps use arrays: children at 2i+1 and 2i+2, parent at (i-1)/2.

text: What is the time complexity of inserting into a binary heap?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
O(log n)
explanation: Insertion places the element at the end and bubbles up, taking at most O(log n) time.

text: What is an adjacency matrix representation?
options: A linked list of neighbors per vertex
A 2D array where entry (i,j) indicates an edge between i and j
A sorted list of all edges
A hash table mapping vertices to degrees
explanation: An adjacency matrix uses M[i][j] = 1 (or weight) for edge from i to j, else 0.

text: What is the space complexity of an adjacency list for V vertices and E edges?
options: O(V + E)
O(V * E)
explanation: Adjacency lists use O(V) for vertex array and O(E) for edge entries, totaling O(V + E).

text: What BST property makes searching efficient?
options: All nodes are at the same depth level
Left child values are less than parent, right child values are greater
Each node stores a hash of its subtree
The tree is always perfectly balanced after insertion
explanation: The BST property enables binary search-style traversal with left smaller and right larger.

text: What happens during heapify-down in a max-heap?
options: The root is removed and replaced by the last element
A node is swapped with its largest child until heap property holds
All elements are re-sorted in descending order
A new element is inserted at the correct position directly
explanation: Heapify-down swaps a node with its largest child until the max-heap property is restored.

text: What is a weighted graph?
options: A graph with assigned importance scores per vertex
A graph where each edge has an associated numerical value
A graph with more edges than vertices
A graph where vertex degrees are all equal
explanation: In a weighted graph, edges carry numerical weights representing distances, costs, or capacities.

text: What is the time complexity of building a heap from an unsorted array?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: Bottom-up heapify takes O(n) since most nodes are near the bottom and need little sifting.

text: What is the in-order successor in a BST?
options: The parent of the current node
The smallest node in the right subtree
The largest node in the left subtree
The node inserted immediately after
explanation: The in-order successor is the smallest value greater than the current node, found as the leftmost of the right subtree.

text: What is a Fibonacci heap's advantage over a binary heap?
options: It uses less memory per node
It supports decrease-key in O(1) amortized time
It guarantees worst-case O(1) for all operations
It maintains perfectly balanced structure always
explanation: Fibonacci heaps support decrease-key in O(1) amortized, crucial for Dijkstra's and Prim's algorithms.

text: What is the time complexity of delete-min in a Fibonacci heap?
options: O(1) amortized
O(log n) amortized
O(n) amortized
O(n log n) amortized
explanation: Delete-min runs in O(log n) amortized due to consolidation of trees of equal degree.

text: What is a threaded binary tree?
options: A tree using multiple processor threads for parallel access
A tree where null pointers become in-order predecessor/successor links
A tree where each node stores a thread ID
A tree linking sibling nodes for level-order traversal
explanation: Threaded binary trees replace null pointers with in-order predecessor/successor links for stackless traversal.

text: What is the worst-case insertion time in an unbalanced BST?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
O(n log n)
explanation: An unbalanced BST can degenerate into a linked list with sorted insertions, making insertion O(n).

text: What is a binomial heap?
options: A heap based on binomial coefficients for values
A collection of binomial trees satisfying the heap property
A heap using binary representation for storage
A two-level heap with inner and outer queues
explanation: A binomial heap is a collection of binomial trees, each satisfying heap property with no two of same order.

text: What is the Euler tour technique for trees?
options: It converts tree problems into range query problems on arrays
It finds the shortest Euler path in a weighted tree
It detects cycles in undirected graphs
It balances trees using rotation operations
explanation: The Euler tour linearizes a tree into an array for range queries with segment trees or RMQ.

text: What is a van Emde Boas tree used for?
options: Storing strings with common prefixes
Supporting priority queue operations in O(log log u) time
Balancing BSTs after insertions and deletions
Indexing multidimensional spatial data
explanation: vEB trees support insert, delete, predecessor, and successor in O(log log u) where u is universe size.

text: Why are adjacency lists better than matrices for sparse graphs?
options: Matrices are always more efficient for sparse graphs
Lists use O(V+E) space versus O(V^2) for matrices
Both use identical space for sparse graphs
Matrices support faster edge insertion for sparse graphs
explanation: For sparse graphs (E << V^2), adjacency lists use significantly less space.

text: What is a pairing heap?
options: A heap storing elements in pairs for sorting
A self-adjusting heap with simple structure and efficient amortized operations
A heap pairing nodes by binary representation
A combination of min-heap and max-heap
explanation: Pairing heaps achieve O(1) amortized insert and O(log n) amortized delete-min with simple implementation.

text: What is the amortized time of union in a Fibonacci heap?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
O(sqrt(n))
explanation: Fibonacci heap union concatenates two root lists in O(1), very efficient for frequent merges.

text: What is linear search?
options: A search dividing data in half each time
A search checking each element sequentially from start to end
A search using a hash table for lookups
A search requiring sorted data to function
explanation: Linear search examines each element one by one until the target is found or the end is reached.

text: What is the prerequisite for binary search?
options: The array must contain unique elements only
The array must be sorted in order
The array must have an even number of elements
The array must be stored in a linked list
explanation: Binary search requires sorted data to eliminate half the remaining elements each step.

text: What is the worst-case time complexity of linear search?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
explanation: In the worst case, linear search checks every element, taking O(n) time.

text: What is the time complexity of binary search?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: Binary search halves the search space each step, resulting in O(log n).

text: In binary search, what happens when the target is less than the middle?
options: Search continues in the right half
Search continues in the left half
Search restarts from the beginning
The middle element is returned
explanation: If target < middle, binary search eliminates the right half and continues left.

text: What does binary search return when the element is not found?
options: The index of the nearest element
An indication the element does not exist
The index of the last element checked
The middle index of the search range
explanation: When the range is exhausted, binary search returns a sentinel value indicating absence.

text: Which search is best for small unsorted datasets?
options: Binary search
Interpolation search
Linear search
Exponential search
explanation: Linear search is simplest and most practical for small unsorted datasets.

text: What is the best-case time complexity of linear search?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: Best case: target is the first element, requiring one comparison for O(1).

text: How many comparisons does binary search make at most for 1024 elements?
explanation: log2(1024) = 10 comparisons maximum.

text: What is a sentinel in linear search?
options: A special value placed at the end to avoid boundary checks
A variable counting comparisons made
A flag indicating search success
A pointer to the middle element
explanation: A sentinel eliminates the end-of-array check by placing the target at the end.

text: What is exponential search?
options: Checking exponentially increasing indices then binary searching the range
Doubling the search key value at each step
A search only for exponentially growing datasets
Using exponentiation for hash value computation
explanation: Exponential search checks indices 1,2,4,8,... then binary searches within the found range.

text: What is the average time of interpolation search on uniform data?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
O(log log n)
O(sqrt(n))
explanation: For uniformly distributed sorted data, interpolation search achieves O(log log n) on average.

text: How does ternary search differ from binary search?
options: Ternary works on unsorted arrays
Ternary divides the range into three parts instead of two
Ternary uses three pointers from different directions
Ternary is always faster than binary search
explanation: Ternary search splits into three parts but makes more comparisons than binary search.

text: What is jump search and its optimal jump size?
options: Random positions with jump log(n)
Jumping ahead by blocks of sqrt(n) then linear search
Forward/backward jumps of n/2
Jumping between sorted sublists of size n/3
explanation: Jump search moves in sqrt(n) blocks then does linear search within, giving O(sqrt(n)).

text: What is the search time in a balanced BST?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
O(n log n)
explanation: A balanced BST has O(log n) height, so search follows a root-to-leaf path.

text: What is the worst-case time of interpolation search?
options: O(log log n)
O(log n)
O(n)
O(n log n)
explanation: With highly skewed data, interpolation search degrades to O(n).

text: How does binary search find the first occurrence of a duplicate?
options: Returns any occurrence requiring post-processing
Continues searching left even after finding a match
Automatically returns the first occurrence
Removes duplicates before searching
explanation: Modified binary search narrows the left boundary after finding the target to locate the leftmost match.

text: What is Fibonacci search's advantage over binary search?
options: Lower time complexity overall
Uses addition and subtraction instead of division
Works on unsorted data efficiently
Requires less memory for the search range
explanation: Fibonacci search avoids division, using only addition/subtraction which can be faster on some hardware.

text: What determines a search algorithm's space complexity?
options: The input array size
Additional memory used beyond the input data
The number of comparisons made
The size of the element searched for
explanation: Space complexity measures extra memory beyond input, such as stacks or auxiliary structures.

text: Why is binary search preferred for large sorted arrays?
options: Binary search uses less memory
It eliminates half the data each step giving O(log n) versus O(n)
It handles unsorted data efficiently
It requires fewer lines of code
explanation: O(log n) is dramatically faster than O(n) for large datasets.

text: What is fractional cascading?
options: Distributing queries across threads fractionally
Speeding up binary search across multiple sorted lists by sharing information
Cascading hash functions for collision-free searching
Fractionally splitting ranges for parallel processing
explanation: Fractional cascading preprocesses multiple sorted lists so a search in one speeds up searches in others.

text: What is the lower bound for comparison-based searching in a sorted array?
options: O(log log n)
O(log n)
O(sqrt(n))
explanation: Any comparison-based search requires at least log2(n) comparisons in the worst case.

text: What is the worst-case search time in a skip list?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
O(n log n)
O(sqrt(n))
explanation: Although expected O(log n), worst case is O(n) with degenerate random level assignments.

text: How does van Emde Boas tree achieve O(log log u) search?
options: Using a balanced BST with augmented nodes
Recursively dividing universe into sqrt(u) clusters
Hashing into buckets of logarithmic size
Maintaining a sorted doubly linked list
explanation: A vEB tree divides universe u into sqrt(u) clusters, reducing problem size from u to sqrt(u) at each level.

text: What is a search tree with implicit keys?
options: Keys computed from node positions rather than stored
Keys encrypted for secure searching
All keys hashed before insertion
Keys derived from parent-child relationships only
explanation: Implicit key trees determine keys from positions (e.g., subtree sizes) rather than storing them.

text: What is the search complexity in an optimal BST?
options: O(1) for all queries
O(log n) worst case but minimizes expected cost
O(n) with guaranteed uniform distribution
O(n log n) across all queries
explanation: An optimal BST minimizes expected search cost given known frequencies, still O(log n) worst case.

text: What role does the summary structure play in vEB trees?
options: Replaces cluster structures entirely
Stores min and max of each cluster
Links all elements in sorted order
Tracks which clusters are non-empty for fast navigation
explanation: The summary is a smaller vEB tree tracking which clusters contain elements, enabling O(log log u) queries.

text: What is finger search?
options: Starting from a given reference point rather than the root
Using fingerprint hashing for fast matching
Optimized for touch-screen input
Probing at finger-like branching points
explanation: Finger search starts from a given element and finds nearby targets in O(log d) where d is the distance.

text: What is the rank-select problem?
options: Determining a search algorithm's rank among alternatives
Supporting count of elements up to a value and finding the kth smallest
Selecting the best strategy based on data distribution
Ranking hash functions by collision resistance
explanation: Rank returns count of elements <= x; select returns the kth smallest, crucial for compressed structures.

text: How does a bloom filter assist in search operations?
options: Provides exact results with zero false positives
Quickly determines if an element is definitely not in a set
Compresses search space into a smaller sorted array
Accelerates binary search by predicting the midpoint
explanation: Bloom filters test membership with no false negatives but possible false positives, useful as a pre-filter.

text: What is the basic idea behind bubble sort?
options: Dividing the array into halves and merging
Repeatedly swapping adjacent elements if in wrong order
Selecting the minimum and placing it at the start
Inserting each element into its correct position
explanation: Bubble sort compares and swaps adjacent elements, bubbling larger elements to correct positions.

text: What is the time complexity of selection sort in all cases?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
O(log n)
explanation: Selection sort always scans unsorted portion for the minimum, giving O(n^2) regardless of input.

text: Which sorting algorithm is considered stable?
options: Selection sort
Heap sort
Insertion sort
Quick sort
explanation: Insertion sort is stable: it only moves elements past those strictly greater, preserving equal-key order.

text: What does it mean for a sort to be in-place?
options: It sorts without comparing elements
It uses only constant extra memory
It sorts in descending order only
It sorts in a single pass
explanation: An in-place sort uses O(1) additional memory beyond the input array.

text: What is the best-case time complexity of insertion sort?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
explanation: Already sorted input needs one comparison per element, giving O(n) best case.

text: How does selection sort work?
options: Divides array into sorted/unsorted halves repeatedly
Finds the minimum from unsorted part and places it at the beginning
Compares adjacent elements and swaps if needed
Inserts each new element into a sorted subarray
explanation: Selection sort finds the minimum in the unsorted portion and swaps it to the correct position.

text: What is the space complexity of bubble sort?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: Bubble sort is in-place, using only a constant amount of extra space.

text: Which sorting algorithm builds the sorted array one element at a time?
options: Merge sort
Quick sort
Insertion sort
Heap sort
explanation: Insertion sort inserts each new element into its correct position in the already-sorted portion.

text: What is the worst-case time complexity of bubble sort?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
explanation: Reverse-ordered input causes maximum swaps, resulting in O(n^2).

text: What does a stable sort guarantee?
options: It always runs in O(n log n)
It preserves relative order of elements with equal keys
It sorts in both ascending and descending order
It never performs unnecessary comparisons
explanation: A stable sort maintains original relative order of records with equal keys.

text: What is the worst-case time complexity of quicksort?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: Quicksort degrades to O(n^2) when pivots consistently produce highly unbalanced partitions.

text: Why is merge sort preferred for linked lists?
options: Better time complexity for linked lists
Does not need random access, uses sequential operations
Quicksort cannot be implemented for linked lists
Merge sort uses less memory for linked lists
explanation: Merge sort works well with linked lists since it only needs sequential access for merging.

text: What partitioning scheme does quicksort use?
options: Divides into three equal parts always
Places elements smaller than pivot before it and larger after
Splits even/odd indexed elements separately
Groups elements by digit values
explanation: Quicksort partitions so elements less than pivot go left, greater go right.

text: What advantage does counting sort have?
options: Works on any data type including strings
Runs in O(n+k) time when range k is small
Uses less memory than any comparison sort
Is always stable without extra effort
explanation: Counting sort achieves O(n+k) by counting occurrences, faster than O(n log n) when k is small.

text: How does radix sort achieve linear time?
options: Using a hash table for one-pass sorting
Sorting digit by digit from least to most significant using a stable sort
Recursively dividing until single elements remain
Selecting pivots guaranteeing balanced partitions
explanation: Radix sort processes digits using a stable sort like counting sort, taking O(d(n+k)) time.

text: What is the time complexity of heap sort?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
O(log n)
explanation: Heap sort builds a heap in O(n) and extracts max n times at O(log n) each, giving O(n log n).

text: What is the key difference between merge sort and quicksort?
options: Merge sort is iterative, quicksort is recursive
Merge sort works during merging, quicksort during partitioning
Merge sort only works on integers
Merge sort uses hashing, quicksort uses comparison
explanation: Merge sort splits trivially and merges; quicksort partitions and combines trivially.

text: What is median-of-three pivot selection for?
options: Guaranteeing O(n log n) worst case
Reducing worst-case likelihood by choosing a better pivot
Eliminating the partition function
Ensuring the pivot is the exact median
explanation: Median-of-three picks the median of first, middle, last elements to avoid unbalanced partitions.

text: Why is insertion sort efficient for nearly sorted arrays?
options: It skips elements already in position
Each element moves only a few positions, giving near O(n)
It detects sorted subarrays and skips them
It uses binary search for insertion positions
explanation: In nearly sorted arrays, each element is close to its final position, so inner loop runs few times.

text: What is shell sort?
options: A sort using shell scripts for external sorting
A generalized insertion sort comparing elements separated by a gap sequence
A sort based on the shell data structure
A sort wrapping around the array circularly
explanation: Shell sort improves insertion sort by comparing far-apart elements using a decreasing gap sequence.

text: What is the Sedgewick gap sequence's significance in shell sort?
options: Guarantees O(n log n) worst case
Provides gaps achieving O(n^(4/3)) worst-case time
Generates random gaps for probabilistic guarantees
Produces power-of-two gaps for cache efficiency
explanation: Sedgewick's gap sequence achieves O(n^(4/3)) worst case, among the best known.

text: What is introsort?
options: A sort using interpolation to place elements
A hybrid starting with quicksort then switching to heapsort when depth exceeds a limit
A sort designed for integers only
A sort introspecting data distribution to choose between counting and radix sort
explanation: Introsort switches to heapsort when recursion depth exceeds 2*log(n), guaranteeing O(n log n) worst case.

text: What is Timsort's worst-case time complexity?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
O(n log^2 n)
explanation: Timsort achieves O(n log n) worst case by merging natural runs with galloping mode.

text: What is a sorting network?
options: A distributed system for sorting across machines
A fixed sequence of comparators sorting any input of given size
A neural network for sorted orderings
A network topology for parallel data transmission
explanation: A sorting network is a fixed compare-and-swap sequence that correctly sorts any input of size n.

text: What is the AKS sorting network's depth?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
O(log n)
O(log^2 n)
explanation: AKS achieves optimal O(log n) depth, though with a very large constant factor.

text: How do sort lower bounds relate to information theory?
options: Each comparison gains one bit; log2(n!) bits needed to determine the permutation
Information theory proves sorts cannot be parallelized
Input entropy determines the upper bound of sorting time
Stable sorts always require more comparisons
explanation: Each comparison yields one bit; distinguishing n! permutations requires log2(n!) = Theta(n log n) bits.

text: What is dual-pivot quicksort used in Java?
options: A quicksort using two pivots to partition into three parts
A quicksort running two sorts and merging results
A quicksort with two recursive calls using same pivot
A quicksort sorting even/odd elements separately
explanation: Dual-pivot creates three partitions (< p1, between, > p2), achieving fewer swaps on average.

text: What is adaptive sorting?
options: Sorts adapting to hardware architectures
Sorts whose performance improves when input has existing order
Sorts changing comparison function during execution
Sorts adapting memory usage to available RAM
explanation: Adaptive sorts exploit existing order (inversions, runs), like Timsort achieving O(n) on nearly sorted data.

text: What is the optimal number of comparisons to sort 5 elements?
explanation: Exactly 7 comparisons are necessary and sufficient, matching ceil(log2(5!)) = 7.

text: What is patience sorting's connection to LIS?
options: Sorts data in arrival order
Uses card game piles where pile count equals LIS length
Waits for all data before sorting
Repeatedly finds and removes the minimum
explanation: Patience sorting places elements into piles following card game rules; pile count equals the longest increasing subsequence length.

text: What is a hash function?
options: A function encrypting data for secure storage
A function mapping data to a fixed-size value or index
A function sorting elements by hash codes
A function compressing data for storage
explanation: A hash function maps input data to a fixed-size output, typically an index in a hash table.

text: What is a collision in hashing?
options: When a hash table runs out of space
When two different keys produce the same hash value
When the hash function produces a negative index
When a key is inserted into a full bucket
explanation: A collision occurs when two distinct keys map to the same hash table index.

text: What is the average time of a hash table lookup?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: With a good hash function and low load factor, lookups take O(1) average time.

text: What is chaining in collision resolution?
options: Linking multiple hash tables together
Storing colliding elements in a linked list at the same index
Creating a chain of hash functions
Moving colliding elements to the next index
explanation: Chaining maintains a linked list at each slot for all elements mapping to that slot.

text: What is a hash table?
options: A table storing elements sorted by key values
A data structure using hash functions for fast key-value access
A table of hash functions for encryption
A lookup table for data type conversions
explanation: A hash table stores key-value pairs with a hash function for fast insertion, deletion, and lookup.

text: What is the load factor of a hash table?
options: The maximum elements the table can store
The ratio of stored elements to total table size
The time to insert one element
The percentage of collisions occurred
explanation: Load factor is n/m (stored elements / table size), indicating table fullness.

text: What is a desirable hash function property?
options: Always returning the same value for any input
Distributing keys uniformly across the table
Producing output larger than input size
Only working with numeric keys
explanation: Good hash functions distribute keys uniformly to minimize collisions.

text: What happens when searching for an absent key with chaining?
options: The hash table throws an error
The chain at the hashed index is searched finding nothing
The last inserted element is returned
The key is automatically inserted
explanation: The hash function computes the index, the chain is searched, and absence is reported if not found.

text: What is the division method of hashing?
options: Dividing the key by itself for a value of one
Computing hash as key modulo table size
Dividing the table into equal partitions
Using long division to convert to binary
explanation: h(k) = k mod m, where m is table size, typically chosen as a prime number.

text: What is the worst-case time of a hash table lookup?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
explanation: Worst case: all keys hash to one slot, requiring O(n) chain traversal.

text: What is double hashing?
options: Applying the same hash function twice
Using two different hash functions for the probe sequence
Maintaining two separate hash tables
Hashing the output of one hash function with another
explanation: Double hashing uses a second hash function for probe step size, reducing clustering.

text: How do linear probing and quadratic probing differ?
options: Linear checks adjacent slots; quadratic checks at quadratic intervals
Linear is faster but quadratic uses less memory
Linear works for integer keys only
Linear uses chaining while quadratic uses open addressing
explanation: Linear probing: h+1,h+2,...; quadratic: h+1,h+4,h+9,... to reduce primary clustering.

text: What is primary clustering in open addressing?
options: Grouping similar keys for faster access
Long runs of occupied slots forming with linear probing
Organizing the table into key-range clusters
Initial element placement before rehashing
explanation: Primary clustering: linear probing creates long occupied runs, increasing average probe length.

text: What triggers rehashing?
options: First collision occurrence
Load factor exceeding a threshold
Hash function producing a negative value
Duplicate key insertion
explanation: Rehashing triggers when load factor exceeds a threshold (commonly 0.7), creating a larger table.

text: What is the multiplication method of hashing?
options: Multiplying key by table size directly
Multiplying key by a constant, taking fractional part, scaling by table size
Using repeated multiplication for distribution
Multiplying hash values of substrings
explanation: h(k) = floor(m * (kA mod 1)) where A is a constant like the golden ratio.

text: Why choose a prime number as hash table size?
options: Primes make hash functions run faster
Prime sizes distribute keys more uniformly reducing clustering
Primes prevent the table from becoming full
Prime sizes guarantee no collisions
explanation: Prime table size reduces modulo patterns, distributing keys more uniformly.

text: What is Robin Hood hashing?
options: Stealing slots from rich buckets for poor ones
Displacing elements closer to home to reduce probe length variance
Hashing with a Robin Hood cipher
Distributing elements equally across positions
explanation: Robin Hood hashing reduces variance by displacing elements with shorter probe distances.

text: What is the expected probe count for successful search with linear probing at load alpha?
options: 1/(1-alpha)
alpha * n
log(1/alpha)
explanation: Under uniform hashing, approximately (1/2)(1 + 1/(1-alpha)) probes for a successful search.

text: What is universal hashing?
options: A single hash function for all data types
Randomly selecting from a hash function family for good average performance
A hash function standardized across languages
Hashing with every function and taking the minimum
explanation: Universal hashing randomly selects from a designed family, ensuring expected collisions <= 1/m for any pair.

text: What is the advantage of cuckoo hashing?
options: It guarantees worst-case O(1) lookup time
It never requires rehashing
It uses a single hash function
It handles deletions fastest
explanation: Cuckoo hashing uses two hash functions, guaranteeing O(1) lookup since keys are in at most two locations.

text: What is the expected insertion time for cuckoo hashing?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
O(1) amortized
O(sqrt(n))
explanation: Cuckoo hashing achieves O(1) amortized expected insertion despite possible displacement chains.

text: What is consistent hashing?
options: A scheme always producing the same hash for same input
A distributed technique minimizing key redistribution when nodes change
Making hash functions deterministic across platforms
Ensuring hash values are consistently increasing
explanation: Consistent hashing maps keys and nodes onto a ring, redistributing only ~K/n keys on node changes.

text: What is a perfect hash function?
options: A hash function with no computational overhead
A function mapping n keys to n distinct values with no collisions
A function producing uniformly random output
A function working for all possible key sets
explanation: A perfect hash maps a specific set of n keys to n distinct slots with zero collisions.

text: What is the FKS hashing scheme?
options: Two-level hashing achieving O(1) worst-case lookups with O(n) space
A scheme using Fibonacci numbers for probes
A scheme combining linear probing with chaining
A randomized scheme for approximate nearest neighbor
explanation: FKS uses two-level universal hashing for O(1) worst-case lookup with O(n) total space.

text: How does dynamic resizing relate to amortized hash table analysis?
options: Each resize takes O(1) with no amortization needed
Doubling ensures O(1) amortized insert despite O(n) resize cost
Amortized analysis proves resizing is always O(log n)
Resizing eliminates the need for hash functions
explanation: Doubling spreads the O(n) copy cost across n insertions, yielding O(1) amortized insertion.

text: What is tabulation hashing?
options: Storing precomputed hash tables for each byte of the key
Tabulating collision statistics for adaptive rehashing
Using truth tables to compute hash values
A scheme for tabular database structures
explanation: Tabulation hashing XORs precomputed random values for each byte, providing 3-wise independence.

text: What is the birthday paradox's relevance to hashing?
options: Hash tables always need more than n slots for n elements
Collisions are likely even with far fewer elements than slots
It determines optimal constants for hash functions
Hash functions cannot be uniformly distributed
explanation: With m slots, a collision becomes probable after ~sqrt(m) insertions, informing table size choices.

text: What is locality-sensitive hashing (LSH)?
options: A technique optimized for CPU cache locality
A family where similar items hash to the same bucket with high probability
A function preserving memory address locality
A scheme for local area network routing
explanation: LSH hashes similar items together, used for approximate nearest neighbor in high dimensions.

text: What differentiates 2-choice from cuckoo hashing?
options: 2-choice inserts into less loaded of two locations; cuckoo displaces existing elements
Cuckoo uses two choices while 2-choice uses one
2-choice guarantees O(1) lookup while cuckoo does not
They are the same algorithm
explanation: 2-choice places in the less loaded bucket without displacement; cuckoo may evict existing elements.

text: What is hopscotch hashing?
options: Elements hop between two separate tables
Displacement bounded to a small neighborhood around the home slot
Hop count used as secondary hash function
Alternating between open addressing and chaining
explanation: Hopscotch ensures elements stay near their home slot, combining cache-friendliness with good worst-case performance.

text: What is in-order traversal of a binary tree?
options: Visit root, left subtree, right subtree
Visit left subtree, root, right subtree
Visit left subtree, right subtree, root
Visit nodes level by level
explanation: In-order visits left, root, right, producing sorted output for BSTs.

text: What is pre-order traversal of a binary tree?
options: Visit root, left subtree, right subtree
Visit left subtree, root, right subtree
Visit left subtree, right subtree, root
Visit all leaf nodes before internals
explanation: Pre-order visits root first, then left subtree, then right subtree.

text: What is post-order traversal of a binary tree?
options: Visit root, left subtree, right subtree
Visit left subtree, root, right subtree
Visit left subtree, right subtree, root
Visit nodes at even levels first
explanation: Post-order visits left, right, then root.

text: What is level-order traversal also known as?
options: Depth-first traversal
Breadth-first traversal
Reverse in-order traversal
Diagonal traversal
explanation: Level-order visits nodes level by level, a breadth-first approach using a queue.

text: How do you find the height of a binary tree?
options: Count the number of nodes
Recursively compute max of left and right heights plus one
Count the leaf nodes
Compute sum of all node values
explanation: Height = 1 + max(left height, right height), base case returning -1 for null.

text: What data structure is used for level-order traversal?
options: Stack
Queue
Heap
Hash table
explanation: A queue processes nodes in FIFO order for level-by-level traversal.

text: How do you count total nodes in a binary tree?
options: Height multiplied by branching factor
Recursively count left and right subtrees and add one for root
Count only leaves and multiply by two
Traverse leftmost path and double the count
explanation: Total = 1 (root) + count(left) + count(right).

text: What is the time complexity of traversing all n nodes?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
O(n log n)
explanation: Any complete traversal visits each node exactly once for O(n).

text: What does in-order traversal of a BST produce?
options: Elements in reverse sorted order
Elements in sorted ascending order
Elements grouped by level
Elements in insertion order
explanation: In-order of a BST produces ascending order: left (smaller), root, right (larger).

text: What is the space complexity of recursive tree traversal?
options: O(n)
O(h) where h is the height
explanation: Recursive traversal uses call stack proportional to height h.

text: What is Morris in-order traversal?
options: Uses parent pointers for navigation
Uses threading for O(1) space without a stack
Optimized for perfectly balanced trees only
Processes nodes in reverse order
explanation: Morris traversal creates temporary threaded links for stackless O(1) space traversal.

text: How does AVL rotation maintain balance?
options: By swapping parent and child values
By restructuring subtree links to restore height-balance
By removing and reinserting the unbalanced node
By redistributing all elements evenly
explanation: AVL rotations restructure links to restore the property that heights differ by at most 1.

text: What differentiates left from right rotation?
options: Left moves root down-left, right down-right
Left promotes right child to root, right promotes left child
Left only for left-heavy trees and vice versa
Left reduces height, right increases it
explanation: Left rotation makes right child the new root; right rotation makes left child the root.

text: What is the lowest common ancestor (LCA)?
options: Node with smallest value that is ancestor of both
Deepest node that is ancestor of both given nodes
The root which is always ancestor of all
Parent node directly shared by both
explanation: LCA is the deepest node that has both given nodes as descendants.

text: How do you validate a binary search tree?
options: Verify every node has at most two children
Perform in-order traversal checking values are strictly increasing
Check that root is the median value
Verify leaves have no children
explanation: A valid BST produces strictly increasing values on in-order traversal.

text: What is the time complexity of Red-Black tree insertion?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
O(log n)
explanation: Red-Black insertion: O(log n) to find position plus O(log n) for rotations and recolorings.

text: What is the diameter of a binary tree?
options: Number of nodes on the longest path
Number of edges on the longest path between any two nodes
Maximum depth of the leftmost branch
Difference between max and min node values
explanation: Diameter is the longest path (in edges) between any two nodes, which may not pass through the root.

text: How does a B-tree differ from a BST?
options: B-trees store data only in leaves
B-trees have nodes with multiple keys and children for disk efficiency
B-trees are always unbalanced
B-trees use hashing instead of comparison
explanation: B-tree nodes store multiple keys and children, minimizing disk I/O by reducing height.

text: What is a segment tree used for?
options: Segmenting a tree into equal subtrees
Answering range queries and supporting updates efficiently
Storing line segments for geometry
Dividing arrays for parallel processing
explanation: Segment trees support range queries (sum, min, max) and updates in O(log n).

text: What is lazy propagation in a segment tree?
options: Delaying node creation until query access
Deferring updates to children until they are needed
Slowly building the tree level by level
Caching query results
explanation: Lazy propagation defers range updates, pushing down only when nodes are accessed.

text: What is a splay tree's amortized time per operation?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
O(sqrt(n))
explanation: Splay trees achieve O(log n) amortized via splaying, moving accessed nodes to the root.

text: What is heavy-light decomposition used for?
options: Decomposing into heavy/light nodes for memory optimization
Decomposing tree paths into chains for O(log^2 n) path queries
Splitting heavy trees for parallel processing
Classifying edges by weight for MST
explanation: HLD decomposes into heavy-edge chains, allowing path queries via O(log n) chains with segment trees.

text: What is a link-cut tree?
options: Supports link and cut in O(log n) amortized time
Links nodes to nearest cut vertex
A tree for network flow with link constraints
A B-tree variant for disk block operations
explanation: Link-cut trees maintain dynamic forests supporting link, cut, and path queries in O(log n) amortized.

text: What is a Cartesian tree's relationship to an array?
options: Stores Cartesian coordinate pairs for 2D queries
A heap-ordered tree whose in-order traversal yields the original array
Built from Cartesian products of sorted arrays
Stores distances between elements in Cartesian space
explanation: A Cartesian tree satisfies min-heap property and its in-order traversal gives the original sequence.

text: What is the time to construct a Cartesian tree from an array?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
O(n log^2 n)
explanation: A stack-based algorithm processes elements left to right in O(n).

text: What is an Euler tour tree?
options: Represents Euler tour of a graph for bridge detection
A balanced BST of the Euler tour for dynamic forest operations
Visits every edge once for path counting
Derived from Euler's formula for planar graphs
explanation: ETT stores a tree's Euler tour in a balanced BST for O(log n) dynamic operations.

text: What is a persistent segment tree?
options: Saves all previous versions after each update
Stored permanently on disk for crash recovery
Maintains same structure across rebuilds
Has persistent connections to other structures
explanation: A persistent segment tree creates new versions by copying only the O(log n) affected path.

text: What is centroid decomposition?
options: Repeatedly finding and removing the centroid node
Finding the center minimizing max distance to all
Splitting by edge removal into equal components
Decomposing by centrality metric
explanation: Centroid decomposition recursively removes centroids (no subtree > n/2), useful for path queries.

text: What is the query time for LCA with sparse table?
options: O(log n) query, O(n) preprocessing
O(1) query, O(n log n) preprocessing
O(sqrt(n)) query, O(n) preprocessing
O(log^2 n) query, O(n log n) preprocessing
explanation: Euler tour + RMQ sparse table gives O(1) queries after O(n log n) preprocessing.

text: What is a treap and how does it stay balanced?
options: Tree-heap hybrid using random priorities for expected O(log n) height
A tree adapting structure based on access patterns
A tree-queue combination for BFS
A trie with heap properties for string sorting
explanation: A treap assigns random priorities, maintaining BST on keys and heap on priorities for expected O(log n) height.

text: What is BFS (Breadth-First Search)?
options: Explores the deepest path first
Explores all neighbors at current depth before going deeper
Uses a stack for exploration
Finds minimum spanning trees
explanation: BFS explores all vertices at current depth before moving deeper, using a queue.

text: What is DFS (Depth-First Search)?
options: Explores as deep as possible before backtracking
Visits all neighbors before proceeding
Finds shortest paths in weighted graphs
Detects strongly connected components only
explanation: DFS explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking.

text: What data structure does BFS use?
options: Stack
Queue
Heap
Hash table
explanation: BFS uses a queue for level-by-level exploration order.

text: What data structure does DFS typically use?
options: Queue
Heap
Stack or recursion
Priority queue
explanation: DFS uses a stack (explicit or via recursion call stack) for path tracking.

text: What is a connected graph?
options: Every vertex has the same degree
There is a path between every pair of vertices
It contains at least one cycle
All edges have the same weight
explanation: A connected graph has a path between every pair of vertices.

text: What is the time complexity of BFS with V vertices and E edges?
options: O(V)
O(E)
O(V + E)
O(V * E)
explanation: BFS visits each vertex once and examines each edge once: O(V + E).

text: What is a cycle in a graph?
options: An edge connecting a vertex to itself
A path starting and ending at the same vertex
A path visiting every vertex once
A set of edges forming a tree
explanation: A cycle starts from a vertex and returns to it without repeating edges.

text: What is a spanning tree?
options: A tree spanning multiple disconnected graphs
A subgraph including all vertices and being a tree
A tree with maximum possible edges
A binary tree from graph vertex values
explanation: A spanning tree includes all vertices, is connected, and has no cycles (V-1 edges).

text: What is a directed graph (digraph)?
options: All edges point in both directions
Each edge has a direction from one vertex to another
Edges arranged in a specific pattern
Only traversable using BFS
explanation: In a digraph, each edge goes from a source to a destination vertex.

text: What is the degree of a vertex in an undirected graph?
options: Number of paths through it
Number of edges incident to it
Shortest distance to the root
Weight of the heaviest connected edge
explanation: Degree is the number of edges connected to a vertex, counting self-loops twice.

text: What is the time complexity of Dijkstra's with a binary heap?
options: O(V + E)
O((V + E) log V)
O(V * E)
explanation: V extract-min and E decrease-key operations, each O(log V), giving O((V + E) log V).

text: What is topological sorting used for?
options: Sorting edges by weight
Ordering DAG vertices so every edge goes from earlier to later
Finding shortest paths
Sorting vertices by degree
explanation: Topological sort orders DAG vertices such that for every edge (u,v), u precedes v.

text: What is Kruskal's algorithm for?
options: Single-source shortest paths
Finding MST by sorting and adding edges greedily
Detecting cycles in directed graphs
Computing maximum flow
explanation: Kruskal's sorts edges by weight and adds them without creating cycles, using Union-Find.

text: What is Bellman-Ford's advantage over Dijkstra's?
options: Runs faster on dense graphs
Handles negative edge weights
Uses less memory
Works only on undirected graphs
explanation: Bellman-Ford handles negative weights and detects negative cycles.

text: What is Floyd-Warshall for?
options: Single-source shortest paths
All-pairs shortest paths in weighted graphs
Finding minimum spanning trees
Finding strongly connected components
explanation: Floyd-Warshall computes all-pairs shortest paths in O(V^3) with dynamic programming.

text: How does Prim's build an MST?
options: Sorting all edges and adding without cycles
Growing from a start vertex adding cheapest edge to a new vertex
Removing most expensive edges until a tree remains
DFS selecting edges in traversal order
explanation: Prim's grows the MST one vertex at a time, adding the minimum-weight edge to a non-tree vertex.

text: What is a bipartite graph?
options: Divisible into two sets with edges only between different sets
A graph with exactly two components
Every vertex has exactly two neighbors
Edges each connect exactly two vertices
explanation: A bipartite graph partitions into two sets where every edge connects vertices from different sets.

text: How to detect a cycle in a directed graph with DFS?
options: Check if any vertex is visited more than twice
Find a back edge to a vertex in the current recursion stack
Count edges versus vertices
BFS and check if queue empties
explanation: A cycle exists if DFS finds a back edge to an ancestor in the current recursion stack.

text: What is the time complexity of Bellman-Ford?
options: O(V + E)
O(V * E)
O(E log V)
explanation: Bellman-Ford relaxes all E edges V-1 times: O(V * E), plus one pass for negative cycle detection.

text: What is a strongly connected component?
options: A component where every vertex has equal in/out-degree
A maximal subgraph where every vertex is reachable from every other
A component with maximum edges
A subgraph with all edges in the same direction
explanation: An SCC is a maximal set of vertices with directed paths between every pair.

text: What is the time complexity of Tarjan's SCC algorithm?
options: O(V * E)
O(V + E)
O(V^2 log V)
explanation: Tarjan's finds all SCCs in one DFS pass: O(V + E).

text: What is the Ford-Fulkerson method?
options: Finding min cut by removing edges greedily
Repeatedly finding augmenting paths and increasing flow
Using DP to compute flow values
Converting flow to shortest path problem
explanation: Ford-Fulkerson finds augmenting paths in the residual graph and pushes flow until none remain.

text: What is the max-flow min-cut theorem?
options: Max flow = minimum edges in any path
Max flow = minimum capacity of any cut separating source from sink
Min flow = max cut capacity
Max flow bounded by edge count
explanation: Maximum flow equals the minimum total capacity of edges in any s-t cut.

text: What is Hopcroft-Karp's time complexity?
options: O(V * E)
O(E * sqrt(V))
O(V + E)
explanation: Hopcroft-Karp finds maximum bipartite matching in O(E * sqrt(V)).

text: What is the A* search algorithm?
options: Expands all nodes at each depth level
Best-first search using f(n)=g(n)+h(n) with admissible heuristic
Approximation guaranteeing 2-approximation
Randomized search finding paths with high probability
explanation: A* combines actual cost g(n) and heuristic h(n); with admissible heuristic, finds optimal paths.

text: What is push-relabel for maximum flow?
options: Pushes flow and relabels vertices to maintain a valid preflow
Relabels edges to convert directed to undirected
Pushes vertices closer to source during relaxation
Relabels flow values to detect negative cycles
explanation: Push-relabel maintains a preflow with push and relabel operations, achieving O(V^2*E) or O(V^3).

text: What is the planar separator theorem?
options: Every planar graph separable by removing O(sqrt(n)) vertices
Every planar graph has a separator dividing edges equally
Planar graphs separate into bipartite components in linear time
The separator is always a single vertex
explanation: Any n-vertex planar graph can be partitioned into roughly equal parts by removing O(sqrt(n)) vertices.

text: What is Johnson's all-pairs shortest path algorithm?
options: Faster Floyd-Warshall using matrix multiplication
Reweights edges to remove negatives then runs Dijkstra from each vertex
Uses Johnson's rule for scheduling
Parallel graph partitioning for concurrent computation
explanation: Johnson's uses Bellman-Ford for potentials, reweights, then runs Dijkstra from each vertex: O(V^2 log V + VE).

text: What is a minimum vertex cut?
options: Minimum vertices whose removal disconnects the graph
Minimum weight of all vertices combined
Vertex with minimum degree
Minimum edges incident to any vertex
explanation: Minimum vertex cut is the smallest set of vertices whose removal disconnects the graph.

text: What is Edmonds-Karp?
options: Minimum cost maximum matching algorithm
BFS-based Ford-Fulkerson achieving O(V * E^2)
DFS-based Euler circuit algorithm
Maximum independent set algorithm
explanation: Edmonds-Karp uses BFS for shortest augmenting paths, guaranteeing O(V * E^2).

text: What is the greedy algorithm approach?
options: Trying all solutions exhaustively
Making the locally optimal choice at each step
Dividing into overlapping subproblems
Backtracking when choices fail
explanation: Greedy makes the best local choice at each step hoping for a global optimum.

text: What is divide and conquer?
options: Breaking into smaller independent subproblems, solving, and combining
Dividing data in half and discarding one half
Brute force on small inputs
Splitting work between processors
explanation: Divide and conquer breaks problems into subproblems, solves recursively, and combines solutions.

text: What is dynamic programming?
options: Programming that changes algorithms at runtime
Solving by breaking into overlapping subproblems and storing solutions
Writing code adapting to different input types
A paradigm using dynamic memory allocation
explanation: DP breaks problems into overlapping subproblems, solving each once and caching results.

text: What is backtracking?
options: Tracing back through code to find bugs
Building solutions incrementally, abandoning those failing constraints
Reversing algorithm execution steps
Going back to the beginning when a match fails
explanation: Backtracking builds candidates incrementally and abandons those that cannot lead to valid solutions.

text: What is a brute force algorithm?
options: Uses random choices to find solutions
Systematically tries all possible solutions
Uses physical force computations
Designed to break encryption only
explanation: Brute force exhaustively checks all candidates, guaranteeing correctness at high cost.

text: What is memoization?
options: Writing down algorithm steps as documentation
Caching results of expensive function calls to reuse later
Memorizing input data for faster access
Converting recursive solutions to iterative ones
explanation: Memoization stores computed results so repeated calls return the cached result.

text: Which paradigm does binary search use?
options: Dynamic programming
Greedy approach
Divide and conquer
Backtracking
explanation: Binary search divides the sorted array, chooses one half, and discards the other.

text: What is an example of a greedy algorithm?
options: Fibonacci via recursion
N-Queens via backtracking
Coin change using largest denomination first
Matrix chain via DP
explanation: Greedy coin change selects the largest denomination not exceeding the remainder.

text: What is the key requirement for DP applicability?
options: Independent subproblems only
Overlapping subproblems and optimal substructure
Solvable in polynomial time
Exactly two recursive calls
explanation: DP requires overlapping subproblems (same subproblems recur) and optimal substructure.

text: What differentiates top-down from bottom-up DP?
options: Top-down starts from largest, bottom-up from smallest subproblem
Top-down uses recursion with memoization, bottom-up uses iterative tabulation
Top-down is faster, bottom-up uses less memory
Top-down for trees, bottom-up for arrays only
explanation: Top-down: recursion + memoization. Bottom-up: iterative table filling from smallest subproblems.

text: What is the time complexity of LCS using DP?
options: O(m + n)
O(m * n)
explanation: LCS fills an m x n table, giving O(m * n).

text: What is the greedy choice property?
options: Globally optimal via any random choice
Globally optimal via locally optimal choices
Every choice must be validated against all futures
Greedy guarantees polynomial time for all problems
explanation: The greedy choice property: global optimum is achievable by always choosing the local optimum.

text: How does rod cutting demonstrate DP?
options: Cutting randomly and measuring profit
Computing max revenue per length reusing subsolutions
Always cutting into equal pieces
Sorting cuts by profitability and selecting greedily
explanation: Rod cutting tries all first cuts and looks up optimal revenue for the remainder.

text: What distinguishes divide and conquer from DP?
options: D&C cannot handle recursion
D&C has independent subproblems, DP has overlapping ones
DP always uses more memory
D&C is iterative, DP is recursive
explanation: D&C: independent subproblems. DP: overlapping subproblems with cached solutions.

text: How is the activity selection problem solved?
options: DP with a 2D table
Greedy selecting activities with earliest finish times
Backtracking with constraints
Brute force enumeration
explanation: Greedy: always select the activity with earliest compatible finish time.

text: What is the edit distance problem?
options: Shortest path between two graph nodes
Minimum operations to transform one string into another
Distance between two coordinate points
Time complexity difference between algorithms
explanation: Edit distance finds minimum insertions, deletions, and substitutions to transform strings, O(m*n) with DP.

text: What is branch and bound?
options: Branching calls and bounding depth for stack safety
Exploring solution space while pruning branches that cannot improve best known solution
Dividing into branches solved in bounded time
Decision trees with bounded height
explanation: Branch and bound prunes subtrees that cannot contain optimal solutions.

text: What is matrix chain multiplication?
options: Multiplying matrices with parallel hardware
Finding optimal parenthesization to minimize scalar multiplications
Chaining operations in sequence
D&C multiplication only
explanation: Matrix chain multiplication uses DP to find parenthesization minimizing total scalar multiplications.

text: How does Huffman coding work?
options: Equal-length codes for all symbols
Greedily combining two lowest-frequency nodes into a tree
DP for optimal prefix-free codes
Sorting by frequency and assigning sequential codes
explanation: Huffman greedily merges lowest-frequency nodes using a priority queue for optimal prefix-free codes.

text: What is the subset sum problem?
options: Finding sum of all elements
Determining if a subset sums to a target value
Computing minimum sum among subsets
Finding two subsets with equal sums
explanation: Subset sum asks if any subset sums exactly to a target, solvable in O(n*target) with DP.

text: What is the time complexity of optimal BST construction with DP?
options: O(n log n)
explanation: Three nested loops (subproblem size, start, root choice) give O(n^3).

text: What is Knuth's optimization for optimal BST?
options: Reduces from O(n^3) to O(n^2) by constraining root choices
Eliminates need for DP entirely
Converts to MST computation
Randomization for expected O(n log n)
explanation: Knuth's uses monotonicity of optimal roots to reduce search space to O(n^2).

text: What is meet-in-the-middle?
options: D&C processing only the middle portion
Splitting input in half, solving each, combining to reduce exponential time
Finding median as meeting point for sorted arrays
Two processors meeting at computation middle
explanation: Meet-in-the-middle enumerates half-solutions and combines, reducing O(2^n) to O(2^(n/2)).

text: What differentiates 0/1 from unbounded knapsack?
options: 0/1 has weight limits, unbounded does not
In 0/1 each item used at most once, unbounded allows unlimited copies
Unbounded always has greedy solution
0/1 is NP-hard, unbounded is polynomial
explanation: 0/1: each item at most once. Unbounded: unlimited copies of each item.

text: What is TSP time complexity with bitmask DP?
options: O(n!)
explanation: Held-Karp: O(2^n) states with O(n) transitions each = O(n^2 * 2^n).

text: What is the primal-dual method?
options: Simultaneously optimizing primal and dual LP objectives
Using two algorithms and selecting the faster
Alternating between greedy and DP
Designing for both directed and undirected graphs
explanation: Primal-dual designs approximation algorithms by maintaining feasible dual solutions.

text: What is FPTAS?
options: Exact solutions in fully polynomial time
A scheme running in time polynomial in both input size and 1/epsilon
Polynomial algorithm for all NP-complete problems
Fully parallelizing polynomial algorithms
explanation: FPTAS produces (1+epsilon)-approximate solutions in time polynomial in n and 1/epsilon.

text: What is the D&C optimization for DP?
options: Binary search within each DP transition
Exploiting monotone split points to reduce transitions from O(n^2) to O(n log n)
Dividing DP table into quadrants for parallel computation
Replacing DP with D&C when subproblems do not overlap
explanation: When split points are monotone, D&C optimization processes transitions in O(n log n) total.

text: What distinguishes online from offline algorithms?
options: Online runs on internet, offline locally
Online processes input as it arrives, offline has all input upfront
Online uses O(1) memory, offline O(n)
Online is randomized, offline deterministic
explanation: Online: decide as input arrives without future knowledge. Offline: have all input beforehand.

text: What is the competitive ratio of an online algorithm?
options: Ratio of running time to optimal offline
Worst-case ratio of solution quality to optimal offline solution
Ratio of memory: online vs offline
Ratio of correct to total decisions
explanation: Competitive ratio measures worst-case cost ratio between online and optimal offline solutions.

text: What is a trie data structure?
options: A balanced BST variant
A tree storing strings where each node represents a character
A graph structure for routing
A hash table variant for collision-free storage
explanation: A trie stores strings character by character; paths from root represent prefixes.

text: What is Union-Find used for?
options: Finding union of two sorted arrays
Tracking disjoint sets with union and find operations
Searching in a unified database
Finding common elements between sets
explanation: Union-Find maintains a partition into disjoint sets with union and find operations.

text: What is find's time with path compression in Union-Find?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
Nearly O(1) amortized
explanation: With path compression and union by rank, amortized time is O(alpha(n)), practically constant.

text: What is a Bloom filter?
options: A filter removing duplicates
A space-efficient probabilistic structure for membership testing
A sorting filter by value ranges
A data filter for image processing
explanation: Bloom filters use hash functions and a bit array: possible false positives, no false negatives.

text: What is a trie's advantage over a hash table for strings?
options: Tries use less memory
Tries support efficient prefix-based searching and autocomplete
Tries have faster insertion for all inputs
Tries guarantee O(1) lookup
explanation: Tries excel at prefix operations like autocomplete, which hash tables cannot efficiently support.

text: What is union by rank in Union-Find?
options: Ranking elements by value before union
Attaching shorter tree under root of taller tree
Performing unions in element rank order
Sorting sets by size before merging
explanation: Union by rank keeps trees balanced by attaching smaller-rank tree under larger-rank root.

text: What does a Bloom filter return for membership?
options: The exact element if it exists
Definitely not in set, or possibly in set
Insertion count of the element
A probability score between 0 and 1
explanation: Bloom filter: definitely not (no false negatives) or possibly in (may have false positives).

text: What is a trie's space complexity for n strings of average length L?
options: O(n)
O(n * L)
O(L)
explanation: Worst case O(n * L) when strings share few common prefixes.

text: What is a segment tree primarily for?
options: Segmenting files for network transfer
Answering range queries on arrays efficiently
Storing geometric line segments
Dividing trees into equal segments
explanation: Segment trees answer range queries (sum, min, max) and support updates in O(log n).

text: What is a Fenwick tree (BIT)?
options: A tree with binary nodes indexed by depth
A structure for efficient prefix sum queries and point updates
A BST with indexed leaves only
A tree storing data in binary format
explanation: Fenwick trees support prefix sum queries and point updates in O(log n).

text: How does a compressed trie save space?
options: Compressing characters with Huffman coding
Merging single-child chains into one edge with a substring label
Storing only first and last characters
Using hash tables at each node
explanation: Compressed tries collapse single-child chains into single edges, reducing node count significantly.

text: What is a 2D segment tree?
options: A segment tree in 2D array layout
Each node contains a segment tree for the second dimension
A pair of independent segment trees
A tree supporting exactly two query types
explanation: Outer tree on rows, inner trees on columns, supporting 2D range queries in O(log^2 n).

text: How do Fenwick trees and segment trees differ?
options: Fenwick supports range queries, segment trees only point queries
Fenwick is simpler with less memory but segment trees handle more query types
Segment trees are always slower
Fenwick works only on sorted arrays
explanation: Fenwick: simpler, less memory for prefix sums. Segment trees: more versatile with lazy propagation.

text: What is an interval tree for?
options: Storing intervals and finding overlaps efficiently
Calendar scheduling only
Decision trees with interval splits
Tracking intervals between sorted elements
explanation: Interval trees store intervals and find overlaps in O(log n + k) time.

text: What is weighted Union-Find?
options: Edge weights from elements to roots for path-weighted queries
Higher priority for heavier elements
Weighting find for shorter paths
Balancing by total element weight
explanation: Weighted Union-Find maintains edge weights for relative weight queries between same-set elements.

text: What is a K-D tree for?
options: K-dimensional data for spatial queries like nearest neighbor
Data in K sorted orders simultaneously
Trees with exactly K children per node
Decomposing into K subproblems
explanation: K-D trees partition K-dimensional space by alternating split dimensions for spatial queries.

text: What is the time to build a segment tree from n elements?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: Bottom-up construction processes each O(n) node once: O(n) total.

text: What is the count-min sketch?
options: Counts the minimum element in a stream
Probabilistic structure using multiple hashes to estimate frequencies
Sketch-based structure for graph drawing
Maintains exact frequencies with minimal memory
explanation: Count-min sketch estimates element frequencies with multiple hash functions, with one-sided overestimation error.

text: What is a persistent data structure?
options: Persists data to disk for durability
Preserves all previous versions after modifications
Permanently locks elements after insertion
Designed for long-running processes
explanation: Persistent structures retain all previous versions, allowing access to any historical state.

text: What is a sparse table for?
options: Storing sparse matrices in compressed format
Static range minimum queries in O(1) after O(n log n) preprocessing
Hash tables with sparse occupancy
Indexing sparse data for nearest neighbor
explanation: Sparse tables preprocess in O(n log n) for O(1) range minimum queries on static arrays.

text: What is a wavelet tree?
options: Based on wavelet transform for signal processing
Answers range frequency, quantile, and rank queries on sequences
Stores data in wave patterns for cache efficiency
Balanced tree for multimedia storage
explanation: Wavelet trees recursively partition alphabets for range rank, select, and quantile queries in O(log sigma).

text: What is a link-cut tree's amortized time per operation?
options: O(log n)
O(sqrt(n))
O(log^2 n)
explanation: Link-cut trees: access, link, cut, path aggregation in O(log n) amortized using splay trees.

text: What is the inverse Ackermann function's significance in Union-Find?
options: Proves operations take exactly constant time
Provides nearly-constant amortized bound alpha(n) growing incredibly slowly
Shows Union-Find is NP-hard
Determines optimal hash function count
explanation: alpha(n) <= 4 for all practical n, making Union-Find effectively O(1) per operation.

text: What is a succinct data structure?
options: A structure with brief textual description
Uses space near information-theoretic minimum while supporting fast queries
Optimized for short string storage
Trades query time for space savings
explanation: Succinct structures use space within lower-order terms of the minimum while supporting efficient operations.

text: How does fractional cascading work?
options: Cascading fractional data across levels
Augmenting sorted catalogs so search in one guides searches in others
Splitting data between memory and disk
Cascading hash functions for collision resolution
explanation: Fractional cascading adds fractions with cross-pointers, reducing k searches from O(k log n) to O(k + log n).

text: What is a range tree?
options: Stores elements within a specified range only
Multi-level tree for orthogonal range searching in d dimensions
B-tree variant for range disk access
Each node stores its subtree value range
explanation: Range trees support d-dimensional orthogonal range queries in O(log^d n + k).

text: What is a top tree?
options: Keeps most-accessed elements at top
Maintains dynamic trees with path and subtree operations
Only top level stored in memory
Priority queue extracting top element
explanation: Top trees support dynamic tree operations (path/subtree queries, link, cut) in O(log n).

text: What is K-D tree nearest neighbor search complexity?
options: O(log n) expected, O(n) worst case
O(1) amortized for all cases
O(n log n) in all cases
O(K * n) where K is dimensions
explanation: Expected O(log n) but degrades to O(n) worst case with adversarial distributions.

text: What is the Euler tour tree (ETT) used for?
options: Representing traversals for visualization
Maintaining dynamic forests with subtree aggregates in O(log n)
Encoding Euler's formula for planar graphs
Storing Euler paths for circuit detection
explanation: ETT stores Euler tour in a balanced BST for link, cut, and subtree aggregates in O(log n).

text: What is a fusion tree?
options: Merges multiple structures into one
Achieves O(log_w n) search using word-level parallelism
Fuses B-trees into one balanced structure
Hybrid of tries and BSTs
explanation: Fusion trees use w-bit word operations for O(log_w n) search, beating comparison-based O(log n).

text: What is string matching?
options: Comparing strings for alphabetical ordering
Finding occurrences of a pattern within a text string
Matching strings by hash values only
Converting one string format to another
explanation: String matching finds all positions where a pattern occurs in a text.

text: What is the time complexity of naive string matching?
options: O(n)
O(m + n)
O(n * m)
O(m * log n)
explanation: Naive algorithm checks pattern at every position, comparing up to m characters: O(n * m).

text: What is a palindrome?
options: A string with all identical characters
A string reading the same forwards and backwards
A string with exactly two distinct characters
A string whose length is prime
explanation: A palindrome reads the same forward and backward, like "racecar".

text: What is the simplest way to check for anagrams?
options: Compare hash values directly
Sort both strings and compare
Check same first and last characters
Reverse one and compare with the other
explanation: Sort both strings and compare: if identical, they are anagrams. O(n log n).

text: What is a substring?
options: Any rearrangement of characters
A contiguous sequence of characters within a string
A string from removing every other character
A string longer than the original
explanation: A substring is a contiguous character sequence within a string.

text: What is a prefix of a string?
options: The first character only
A substring starting at the beginning
Any character before the last
The longest repeated sequence at start
explanation: A prefix starts from the first character, including empty string and the full string.

text: What is a suffix of a string?
options: The last character only
A substring ending at the last character
Any character after the first
The longest non-repeating end sequence
explanation: A suffix ends at the last character, including empty string and the full string.

text: How many substrings does a string of length n have?
options: n
explanation: n(n+1)/2 non-empty substrings plus the empty string.

text: What is string concatenation?
options: Splitting a string into characters
Joining two strings end to end
Reversing characters in a string
Removing duplicate characters
explanation: Concatenation joins two strings sequentially to form a new string.

text: What is the time to compare two strings of length n?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: Comparing requires checking each character pair: O(n) worst case.

text: What is the Rabin-Karp algorithm?
options: Pattern matching via comparison sorting of substrings
Pattern matching using rolling hash for fast comparison
String compression from repeated patterns
String search building a finite automaton
explanation: Rabin-Karp uses rolling hashes, doing character comparison only on hash matches.
