text: What is the failure function in KMP?
options: Reports when the algorithm fails
Stores longest proper prefix-suffix length for each position
Detects invalid characters in the pattern
A fallback hash function for collisions
explanation: KMP failure function stores longest proper prefix that is also a suffix for each position.

text: What is a suffix array?
options: Array of all prefixes sorted
Sorted array of all suffixes by starting indices
Array mapping suffixes to occurrence counts
Structure storing suffix lengths
explanation: A suffix array is a sorted array of all suffixes represented by their starting positions.

text: What is the Z-algorithm?
options: Compresses strings using zero-run encoding
Computes Z-array where Z[i] is longest prefix match starting at i
Finds zero-length matches in search
Sorts strings by last character first
explanation: Z-algorithm computes Z[i] = length of longest prefix match at position i, in O(n) time.

text: What is KMP's time complexity?
options: O(n * m)
O(n + m)
O(n * log m)
explanation: KMP preprocesses pattern in O(m) and scans text in O(n): O(n + m) total.

text: What is the LCP array?
options: Longest common prefix between string and each suffix
Longest common prefix lengths between adjacent sorted suffixes
Prefix occurrence count mapping
Longest common prefix between every suffix pair
explanation: LCP[i] = length of longest common prefix between consecutive suffixes in the sorted suffix array.

text: How does Rabin-Karp rolling hash work?
options: Rehashes entire substring from scratch each time
Updates by removing leftmost char and adding rightmost in O(1)
Hashes alternating characters only
Uses a different function at each position
explanation: Rolling hash updates in O(1) by subtracting outgoing and adding incoming character contributions.

text: What is Aho-Corasick for?
options: Finding longest palindromic substring
Searching multiple patterns simultaneously in text
Sorting strings lexicographically
Computing edit distance between pairs
explanation: Aho-Corasick builds a trie with failure links for multi-pattern matching in O(n + m + z) time.

text: What is Manacher's algorithm for?
options: Finding anagrams of a pattern in text
Finding longest palindromic substring in linear time
Computing minimum edits between strings
Building suffix trees in linear time
explanation: Manacher's finds the longest palindromic substring in O(n) using palindrome symmetry properties.

text: What is Boyer-Moore's key optimization?
options: Preprocesses text instead of pattern
Compares right to left using bad character and good suffix rules to skip ahead
Uses hash values avoiding character comparison
Builds finite automaton for O(n) matching
explanation: Boyer-Moore compares right-to-left with skip rules, achieving sublinear time in practice.

text: What is Ukkonen's algorithm?
options: Builds suffix arrays in O(n log n)
Online algorithm constructing suffix trees in O(n)
Computes edit distance in subquadratic time
Finds all maximal palindromes
explanation: Ukkonen's builds suffix trees online in O(n) using suffix links and active point.

text: What is the DC3/Skew algorithm?
options: D&C building suffix arrays in O(n)
Skews comparison function for faster sorting
Three-way partitioning for string sorting
Constructs suffix trees from suffix arrays
explanation: DC3 constructs suffix arrays in O(n) by dividing suffixes into groups by position mod 3.

text: What is the Burrows-Wheeler Transform?
options: Compression replacing patterns with codes
Reversible transform grouping similar characters for compression
Converting strings to phonetic representations
Encryption permuting characters by key
explanation: BWT sorts cyclic shifts and takes the last column, grouping similar characters for effective compression.

text: What is the relationship between suffix arrays and suffix trees?
options: Completely unrelated structures
Any operation on one can be simulated on the other in same time
Suffix arrays replace trees only for pattern matching
Suffix trees are strictly more powerful
explanation: Suffix arrays with LCP arrays simulate all suffix tree operations in the same time; each converts to the other in O(n).

text: What is SA-IS's time complexity?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
O(n log^2 n)
explanation: SA-IS constructs suffix arrays in O(n) using suffix classification and induced sorting.

text: What is the palindromic tree (eertree)?
options: Binary tree storing palindromic strings
Tree storing all distinct palindromic substrings with links
Balanced BST sorting palindromes
Trie storing only palindromes
explanation: The palindromic tree stores all unique palindromic substrings, built in O(n) time and space.

text: What is Lyndon factorization?
options: Factoring into prime character components
Decomposing into a non-increasing sequence of Lyndon words
Splitting at every repeated character boundary
Factoring based on suffix array ordering
explanation: Lyndon factorization uniquely decomposes into non-increasing Lyndon words, computable in O(n).

text: What is the purpose of suffix links in suffix trees?
options: Linking suffixes to prefix nodes
Connecting internal nodes for efficient traversal during construction
Linking leaves into a suffix list
Connecting the tree to a hash table
explanation: Suffix links connect node xA to node A, enabling O(1) transitions during construction.

text: What is the time to find longest repeated substring with suffix array and LCP?
options: O(n log n)
O(n)
O(n * m)
explanation: After O(n) construction, the longest repeated substring is the maximum LCP value found in O(n) scan.

text: What is collision probability in polynomial string hashing?
options: Hashing for cryptographic security
Bounded by m/p for prime p and strings of length m
Analyzing CPU cache collisions
Computing collision-free hash values
explanation: Polynomial hashing modulo prime p has collision probability bounded by m/p for length-m strings.

text: What does the P complexity class represent?
options: Problems provably unsolvable
Problems solvable in polynomial time
Problems requiring parallel processing
Problems with polynomial space needs
explanation: P contains decision problems solvable in polynomial time by a deterministic Turing machine.

text: What does the NP class represent?
options: Problems unsolvable in polynomial time
Problems whose solutions can be verified in polynomial time
Problems not practical to solve
Problems needing non-polynomial space
explanation: NP contains problems whose solutions can be verified in polynomial time.

text: What is space complexity?
options: Physical space for source code
Memory an algorithm uses relative to input size
Number of declared variables
Disk space for output
explanation: Space complexity measures total memory needed as a function of input size.

text: What does polynomial time mean?
options: Uses polynomial expressions in code
Running time bounded by a polynomial of input size
Processes polynomials as input
Takes fixed time regardless of input
explanation: Polynomial time: worst case O(n^k) for some constant k.

text: What is an optimization problem?
options: Optimizing code for compilation speed
Seeking the best solution among all feasible ones
Having exactly one correct solution
Minimizing code line count
explanation: Optimization problems seek the best solution (max/min of objective) among feasible solutions.

text: What is the P vs NP relationship?
options: P and NP are completely disjoint
P is a subset of NP; whether P=NP is unknown
NP is a strict subset of P
P=NP is proven
explanation: Every P problem is in NP, but whether P=NP remains open.

text: What is a decision problem?
options: Choosing between two algorithms
A problem with a yes or no answer
Solved by decision trees exclusively
Deciding which data structure to use
explanation: A decision problem asks a yes/no question, forming the basis for complexity classes.

text: What is exponential time complexity?
options: Time doubling with each unit input increase
Time bounded by O(c^n) for c > 1
Time growing as fast as factorial
Time proportional to input exponents
explanation: Exponential: O(c^n) where c > 1, impractical for large inputs.

text: What is a reduction in complexity theory?
options: Simplifying to fewer code lines
Transforming one problem into another to show relative difficulty
Reducing input size for speed
Decreasing a structure's space complexity
explanation: A reduction transforms one problem into another, proving relative difficulty.

text: What does O(n log n) indicate?
options: n comparisons of log-sized data
Running time slightly faster than linear
n nested loops each running log n
Logarithmic space and linear time
explanation: O(n log n) grows proportional to n * log(n), common in efficient sorting.

text: What is polynomial-time reduction?
options: Reducing polynomial equation degree
Transforming one problem into another in polynomial time
Reducing exponential to polynomial time
Converting polynomial-space to constant-space
explanation: A polynomial reduction transforms problem A into problem B in polynomial time, preserving the answer.

text: What is NP-hard?
options: Hardest problems within NP
Problems at least as hard as hardest NP problems, not necessarily in NP
Problems with no polynomial verification
Problems hard to approximate
explanation: NP-hard: at least as hard as any NP problem, but may not be in NP themselves.

text: What is co-NP?
options: Complement of NP: no-instances verifiable in polynomial time
Problems not in NP
Problems for cooperative non-deterministic machines
Intersection of P and NP
explanation: co-NP: "no" instances have polynomial-time verifiable certificates.

text: What is the significance of 3-SAT?
options: Simplest problem in P
Canonical NP-complete problem used as basis for reductions
Always solvable in O(n^3)
Proves P=NP for three variables
explanation: 3-SAT is NP-complete and the common starting point for NP-completeness proofs.

text: What is an approximation algorithm?
options: Finds exact solutions half the time
Finds provably near-optimal solutions in polynomial time
Approximates input data for speed
Rounds floating-point to integers
explanation: Approximation algorithms find solutions within a provable factor of optimal in polynomial time.

text: What is PSPACE?
options: Problems solvable in polynomial space by a deterministic TM
Polynomial space but exponential time required
Parallel space allocation problems
Pseudo-polynomial space problems
explanation: PSPACE: problems solvable in polynomial space; P subset NP subset PSPACE.

text: What is the vertex cover problem?
options: Finding minimum vertices covering all edges
Finding vertices covering maximum graph area
Finding all reachable vertices from source
Finding a vertex connected to all others
explanation: Vertex cover: smallest set of vertices such that every edge is incident to at least one.

text: What is a 2-approximation for vertex cover?
options: Cover of exactly twice the minimum
Cover at most twice the minimum size
Minimum cover in O(n^2)
Considering every vertex pair
explanation: Pick uncovered edges and add both endpoints: produces a cover at most 2x optimal.

text: What differentiates pseudo-polynomial from polynomial time?
options: Same thing, different terminology
Pseudo-polynomial is polynomial in numeric value, not bit length
Pseudo-polynomial is always faster
Polynomial depends on value, pseudo on bit length
explanation: Pseudo-polynomial: polynomial in numeric value (e.g., O(nW) for knapsack), exponential in bit length.

text: What is graph coloring's complexity?
options: Polynomial for all graphs
NP-complete for k-colorability with k >= 3
NP-hard only for directed weighted graphs
O(V+E) with greedy coloring
explanation: k-colorability for k >= 3 is NP-complete; k=2 reduces to bipartiteness testing.

text: What is the PCP theorem?
options: Every P problem checked probabilistically in constant time
Every NP proof verifiable by reading constant random and proof bits
Probabilistic computation solves problems outside NP
Polynomial with preprocessing solves NP-hard
explanation: PCP theorem: NP = PCP(log n, 1), verifying NP proofs with O(log n) random bits and O(1) proof bits.

text: What is the unique games conjecture?
options: Every CSP has a unique optimal solution
NP-hard to distinguish almost satisfiable from almost unsatisfiable unique games
Games with unique Nash equilibria are polynomial
Unique solution problems are in P
explanation: UGC: NP-hard to distinguish nearly satisfiable from far-from-satisfiable unique label cover instances.

text: What is BPP?
options: Problems for bounded parallel processors
Problems solvable by probabilistic TM in polynomial time with bounded error
Bounded polynomial preprocessing problems
Polynomial time with bounded space
explanation: BPP: randomized polynomial-time algorithms with error probability at most 1/3.

text: What is ETH (Exponential Time Hypothesis)?
options: All NP problems solvable in subexponential time
3-SAT cannot be solved in 2^(o(n)) time
Exponential algorithms always optimal for NP-complete
Exponential hierarchy collapses to PSPACE
explanation: ETH: 3-SAT requires 2^(Omega(n)) time, implying fine-grained lower bounds.

text: What is fixed-parameter tractability (FPT)?
options: Making all parameters fixed constants
Solving in f(k)*n^c time with exponential confined to parameter k
Fixing DP table parameter count
Tracing parameters for performance tuning
explanation: FPT: f(k)*n^c time, efficient when parameter k is small regardless of n.

text: What is inapproximability?
options: Inability to implement in a specific language
Proving no polynomial algorithm achieves a ratio better than a threshold
Impossibility of converting approximate to exact
Difficulty approximating running time
explanation: Inapproximability proves that achieving a ratio better than a threshold is NP-hard.

text: What is the clique problem's complexity?
options: Maximum clique is polynomial for sparse graphs
NP-complete and hard to approximate within n^(1-epsilon)
In P for planar graphs
Size-3 clique requires O(n^3) unimprovable
explanation: Maximum clique: NP-complete, NP-hard to approximate within n^(1-epsilon) unless P=NP.

text: What is the role of randomness in RP?
options: RP always produces random possibly-incorrect outputs
RP accepts yes-instances with prob >= 1/2 and always rejects no-instances
RP uses randomness only for tie-breaking
RP guarantees correct results with randomized time
explanation: RP: always correctly rejects no-instances; accepts yes-instances with probability >= 1/2.

text: What is SETH?
options: No algorithm solves NP problems faster than brute force
For every epsilon>0, exists k such that k-SAT unsolvable in O(2^((1-epsilon)n))
All SAT needs exactly 2^n time
Strong exponential bounds for all polynomial algorithms
explanation: SETH: as k grows, k-SAT approaches 2^n, implying tight bounds for edit distance, LCS, etc.

text: What is Savitch's theorem?
options: Polynomial-time problems solvable in log space
NSPACE(f(n)) contained in DSPACE(f(n)^2) for f(n)>=log n
NP-complete requires exponential space
Non-deterministic equals deterministic space always
explanation: Non-deterministic space S(n) simulable in deterministic O(S(n)^2), implying NPSPACE = PSPACE.
